- $L_k$ = number of levels in attribute $k$
- $\beta_{kl}$ = part-worth utility of level $l$ of attribute $k$
- $x_{jkl}$ = 1 if profile $j$ contains level $l$ of attribute $k$, else 0

For a specific example, suppose a consumer evaluates **Profile P**: Medium Sweetness, Tetra Pak, ₹30.

We expand $U_P$ as follows:

$$ U_P = \beta_{\text{Med}} + \beta_{\text{Tetra}} + \beta_{30} $$

Substituting hypothetical part-worth values:

$$ U_P = (1.2) + (0.8) + (0.5) $$

$$ U_P = 2.5 $$

The **relative importance** of attribute $k$ is calculated as:

$$ I_k = \dfrac{\text{Range}_k}{\sum_{k=1}^{K} \text{Range}_k} \times 100 $$

Where:

$$ \text{Range}_k = \max_l(\beta_{kl}) - \min_l(\beta_{kl}) $$

### Worked Example with Three Attributes

Let the estimated part-worths from a survey of $n = 100$ consumers be:

- **Sweetness:** None = $-0.8$, Light = $+0.4$, Medium = $+1.2$
- **Packaging:** Tetra Pak = $+0.8$, Glass = $+0.3$, PET = $-0.6$
- **Price:** ₹20 = $+0.9$, ₹30 = $+0.5$, ₹40 = $-1.0$

Step 1: Compute Range per attribute.

$$ \text{Range}_{\text{sweet}} = 1.2 - (-0.8) = 2.0 $$

$$ \text{Range}_{\text{pack}} = 0.8 - (-0.6) = 1.4 $$

$$ \text{Range}_{\text{price}} = 0.9 - (-1.0) = 1.9 $$

Step 2: Total range.

$$ \sum \text{Range} = 2.0 + 1.4 + 1.9 = 5.3 $$

Step 3: Relative importance.

$$ I_{\text{sweet}} = \dfrac{2.0}{5.3} \times 100 = 37.74\,\% $$

$$ I_{\text{pack}} = \dfrac{1.4}{5.3} \times 100 = 26.42\,\% $$

$$ I_{\text{price}} = \dfrac{1.9}{5.3} \times 100 = 35.85\,\% $$

**Interpretation for R&D:** Sweetness and price matter most; packaging is secondary. The R&D team should *not* over-engineer the package — focus on sweetness and price-value balance.

## 3.2 Derivation: Breakeven Analysis for Gate 2 Business Case

### Given (Hypothetical KTU Numerical)

A startup plans to launch a **frozen paratha** line. Data:
- Fixed cost (factory setup, machinery) = $F = \text{₹}50{,}00{,}000$
- Selling price per pack = $P = \text{₹}60$
- Variable cost per pack (ingredients, packaging, labour) = $V = \text{₹}35$

### Step 1: Contribution Margin per unit

$$ CM = P - V = 60 - 35 = \text{₹}25 \text{ per pack} $$

### Step 2: Breakeven Units

$$ BEU = \dfrac{F}{P - V} = \dfrac{50{,}00{,}000}{25} = 2{,}00{,}000 \text{ packs} $$

### Step 3: Breakeven Revenue

$$ BER = BEU \times P = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 60 = \text{₹}1{,}20{,}00{,}000 $$

### Step 4: Payback Period (assuming annual volume of 5,00,000 packs)

$$ \text{Annual Cash Inflow} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 25 = \text{₹}1{,}25{,}00{,}000 $$

$$ PB = \dfrac{50{,}00{,}000}{1{,}25{,}00{,}000} = 0.4 \text{ years} \approx 4.8 \text{ months} $$

### Step 5: ROI over 3 years

$$ \text{Total Profit (3 yr)} = 3 \times 1{,}25{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}3{,}75{,}00{,}000 $$

$$ ROI = \dfrac{3{,}75{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 650\,\% $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule for Gate 2:** If $ROI \geq Hurdle Rate$ (typically 25–30 % for FMCG food), and $PB \leq 24$ months, the project gets a **GO**. Otherwise **KILL** or **HOLD**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing All Three Market-Oriented Frameworks

| Criterion | Kotler 8-Stage | Cooper Stage-Gate | QFD / House of Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| Year of Origin | 1967 (Marketing Mgmt) | 1986 (R&D Mgmt) | 1972 (Mitsubishi, Japan) |
| Originator | Philip Kotler | Robert G. Cooper | Yoji Akao |
| Primary Focus | Marketing-led | Cross-functional | Engineering translation of VOC |
| Number of Stages | 8 | 5 + Discovery | 4 matrices (HOQ is first) |
| Gate Decision Points | No formal gates | Yes (5 gates) | Not gate-based |
| Consumer Involvement | High (Stage 3, 7) | High (Gate 2, 4) | Very High (entire matrix) |
| Use in Food Industry | Medium | Very High | High |
| Time-to-Market | Slow | Fast (iterative) | Medium |
| Best For | Conceptual understanding | Industry execution | Technical specification |

## 3.4 Algorithm (Verbal Flow, Not Code) — How to Run a QFD Session

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is a non-CS engineering subject, we present the QFD process as a *verbal decision flow*, NOT as programming code.

1. **Step 1 — Gather VOC:** Conduct focus groups (8–12 consumers per city) and ethnographic home visits. Convert raw quotes into "Whats" (e.g., "I want a snack that doesn't make my hands sticky").
2. **Step 2 — Prioritize Whats:** Use a 5-point importance scale. Drop the bottom 20 %.
3. **Step 3 — Identify Hows:** Translate Whats into technical specs (e.g., "Moisture content $\leq 4\,\%$").
4. **Step 4 — Build Relationship Matrix:** Score (9 = strong, 3 = medium, 1 = weak, 0 = none) for each What–How pair.
5. **Step 5 — Compute Technical Importance:** Multiply customer importance × max relationship score.
6. **Step 6 — Benchmark:** Compare against 2–3 competitor products on each What.
7. **Step 7 — Set Targets:** Define R&D targets for each How (numerical specifications).
8. **Step 8 — Roof Analysis:** Check How–How correlations (synergy $\odot$ or conflict $\otimes$).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Cooper's Stage-Gate Process (Market-Oriented)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 0: Discovery - VOC and Idea Generation] -->|Gate 0: Idea Screen| B[Stage 1: Scoping - Preliminary Market Assessment]
    B -->|Gate 1: Scope Approval| C[Stage 2: Build Business Case - Detailed Consumer Research]
    C -->|Gate 2: Business Case GO| D[Stage 3: Development - Formulation and Process Scale-up]
    D -->|Gate 3: Ready to Test| E[Stage 4: Testing and Validation - Sensory and Test Market]
    E -->|Gate 4: Launch Decision| F[Stage 5: Launch - Commercial Production and Distribution]
    F -->|Post Launch Review| G[Continuous Improvement Loop]

    H[KILL Decision at Any Gate] --> I[Project Archive and Learning]
    J[HOLD Decision at Any Gate] --> K[Re-evaluate at Next Cycle]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Kotler's 8-Stage Market-Oriented NPD

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Stage 1: Idea Generation] --> A2[Stage 2: Idea Screening]
    A2 --> A3[Stage 3: Concept Development and Testing]
    A3 --> A4[Stage 4: Marketing Strategy Development]
    A4 --> A5[Stage 5: Business Analysis]
    A5 --> A6[Stage 6: Product Development]
    A6 --> A7[Stage 7: Test Marketing]
    A7 --> A8[Stage 8: Commercialization]

    M1[Market Intelligence and VOC] -.-> A1
    M1 -.-> A3
    M1 -.-> A5
    M1 -.-> A7
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — House of Quality (QFD) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HOUSE["Quality Function Deployment - House of Quality"]
        direction TB
        A[Customer Requirements - WHATS - Left Wall]
        B[Technical Requirements - HOWS - Ceiling]
        C[Relationship Matrix - Central Room - Whats x Hows]
        D[Correlation Matrix - Roof - How x How Synergy or Conflict]
        E[Customer Importance Ratings - Left]
        F[Technical Importance Ratings - Right]
        G[Competitive Benchmarking - Bottom]
    end

    VOC[Voice of Customer - Focus Groups, Ethnography] --> A
    A --> C
    B --> C
    C --> F
    A --> E
    D -.-> B
    G -.-> A
    F --> OUT[R and D Technical Targets and Specifications]
    E --> OUT
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Decision Tree — Market vs. Technology Push

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[NPD Project Initiated] --> Q1{Who Initiated the Project?}
    Q1 -->|Consumer Need Identified First| MO[Market-Oriented Pathway]
    Q1 -->|Laboratory Discovery First| TP[Technology-Push Pathway]
    Q1 -->|Strategic Gap in Brand Portfolio| PL[Platform-Driven Pathway]

    MO --> S1[Conduct VOC and Ethnography]
    S1 --> S2[Define Target Consumer Segment]
    S2 --> S3[Concept Test with 200 plus Consumers]
    S3 --> S4[Build Business Case and Breakeven]
    S4 --> S5[Gate 2 GO]
    S5 --> S6[Stage Gate Process Activated]

    TP --> T1[Patent Filing and IP Protection]
    T1 --> T2[Seek Market Application]
    T2 --> T3[Customer Validation Required]
    T3 --> S3

    PL --> P1[Portfolio Gap Analysis]
    P1 --> P2[Line Extension Strategy]
    P2 --> S3
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Process Flow — Conjoint Analysis Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X1[Identify Product Attributes and Levels] --> X2[Generate Orthogonal Fractional Factorial Design]
    X2 --> X3[Construct Product Profiles e.g. 9 to 16 cards]
    X3 --> X4[Field Survey with n greater than 200 consumers]
    X4 --> X5[Regression Analysis to Estimate Part-Worths]
    X5 --> X6[Compute Attribute Importance]
    X6 --> X7[Simulate Market Share for New Profile]
    X7 --> X8[Recommend Optimal Product Configuration to R and D]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Market-Oriented New Product Development. List any THREE pillars of market orientation.**

**Model Answer:**

Market-Oriented New Product Development (NPD) is a structured approach to product innovation where the entire development process is initiated and driven by identified consumer needs, market gaps, and competitive intelligence rather than by technological capability alone.

The three pillars of market orientation (Narver and Slater, 1990) are:

1. **Customer Orientation** — Systematic understanding of the target customer's needs, wants, and usage situations.
2. **Competitor Orientation** — Continuous analysis of competitors' strengths, weaknesses, products, and strategies.
3. **Inter-functional Coordination** — Harmonized effort among R&D, marketing, manufacturing, and supply chain to serve the target customer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip (3 marks):** Definition = 1 mark, 3 pillars = 1.5 marks (0.5 each), relevance statement = 0.5 mark.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is Voice of Customer (VOC)? State TWO methods of VOC collection.**

**Model Answer:**

Voice of Customer (VOC) refers to the structured process of capturing customers' explicit and implicit needs, expectations, preferences, and pain points regarding a product category.

Two methods of VOC collection:

1. **Qualitative Methods:** Focus group discussions (FGDs), in-depth interviews, ethnographic home visits, and consumer diaries.
2. **Quantitative Methods:** Large-scale structured surveys using Likert scales, Kano model questionnaires, and online polls.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Definition = 1.5 marks, two methods = 1.5 marks (0.75 each).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the **Stage-Gate process** of new product development. List the stages and gates with their deliverables. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A food company is planning to launch a **low-sugar ready-to-serve (RTS) mango drink**. The fixed cost of setting up the bottling line is ₹80 lakhs, the expected selling price is ₹50 per 200 mL bottle, and the variable cost is ₹28 per bottle. Calculate:
- (i) Contribution margin per unit
- (ii) Breakeven units
- (iii) Breakeven revenue
- (iv) Payback period if expected annual sales volume is 6,00,000 bottles
- (v) Whether the project should receive a GO at Gate 2, given a hurdle rate of 30 % per annum. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for (a):

The Stage-Gate process, developed by **Robert G. Cooper (1986)**, is a conceptual and operational map for moving a new product project from idea to launch. It divides the NPD effort into **five stages** separated by **five gates**.

**Stage 0 — Discovery:** Ideation, VOC capture, market scanning, and trend analysis. Deliverable: Idea pool ranked by strategic fit.

**Gate 0 — Idea Screen:** Quick screen on strategic alignment, market attractiveness, and technical feasibility. Decision: Go / Kill / Recycle.

**Stage 1 — Scoping:** Preliminary market assessment, target segment definition, and rough technical check. Deliverable: Mini business case.

**Gate 1 — Scope Decision:** Decide whether the opportunity warrants a full business case. Decision: Go / Kill / Hold.

**Stage 2 — Build Business Case:** Detailed consumer research, competitive analysis, financial modelling, and technical feasibility. Deliverable: Full business case with NPV, ROI, breakeven.

**Gate 2 — Business Case GO:** Senior management review. Decision: Go / Kill / Hold.

**Stage 3 — Development:** Laboratory formulation, process development, packaging, and in-house validation. Deliverable: Working prototype.

**Gate 3 — Development Decision:** Confirm prototype is ready for market testing. Decision: Go / Kill.

**Stage 4 — Testing and Validation:** In-home use tests, central location tests, sensory evaluation, and limited test marketing. Deliverable: Validated product and launch plan.

**Gate 4 — Launch Decision:** Final go for commercialization. Decision: Go / Kill.

**Stage 5 — Launch:** Full-scale production, distribution, and marketing rollout. Deliverable: National or regional launch.

**Post-Launch Review:** Captures learnings and feeds back to Stage 0.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip (7 marks):** Diagram = 2 marks, 5 stages with deliverables = 2.5 marks, 5 gates with decisions = 2 marks, example/conclusion = 0.5 mark.

---

#### Model Answer for (b):

**Given Data:**
- Fixed Cost $F = \text{₹}80{,}00{,}000$
- Selling Price $P = \text{₹}50$ per bottle
- Variable Cost $V = \text{₹}28$ per bottle
- Annual Volume = $6{,}00{,}000$ bottles

**(i) Contribution Margin per unit:**

$$ CM = P - V = 50 - 28 = \text{₹}22 \text{ per bottle} $$

**(ii) Breakeven Units:**

$$ BEU = \dfrac{F}{P - V} = \dfrac{80{,}00{,}000}{22} = 3{,}63{,}636.36 \approx 3{,}63{,}637 \text{ bottles} $$

**(iii) Breakeven Revenue:**

$$ BER = BEU \times P = 3{,}63{,}637 \times 50 = \text{₹}1{,}81{,}81{,}850 $$

**(iv) Payback Period:**

$$ \text{Annual Cash Inflow} = 6{,}00{,}000 \times 22 = \text{₹}1{,}32{,}00{,}000 $$

$$ PB = \dfrac{80{,}00{,}000}{1{,}32{,}00{,}000} = 0.606 \text{ years} \approx 7.27 \text{ months} $$

**(v) Decision at Gate 2:**

Since $PB = 7.27$ months is **well below the typical 24-month benchmark**, and the project would achieve profitability within the first year, the project **qualifies for a GO at Gate 2** provided the hurdle rate of 30 % is met by the broader NPV analysis. The breakeven volume of 3,63,637 bottles is achievable given the projected 6,00,000 annual volume, giving a **safety margin of ~39 %**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Forgetting to subtract Variable Cost $V$ in contribution margin (most common error: writing CM = ₹50).
> 2. Reporting breakeven units in lakhs instead of absolute number.
> 3. Missing the safety margin / margin-of-safety analysis.
> 4. Writing the answer without showing the formula first.
> 5. Not stating the decision explicitly ("GO" or "KILL") — losing 0.5 mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**Q: (a)** What is **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)**? With the help of a labelled diagram, describe the **House of Quality (HOQ)** and explain its six key components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A consumer study was conducted to identify the **top three drivers** for purchasing a packaged breakfast cereal. Customers rated importance on a 5-point scale. The mean importance scores and the company's product scores (vs. the leading competitor) are given below. Using a **House of Quality importance-performance analysis**, determine which attribute the company should prioritize for improvement. **(7 marks)**

| Driver | Mean Importance (1–5) | Company Score (1–5) | Competitor Score (1–5) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Taste | 4.8 | 3.5 | 4.5 |
| Crispness/Texture | 4.2 | 4.0 | 3.8 |
| Nutritional Value (Protein/Fiber) | 4.6 | 3.0 | 3.2 |
| Price | 4.0 | 3.8 | 3.0 |
| Packaging Convenience | 3.5 | 4.2 | 3.5 |

---

#### Model Answer for (a):

**Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** is a structured planning and product development methodology that translates **Voice of Customer (VOC)** into **measurable technical specifications** for product design and process engineering. It was developed in Japan by **Yoji Akao** in 1972 and first deployed at Mitsubishi Heavy Industries.

The central tool of QFD is the **House of Quality (HOQ)** — a matrix diagram resembling a house. Its six key components are:

1. **Left Wall — Customer Requirements (Whats):** The "voice of the customer" expressed as needs like "low in sugar", "crunchy", "easy to pour".

2. **Ceiling — Technical Requirements (Hows):** Measurable engineering specifications like "moisture content $\leq 4\,\%$", "hardness = 250 N", "particle size 2 mm".

3. **Central Room — Relationship Matrix:** The intersection of Whats and Hows, scored on a 9-3-1-0 scale (Strong-Medium-Weak-None).

4. **Roof — Correlation Matrix (How × How):** Identifies synergies ($\odot$) and conflicts ($\otimes$) between technical requirements (e.g., reducing sugar may increase hardness — a conflict).

5. **Right Wall — Technical Importance Ratings:** Computed as $\text{Customer Importance} \times \text{Max Relationship Score}$. Ranked list of R&D priorities.

6. **Bottom — Competitive Benchmarking:** Numerical comparison of customer satisfaction scores (1–5) for the company's product vs. 2–3 competitors on each What.

A typical output is a set of **R&D targets** for each How, derived from the bottom-up benchmarking and the right-wall priorities.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip (7 marks):** QFD definition = 1 mark, HOQ diagram (properly labelled) = 3 marks, six components = 2.5 marks (0.4 each), industry example = 0.5 mark.

---

#### Model Answer for (b):

**Method: Importance–Performance Analysis (IPA) Quadrant Mapping.**

**Step 1: Compute the Gap (Performance Deficit).**

$$ \text{Gap} = \text{Competitor Score} - \text{Company Score} $$

For each driver:
- Taste: $4.5 - 3.5 = +1.0$
- Crispness: $3.8 - 4.0 = -0.2$ (company is better)
- Nutrition: $3.2 - 3.0 = +0.2$
- Price: $3.0 - 3.8 = -0.8$ (company is better)
- Packaging: $3.5 - 4.2 = -0.3$ (company is better)

**Step 2: Compute Improvement Priority Index (IPI).**

$$ IPI = \text{Importance} \times \text{Gap} $$

- Taste: $4.8 \times 1.0 = 4.80$
- Crispness: $4.2 \times (-0.2) = -0.84$ (negative — no improvement needed)
- Nutrition: $4.6 \times 0.2 = 0.92$
- Price: $4.0 \times (-0.8) = -3.20$ (negative — company is already better)
- Packaging: $3.5 \times (-0.3) = -1.05$ (negative — company is already better)

**Step 3: Rank the Priorities.**

| Driver | IPI | Rank | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Taste | +4.80 | 1 | **URGENT — invest in flavor R&D** |
| Nutrition | +0.92 | 2 | Medium priority — add protein/fiber |
| Crispness | -0.84 | 3 | No action |
| Packaging | -1.05 | 4 | No action |
| Price | -3.20 | 5 | No action (already competitive) |

**Step 4: Decision.**

The company should prioritize **Taste** as the #1 improvement target because it has both the **highest customer importance (4.8)** and the **largest performance gap (1.0)** vs. the leading competitor. Nutrition (Protein/Fiber) is the second priority.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Forgetting to multiply Importance × Gap (just listing them is worth only 2 marks, not 7).
> 2. Treating negative IPI as "high priority" — they mean the company is *already* outperforming.
> 3. Not producing a clear ranking table.
> 4. Failing to convert analysis into a concrete R&D recommendation.
> 5. Mixing up "Importance" with "Performance" — they are different scales (4.8 ≠ 3.5 means there IS a gap, but the magnitude is what matters).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2, Market-Oriented NPD Methodologies**

- **Market-Oriented NPD** starts with the *customer*, not the *lab*. The three pillars are Customer Orientation, Competitor Orientation, and Inter-functional Coordination (Narver & Slater, 1990).
- **Voice of Customer (VOC)** is captured via qualitative (FGD, ethnography) and quantitative (survey, Kano model) methods.
- **Kotler's 8-Stage Model** is the *academic reference* — Stages 1, 3, 5, and 7 are the most-tested in KTU exams.
- **Cooper's Stage-Gate** is the *industry reference* — 5 stages, 5 gates, and a post-launch review. Gates are decision points (Go / Kill / Hold / Recycle).
- **QFD / House of Quality** translates VOC into technical specs. Six components: Whats, Hows, Relationship, Correlation, Technical Importance, Competitive Benchmarking.
- **Conjoint Analysis** uses part-worth utility functions and relative importance to simulate market share for new product profiles.
- **Key Formulas (memorize for Part B):**
  - $PI = \frac{N_{\text{buy}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$
  - $BEU = \frac{F}{P - V}$
  - $ROI = \frac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Investment}} \times 100$
  - $PB = \frac{\text{Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$
  - $IPI = \text{Importance} \times \text{Gap}$
  - $I_k = \frac{\text{Range}_k}{\sum \text{Range}_k} \times 100$
- **Decision Rule at Gate 2:** Approve if $ROI \geq Hurdle Rate$ AND $PB \leq 24$ months AND $BEU \leq 50\,\%$ of expected volume.
- **Kerala / Indian Context Brands** to cite in answers: MTR, ITC Foods, Britannia, Nestlé India, Hindustan Unilever, Amul, Milky Mist.
- **Common Pitfalls:** Using `|x|` inside markdown tables (use `\lvert x \rvert`); not labelling Mermaid node IDs as alphanumeric; not stating the final Go/Kill decision explicitly in numerical questions.
- **Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark Part B questions, always include a *diagram* (Stage-Gate or HOQ), *3 numerical steps with formulas*, and a *concrete R&D recommendation* in the final line.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Organization for Successful NPD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT835 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Organization for Successful NPD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Organization for Successful NPD (New Product Development)** refers to the deliberate structuring of human resources, cross-functional teams, reporting hierarchies, decision-gates, and managerial responsibilities to systematically convert a product idea into a commercially viable food product. It is the architectural backbone that aligns R&D, marketing, production, quality assurance, regulatory affairs, procurement, finance, and supply chain under a unified innovation charter.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, *organizing for NPD* is treated as one of the **four pillars of the innovation strategy framework**, sitting alongside:

1. **Product Innovation Charter (PIC)** — the *what* and *why*
2. **NPD Strategy** — the *where to play / how to win*
3. **NPD Process** — the *how to do it step by step*
4. **NPD Organization** — the *who does it* (current topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under Outcome-Based Education (OBE) for OEFTT835, this topic maps primarily to **CO2** — *"Organize cross-functional teams and frameworks for structured food product development."* Examiner questions will directly test your understanding of *which organizational structure suits which NPD scenario*.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Cricket Team Analogy

Imagine launching a new food product (say, a ready-to-drink protein shake) as analogous to **fielding a cricket team for a World Cup final**:

- **The Captain (Project Manager / Brand Champion)** sets the field placements and calls the plays.
- **The Batters (R&D and Product Development)** actually face the bowlers (market challenges) and score runs (develop prototypes).
- **The Bowlers (Marketing and Sales)** attack and restrict the opposition (competitors).
- **The Wicket-Keeper (Quality Assurance)** catches what slips through.
- **The Coach (Senior Management)** decides team selection, reviews strategy, and signs off on whether to "declare" (launch) or "bat on" (iterate).
- **The Umpire (Stage-Gate System)** objectively decides if the team is ready for the next phase.

Without an organized team — even with 11 talented individuals — you will lose. **The structure (organization) determines whether your innovation dollars convert into commercial wins.**

## 1.3 Why Organizing for NPD is Critical — Core Metrics

The success rate of NPD projects is heavily dependent on organizational design. Industry research consistently shows:

- Projects with **clearly defined NPD organization** have a success rate of approximately **65%–75%**.
- Projects with **vague or no formal organization** have a failure rate exceeding **70%**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric to Remember:** Three structural decisions must be answered for every NPD program — **(1) Who initiates?, (2) Who evaluates?, (3) Who implements?**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 Factors Influencing Choice of NPD Organization

The choice of organizational structure is not arbitrary. It depends on five inter-locking variables:

1. **Strategic Importance of Innovation** — Higher importance → more dedicated, permanent structures.
2. **Size and Complexity of the Company** — Large multinationals → multiple structures layered; SMEs → single lightweight structure.
3. **Product Portfolio Diversity** — Bread vs. product mix — affects whether category-based or project-based teams work better.
4. **Stage of NPD Process** — Idea generation needs creativity-favoring structures; commercialization needs disciplined structures.
5. **Culture and Senior Management Commitment** — Top-down vs. bottom-up innovation DNA.

## 2.2 The Three Major Organizational Models

### Model A: Functional (Departmental) Organization

- Each functional department (R&D, Marketing, Production) handles its slice of the NPD work.
- Coordination occurs through **written memos, formal meetings, and department heads**.
- **Best suited for:** small companies, incremental product line extensions, single-category portfolios.

### Model B: Lightweight Cross-Functional Teams

- A *Product Manager* or *Brand Manager* coordinates across functions on a **part-time basis**.
- Team members retain their functional reporting lines.
- **Best suited for:** moderate innovation, market-driven modification, line extensions.

### Model C: Heavyweight / Venture / Tiger Teams

- A *full-time, dedicated, cross-functional team* with its own budget, leader, and physical workspace.
- Team members are **physically and administratively reassigned** to the project.
- **Best suited for:** breakthrough innovations, strategic projects, platform developments.

## 2.3 Six Specific NPD Organizational Forms (KTU High-Yield)

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly expects familiarity with the following six forms:

| # | Organizational Form | Core Idea | Best Used When |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Functional Department Structure** | Work stays inside silos | Small firm, line extension |
| 2 | **Product / Brand Manager** | Coordinator role overlays functions | Consumer packaged goods, FMCG |
| 3 | **New Product Committee** | Senior executives as gatekeepers | Strategic alignment needed |
| 4 | **New Product Department** | Dedicated permanent unit | Innovation-heavy corporates |
| 5 | **Venture / Tiger Teams** | Full-time autonomous team | Breakthrough / radical innovation |
| 6 | **Stage-Gate Cross-Functional Core Team** | Permanent team owns entire process | Complex multi-stage projects |

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Framework Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this table as your **last-night revision sheet** for Part A and Part B questions on this topic.

| Symbol / Term | Definition | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|
| $PIC$ | Product Innovation Charter | Defines *what* to develop |
| $NPD_{Org}$ | NPD Organization | Defines *who* develops |
| $CFT$ | Cross-Functional Team | Core unit of NPD org |
| $SG$ | Stage-Gate | Decision checkpoint |
| $VT$ | Venture Team | Autonomous, full-time |
| $NPD_{success}$ | $\approx f(Strategy + Process + Org + Culture)$ | All 4 must align |
| $\alpha$ | Senior management commitment index | $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ |
| $\beta$ | Cross-functional integration index | $0 \le \beta \le 1$ |
| $NPD_{success}$ | $\uparrow$ as $\alpha \cdot \beta \uparrow$ | Multiplicative effect |

> **Critical Pitfall to Avoid:** Do not confuse *Product Manager* with *Project Manager*. In KTU valuation, a Product Manager is **functionally based and part-time**, whereas a Project Manager leads a **dedicated cross-functional team**.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility and Industry Application

In the **food industry**, organizing for NPD is uniquely complex because the team must concurrently satisfy:

- **Sensory / Consumer science** (taste, texture, appearance)
- **Food Safety / Regulatory** (FSSAI, Codex, FDA, EFSA compliance)
- **Food Technology / Process Engineering** (scale-up, shelf life)
- **Nutrition Science** (RDAs, fortification policy)
- **Supply Chain** (cold chain, packaging integrity)
- **Marketing / Brand** (positioning, claims, labeling)
- **Finance** (COGS, pricing, payback period)

Without a strong **organizational architecture**, any one of these verticals can derail a project. This is why food MNCs (Nestlé, ITC Foods, Britannia, Mondelez) invest heavily in dedicated NPD centers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown of Organizational Forms

This section provides the **exhaustive analytical expansion** of each organizational form, with KTU-relevant logical transitions, mirroring the type of structured answer expected in 14-mark questions.

## 3.1 Functional Department Structure — Stepwise Analysis

**Step 1 — Define the Setup:**
The NPD project work is distributed *across existing functional departments* such as R&D, Marketing, Production, QA, and Finance.

**Step 2 — Identify the Coordinator:**
The *Marketing Manager* or *R&D Head* acts as informal coordinator. There is **no dedicated project leader**.

**Step 3 — Flow of Information:**
Information flows through **formal channels** — departmental memos, monthly review meetings, and inter-departmental committees.

**Step 4 — Strengths:**

$$S_{functional} = \{Low\ cost,\ Deep\ specialization,\ Stable\ routines\}$$

**Step 5 — Weaknesses:**

$$W_{functional} = \{Slow\ decision\ making,\ Weak\ cross\ integration,\ Innovation\ inertia\}$$

**Step 6 — Decision Rule:**

$$Use\ Functional\ Org\ \Longleftrightarrow\ (Innovation\ Radicalness = LOW)\ \land\ (Project\ Complexity = LOW)$$

## 3.2 Product / Brand Manager — Stepwise Analysis

**Step 1 — Appointment:**
A **single individual** (Product Manager or Brand Manager) is assigned *accountability* for the new product across its lifecycle.

**Step 2 — Authority Type:**
The Product Manager has *influence* but **no direct line authority** over R&D, Production, or QA staff.

**Step 3 — Work Pattern:**

$$\text{Brand Manager} \xrightarrow{\text{Coordinates}} \{R\&D,\ Marketing,\ Sales,\ Finance\}$$

**Step 4 — Best Fit:**
FMCG, packaged foods, beverages, dairy — where **category-based ownership** dominates.

**Step 5 — Limitation:**

$$\text{Authority}_{Brand\ Mgr} \neq \text{Authority}_{Project\ Mgr}$$

Because Brand Managers cannot command resources, **breakthrough innovation struggles** under this model.

## 3.3 New Product Committee — Stepwise Analysis

**Step 1 — Composition:**
A **standing committee** of senior executives from Marketing, R&D, Manufacturing, and Finance.

**Step 2 — Function:**

$$\text{Committee} = \{Gatekeeper + Reviewer + Approver\}$$

**Step 3 — Cadence:**
Typically meets **monthly or quarterly** to evaluate project proposals and review progress.

**Step 4 — Strength:**
Ensures **strategic alignment** with corporate priorities.

**Step 5 — Weakness:**
Tends to become **bureaucratic** and risk-averse; can suppress creative, low-CEA projects.

**Step 6 — Decision Rule:**

$$Use\ NPC\ \Longleftrightarrow\ (Project\ Capital\ Intensity = HIGH)\ \land\ (Strategic\ Risk = HIGH)$$

## 3.4 New Product Department — Stepwise Analysis

**Step 1 — Structural Form:**
A **permanent, independent department** dedicated solely to new product development.

**Step 2 — Reporting Line:**
Reports directly to the **CEO or Managing Director** — bypassing functional silos.

**Step 3 — Internal Structure:**

$$NPD\ Dept = \{R\&D\ Unit + Marketing\ Unit + Pilot\ Plant + Packaging\ Lab\}$$

**Step 4 — Strategic Role:**
Acts as the **innovation engine** of the firm — analogous to corporate R&D in pharma.

**Step 5 — Decision Rule:**

$$Use\ NPD\ Dept\ \Longleftrightarrow\ (Innovation\ Spend\ \%\ of\ Revenue > 5\%)$$

## 3.5 Venture / Tiger Teams — Stepwise Analysis

**Step 1 — Team Constitution:**
A **full-time, multidisciplinary, autonomous team** with members *physically relocated* to work together.

**Step 2 — Team Profile:**

$$Tiger\ Team = \{R\&D,\ Marketing,\ Finance,\ Operations,\ Packaging,\ Regulatory\}$$

**Step 3 — Authority:**
Team leader holds **full P&L responsibility** for the project.

**Step 4 — Lifecycle:**
The team **disbands** once commercialization is achieved; members may return to functions or be reassigned.

**Step 5 — Application in Food Industry:**

$$\text{Plant-based meat},\ \text{Lab-grown dairy},\ \text{Fortified\ RTD\ beverages}\ \rightarrow\ Tiger\ Team$$

**Step 6 — Risk Factor:**

$$Cost_{Tiger} \gg Cost_{Functional},\ but\ Speed_{Tiger} \gg Speed_{Functional}$$

## 3.6 Stage-Gate Cross-Functional Core Team — Stepwise Analysis

**Step 1 — Conceptual Basis:**
Combines **discipline of Stage-Gate process** with **integration of cross-functional teams**.

**Step 2 — Stage-Gate Schematic:**

$$Idea \to Gate_1 \to Development \to Gate_2 \to Testing \to Gate_3 \to Launch$$

**Step 3 — Core Team Composition:**
Each gate is staffed by **representatives from all functions**, with a *Core Team Leader* present throughout.

**Step 4 — Decision Logic at Each Gate:**

$$D_{Gate} = \begin{cases} Go, & \text{if } Score_{project} \geq Threshold \\ Kill, & \text{if } Score_{project} < Threshold \\ Recycle, & \text{if } Revisions\ feasible \end{cases}$$

**Step 5 — Modern Industry Standard:**
This is the **de facto standard** in food MNCs such as Nestlé, ITC, and Mondelez for managing high-volume NPD pipelines.

**Step 6 — Decision Rule:**

$$Use\ SG-CFT \Longleftrightarrow (Portfolio\ Size > 10\ active\ projects)\ \land\ (Risk\ Diversification\ needed)$$

## 3.7 Summary Decision Matrix

| Organization Type | Innovation Type | Cost | Speed | Strategic Fit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Functional | Incremental | Low | Low | Small firm |
| Brand / Product Manager | Line Extension | Low–Medium | Medium | FMCG |
| New Product Committee | Strategic | Medium | Medium | Large firm |
| New Product Department | Platform | High | Medium–High | Innovation-led |
| Venture / Tiger Team | Breakthrough | Very High | Very High | Radical / Disruptive |
| Stage-Gate CFT | Portfolio-wide | Medium–High | High | Modern MNC |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — NPD Organizational Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Senior Management / Board] --> B[New Product Committee]
    B --> C1[Functional Dept Structure]
    B --> C2[Brand / Product Manager]
    B --> C3[New Product Department]
    B --> C4[Venture / Tiger Team]
    B --> C5[Stage-Gate Cross-Functional Team]
    C1 --> D1[R and D Dept]
    C1 --> D2[Marketing Dept]
    C1 --> D3[Production Dept]
    C1 --> D4[Quality Assurance]
    C2 --> E1[Part-time Coordination]
    C2 --> E2[Influence-based Authority]
    C3 --> F1[Permanent NPD Unit]
    C3 --> F2[CEO Reporting Line]
    C4 --> G1[Full-time Team]
    C4 --> G2[Autonomous Budget]
    C4 --> G3[P and L Responsibility]
    C5 --> H1[Stage 1 Discovery]
    C5 --> H2[Stage 2 Development]
    C5 --> H3[Stage 3 Testing]
    C5 --> H4[Stage 4 Launch]
    H1 --> gate1[Gate 1 Decision]
    H2 --> gate2[Gate 2 Decision]
    H3 --> gate3[Gate 3 Decision]
    gate1 --> decision1{Go or Kill or Recycle}
    gate2 --> decision2{Go or Kill or Recycle}
    gate3 --> decision3{Go or Kill or Recycle}
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Cross-Functional Team Composition for Food NPD

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    core[Core Team Leader / Project Manager] --> r1[R and D Food Scientist]
    core --> r2[Sensory and Consumer Specialist]
    core --> r3[Process Engineer]
    core --> r4[Quality and Food Safety Officer]
    core --> r5[Regulatory and FSSAI Expert]
    core --> r6[Marketing and Brand Manager]
    core --> r7[Supply Chain and Procurement]
    core --> r8[Finance and Cost Analyst]
    core --> r9[Packaging Technologist]
    core --> r10[Nutritionist]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Stage-Gate Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Idea Generation] --> s1[Stage 1 Scoping]
    s1 --> g1[Gate 1 Screen]
    g1 --> cond1{Approved}
    cond1 --> no1[Kill]
    cond1 --> yes1[Proceed]
    yes1 --> s2[Stage 2 Concept Development]
    s2 --> g2[Gate 2 Business Case]
    g2 --> cond2{Approved}
    cond2 --> no2[Kill or Hold]
    cond2 --> yes2[Proceed]
    yes2 --> s3[Stage 3 Product Development]
    s3 --> g3[Gate 3 Development Review]
    g3 --> cond3{Approved}
    cond3 --> no3[Kill]
    cond3 --> yes3[Proceed]
    yes3 --> s4[Stage 4 Validation and Testing]
    s4 --> g4[Gate 4 Launch Decision]
    g4 --> launch[Commercial Launch]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Food NPD Organization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Leadership
        L1[CEO]
        L2[NPD Steering Committee]
    end
    subgraph CoreTeam[Core Cross-Functional Team]
        CT1[Team Leader]
        CT2[R and D]
        CT3[Marketing]
        CT4[Operations]
        CT5[QA and RA]
        CT6[Finance]
    end
    subgraph Support[Support Functions]
        SU1[Procurement]
        SU2[Legal]
        SU3[HR]
        SU4[IT and Data]
    end
    subgraph External[External Partners]
        EX1[Co-packers]
        EX2[Contract Research Labs]
        EX3[Distribution Partners]
    end
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> CT1
    CT1 --> CT2
    CT1 --> CT3
    CT1 --> CT4
    CT1 --> CT5
    CT1 --> CT6
    CoreTeam --> Support
    CoreTeam --> External
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the term "Organization for Successful NPD" and list any four organizational structures used for new product development. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Organization for Successful NPD refers to the systematic arrangement of people, teams, decision-making authority, and cross-functional coordination mechanisms that enable an enterprise to convert product ideas into market-ready food products efficiently and effectively.

**Four organizational structures:**
1. Functional Department Structure
2. Product / Brand Manager Structure
3. New Product Committee
4. Venture / Tiger Team

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 each for structures × 4 = 2 marks, totalling 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2: Distinguish between a Brand Manager and a Venture Team with respect to authority, commitment, and innovation type. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Brand Manager | Venture Team |
|---|---|---|
| Authority Type | Influence only, no direct line control | Full P&L responsibility |
| Time Commitment | Part-time | Full-time |
| Innovation Type Suited | Line extensions, incremental | Breakthrough, radical |
| Team Composition | Coordinator role only | Cross-functional members physically relocated |

*(1 mark for table + 2 marks for the three distinct comparative points)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** Examiners *strictly* allocate marks for: **diagram/flowchart (2 marks) + sub-part coverage (1 mark each for clarity) + table or formula use (2 marks) + conclusion or strategic recommendation (1 mark)**. Students who write only paragraphs without structured tables or diagrams **lose 3–4 marks** routinely. Always include at least one **table** and one **flowchart or block diagram** in every 14-mark answer.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **functional department structure** of organizing NPD. Discuss its **strengths, weaknesses, and ideal conditions** for use in a food product company. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Describe the **New Product Committee** and the **New Product Department** as organizational structures. Compare their **strategic roles** in an established food MNC. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer

### (a) Functional Department Structure — 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark):**
A functional department structure organizes NPD activities within existing functional silos such as R&D, Marketing, Production, and Quality Assurance, without creating a separate project-based entity.

**Operational Mechanism (2 marks):**
The R&D department develops the prototype; Marketing conducts consumer research; Production handles scale-up; QA verifies compliance. Coordination occurs through inter-departmental meetings and written memos.

**Strengths (2 marks):**
- Low cost — no dedicated infrastructure
- Deep functional specialization
- Stability and continuity

**Weaknesses (1 mark):**
- Slow decision making
- Weak cross-functional integration
- Risk of innovation inertia

**Ideal Conditions (1 mark):**
Best suited for small companies pursuing incremental product modifications or line extensions.

---

### (b) New Product Committee vs New Product Department — 7 Marks

**New Product Committee (3 marks):**
- A standing committee of senior executives
- Acts as gatekeeper and strategic approver
- Meets monthly or quarterly
- Strength: strategic alignment; Weakness: bureaucratic, risk-averse

**New Product Department (3 marks):**
- A permanent independent unit dedicated to innovation
- Reports directly to CEO
- Houses R&D, marketing, pilot plant, packaging lab
- Suited for innovation-led corporates with high R&D intensity

**Comparative Strategic Role (1 mark):**
| Aspect | Committee | Department |
|---|---|---|
| Longevity | Standing review body | Permanent execution body |
| Strategic Role | Approves & aligns | Builds & executes |
| Cost | Low | High |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

**Q. (a)** Describe the **Venture Team / Tiger Team** model of NPD organization. Explain its **composition, authority, lifecycle, and application in the food industry** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Discuss the **Stage-Gate Cross-Functional Team** model. Illustrate the **four stages and three gates** with a flow diagram, and explain the **Go / Kill / Recycle decision logic**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer

### (a) Venture / Tiger Team — 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark):**
A venture or tiger team is a full-time, autonomous, cross-functional team assembled for a specific breakthrough product development project, with members physically relocated and administratively reassigned.

**Composition (2 marks):**
Members from R&D, Marketing, Finance, Operations, Packaging, Regulatory, and Quality — all working together full-time.

**Authority (1 mark):**
Team leader holds full P&L responsibility and direct command over project budget.

**Lifecycle (1 mark):**
Formed at project initiation → disbanded after commercial launch. Members may return to original functions or move to new ventures.

**Food Industry Example (2 marks):**
- Plant-based meat (Beyond Meat / Impossible Foods style launches)
- Lab-grown dairy proteins
- Functional probiotic RTD beverages

---

### (b) Stage-Gate Cross-Functional Team — 7 Marks

**Conceptual Basis (1 mark):**
Combines the discipline of the Stage-Gate process with the integration of cross-functional teams. Each stage ends at a gate where a Go / Kill / Recycle decision is taken.

**Four Stages (2 marks):**
1. Discovery / Scoping
2. Concept Development
3. Product Development
4. Validation and Commercialization

**Three Gates (2 marks):**
- Gate 1 — Idea Screen
- Gate 2 — Business Case Review
- Gate 3 — Launch Decision

**Decision Logic (2 marks):**

$$D_{Gate} = \begin{cases} Go & \text{if } Score \geq Threshold \\ Kill & \text{if } Score < Threshold \ \& \ no\ revision \\ Recycle & \text{if } revisions\ feasible \end{cases}$$

Flow diagram (see SECTION 4.3 Mermaid Diagram for reference) must accompany the answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Night Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ☐ Organization for NPD answers the question **"Who does it?"** — distinct from strategy (*what/why*), process (*how*), and culture (*climate*).
- ☐ **Six organizational forms** are examinable: Functional, Brand Manager, New Product Committee, New Product Department, Venture/Tiger Team, Stage-Gate CFT.
- ☐ **Brand Manager** = influence, part-time, line extensions. **Venture Team** = full-time, autonomous, breakthrough.
- ☐ **NPD Department** is permanent; **Tiger Team** is temporary and dissolves post-launch.
- ☐ **Stage-Gate** is the *modern industry standard* in food MNCs and is the most frequently tested 14-mark question.
- ☐ Always include a **table** and **diagram** in 14-mark answers — examiners specifically award 2–3 marks for visual structuring.
- ☐ The four pillars of NPD framework: **PIC + Strategy + Process + Organization** — questions often test whether you can *integrate* them.
- ☐ Senior management commitment (α) and cross-functional integration (β) **multiply** to determine success:

$$NPD_{success} \uparrow \quad \text{as} \quad \alpha \cdot \beta \uparrow$$

- ☐ For **radical innovation** → Tiger Team; for **incremental innovation** → Functional / Brand Manager.
- ☐ Food NPD teams *must* include sensory science, regulatory, packaging, and nutrition — a unique requirement versus generic FMCG.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of Traditional Recipe and Modification
Involvement of Consumers, Chefs, and Recipe Experts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT835 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Involvement of Consumers, Chefs, and Recipe Experts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stakeholder Involvement in Food Product Development (FPD)** refers to the systematic integration of end-users (consumers), culinary professionals (chefs), and domain specialists (recipe/formulation experts) into the various phases of the new product development (NPD) pipeline — namely ideation, concept screening, prototype formulation, sensory evaluation, and final commercialization — to ensure that the resulting food product satisfies market demand, gastronomic appeal, technical feasibility, and regulatory compliance.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **OEFTT835**, the involvement of these three pivotal human-resource pillars is treated as the *Voice of the Customer (VoC)* component of the Stage-Gate®-style FPD model, and is mapped to the Course Outcome focused on translating consumer needs into product specifications.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Building a House"**
> Imagine you are commissioning a custom home.
> - The **Consumer** is the *family that will live in the house* — they tell you how many bedrooms they need, their budget, and lifestyle.
> - The **Chef** is the *interior decorator* — they bring creativity, plating artistry, and culinary culture, transforming a functional kitchen into an experience.
> - The **Recipe Expert / Formulation Scientist** is the *structural engineer* — they ensure the recipe (foundation) is stable, scalable, shelf-stable, and compliant with food safety codes.
>
> Without the family, you build a house nobody wants. Without the decorator, the kitchen is dull. Without the engineer, the house collapses. Food product development is *exactly* this triad.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars — At a Glance

| Stakeholder | Primary Role | Core Contribution |
|---|---|---|
| **Consumers** | End-users / Buyers | Define needs, accept/reject prototypes, validate market fit |
| **Chefs** | Culinary Artists | Provide creativity, recipe ideation, gourmet refinement, plating logic |
| **Recipe Experts** | Formulation Scientists | Ensure technical feasibility, scaling, stability, regulatory fit |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme places a renewed emphasis on **co-creation** and **lead-user theory** (von Hippel). This means students must know not just *what* each stakeholder does, but *how* and *when* in the NPD funnel they are engaged.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" — Rationale for Involvement

1. **Risk Mitigation** — Approximately **70–80%** of new food products fail within the first year. Early consumer feedback dramatically lowers this risk.
2. **Sensory Acceptance** — Chefs and recipe experts add the *hedonic* and *technical* dimensions that pure consumer research cannot capture.
3. **Innovation Source** — Chefs and lead-user consumers are often the **source** of disruptive ideas (e.g., molecular gastronomy, plant-based meats).
4. **Process Efficiency** — Recipe experts eliminate the formulation dead-ends before expensive pilot-plant trials.
5. **Regulatory & Commercial Alignment** — Recipe experts bridge the gap between a creative recipe and a label-compliant, mass-producible product.

### 2.2 The "How" — Methods of Involvement

#### A. Consumer Involvement Methods

| Method | Stage of NPD | Description | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Focus Groups** | Ideation / Concept | 8–12 consumers moderated discussion | Qualitative themes, unmet needs |
| **In-Depth Interviews** | Concept | One-on-one probing | Latent needs, emotional drivers |
| **Surveys (Conjoint / MaxDiff)** | Concept / Design | Quantitative preference mapping | Part-worth utilities, attribute importance |
| **Home-Use Tests (HUT)** | Prototype | Consumers use product in real environment | Acceptance, repurchase intent |
| **Central Location Tests (CLT)** | Prototype | In-store or test-kitchen trials | Hedonic scores, preference maps |
| **Sensory Panels (Trained / Consumer)** | Prototype | Descriptive & affective tests | Sensory profile, liking drivers |
| **Lead-User Workshops** | Ideation | Co-creation with extreme users | Breakthrough concepts |
| **Online Communities / Crowdsourcing** | Ideation / Concept | Digital co-creation platforms | Idea banks, concept screening |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Hedonic Score:** A 9-point categorical scale (1 = dislike extremely, 9 = like extremely) used to quantify consumer liking. The **mean hedonic score ≥ 7** is the industry rule-of-thumb threshold for market viability.

#### B. Chef Involvement Methods

| Method | Stage of NPD | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Culinary Brainstorming Sessions** | Ideation | Chefs co-develop flavor pairings, novel formats |
| **Recipe Showcases / Competitions** | Ideation | Competitive ideation (e.g., innovation challenges) |
| **Restaurant Pilot Trials** | Prototype | Real-menu testing for a limited period |
| **Plating & Format Advisory** | Design | Guidance on visual, textural, and serving formats |
| **Trend Forecasting Panels** | Concept | Identification of emerging culinary trends |
| **Collaboration with R&D (Embedded Chef Model)** | All stages | Chef embedded in food company R&D team |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Embedded Chef Model:** Pioneered by companies like McCormick and Unilever, this model places a professional chef *inside* the corporate R&D team to provide continuous culinary insight — a direct answer to the "Ivory Tower" problem where lab-developed products fail in real kitchens.

#### C. Recipe Expert (Formulation Scientist) Involvement Methods

| Method | Stage of NPD | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Bench-Top Prototyping** | Prototype | Lab-scale formulation trials |
| **Ingredient Functionality Mapping** | Concept | Aligns ingredients with desired sensory/nutritional outputs |
| **Shelf-Life & Stability Studies** | Pre-launch | Accelerated and real-time stability testing |
| **Process Scale-Up Trials** | Pre-launch | Pilot-plant and factory-line validations |
| **Cost Engineering / Reformulation** | Design | Recipe optimization against cost targets |
| **Regulatory & Labeling Review** | Pre-launch | FSSAI / FDA / EU compliance verification |
| **Sensory-Statistical Modeling** | Prototype | Preference mapping, PLS regression, just-about-right (JAR) analysis |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Analytical Frameworks

> [!TIP]
> **Framework 1 — The Three-Voice Model (TVM)**
> A consumer is the *Voice of Demand*, a chef is the *Voice of Desire*, and a recipe expert is the *Voice of Deliverability*. A successful food product is the intersection of all three voices.

> [!TIP]
> **Framework 2 — Stage-Gate Integration of Stakeholders**
> - **Stage 1 (Discovery):** Consumers + Chefs
> - **Stage 2 (Concept Development):** Consumers + Recipe Experts
> - **Stage 3 (Formulation):** Chefs + Recipe Experts
> - **Stage 4 (Validation):** Consumers (Sensory + HUT)
> - **Stage 5 (Launch):** All three (Consumer marketing, Chef endorsements, Recipe Expert QA)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** — The *House of Quality* matrix uses consumer inputs (the "Whats") that are translated by recipe experts into the "Hows" (technical specifications).
- **Sensory Science** — Trained panels under recipe expert supervision generate descriptive data that correlate with consumer hedonic data using **Preference Mapping** and **External Preference Mapping (EPM)**.
- **Cost-of-Goods (COGS) Engineering** — Recipe experts use linear programming models to minimize cost while satisfying nutritional and sensory constraints.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Detailed Methodological Breakdown

### 3.1 The "When-Who-How" Analytical Matrix

The following is the exhaustive step-by-step methodology for integrating all three stakeholder groups across the FPD pipeline. Every step is explicitly defined to satisfy KTU 2024 Scheme's outcome-based learning requirement (CO2: "Apply consumer-centric and expert-driven approaches to food product formulation").

---

**Step 1 — Consumer Insight Mining (Stage: Discovery / Ideation)**

$$\text{Consumer Need Vector } \vec{N} = \{n_1, n_2, n_3, \dots, n_k\}$$

where each $n_i$ represents a *weighted consumer need* extracted via qualitative research. Techniques used:

- Ethnographic observation
- Focus groups (typically **6–10 sessions** of 8 participants)
- Online diary studies
- Social-media listening (Netnography)

*Why this step:* Without explicit need-vector definition, downstream formulation is shooting in the dark. KTU examiners specifically test whether students can map qualitative insights to quantitative attributes.

---

**Step 2 — Chef-Led Ideation (Stage: Discovery / Concept)**

$$\text{Idea Pool } I = \{I_1, I_2, \dots, I_m\} \text{ where each } I_i \text{ is co-created by Chef} \times \text{Consumer}$$

*How:*

1. Chef reviews $\vec{N}$ (Consumer Need Vector) from Step 1.
2. Chef proposes **3–5 culinary concept directions** per need.
3. Cross-functional team screens using the **Pugh Matrix** (concept screening against weighted criteria).

*Why this step:* Chefs translate abstract needs (e.g., "comfort") into concrete product concepts (e.g., "warm caramelised onion relish with toasted sourdough crunch").

---

**Step 3 — Recipe Expert Formulation (Stage: Concept-to-Prototype)**

Recipe experts translate the chef's winning concept into a quantifiable formulation:

$$F = \sum_{j=1}^{p} x_j \cdot I_j \quad \text{subject to: } \sum_{j=1}^{p} x_j = 1.00 \text{ (mass balance)}$$

where:
- $F$ = total formulation
- $x_j$ = mass fraction of ingredient $j$
- $I_j$ = ingredient $j$ (each with its own functional role: emulsifier, bulking agent, flavor carrier, etc.)
- $p$ = total number of ingredients

*Constraints typically imposed:*

- **Nutritional constraint:** $\sum x_j \cdot N_j \leq \text{Target Nutrient Limit}$ (e.g., sodium $\leq 400$ mg per serving)
- **Cost constraint:** $\sum x_j \cdot C_j \leq \text{COGS Target}$
- **Sensory constraint:** $S(F) \geq 7.0$ (target hedonic score)
- **Stability constraint:** Water activity $a_w \leq 0.6$ OR $\text{pH} \leq 4.6$ (for shelf-stable products)

*Why this step:* This is the **engineering core** of FPD. KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests mass-balance and constraint-driven formulation.

---

**Step 4 — Consumer Sensory Validation (Stage: Prototype Validation)**

The classical hedonic test statistic is the mean liking score:

$$\bar{L} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i \quad \text{where } L_i \in \{1, 2, \dots, 9\}$$

with the consumer acceptance criterion:

$$\bar{L} \geq 7.0 \quad \text{(commercial acceptance threshold)}$$

For **Just-About-Right (JAR)** scaling, the percentage of JAR responses must typically exceed **70%** for the product to move to next stage.

*Why this step:* Validates that the recipe-expert formulation, when filtered through the chef's concept, still satisfies the original $\vec{N}$ from Step 1. This is the **closed-loop** of the FPD process.

---

**Step 5 — Recipe Expert Scale-Up & Chef Refinement (Stage: Pre-Launch)**

Recipe experts perform scale-up using dimensionless scaling principles:

$$\frac{\text{Pilot Batch}}{\text{Lab Batch}} = k \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Process parameters recalibrated: } T_{\text{pilot}}, t_{\text{residence}}, \text{Re}, \text{Nu}$$

Simultaneously, the chef refines the product for **gourmet positioning** (e.g., premium SKU line), creating a tiered product portfolio.

*Why this step:* Ensures the product is not only acceptable in the lab but also manufacturable, shelf-stable, and premium-credible.

---

**Step 6 — Commercial Launch Feedback Loop (Stage: Launch & Post-Launch)**

$$\text{Market Response Vector } \vec{R} = \{\text{Sales}, \text{NPS}, \text{Repurchase Rate}\}$$

This $\vec{R}$ is fed back into the next NPD cycle as updated $\vec{N}$, completing the continuous improvement loop (Deming / PDCA cycle).

---

### 3.2 Consolidated Analytical Matrix — "When-Who-How"

| NPD Stage | Primary Stakeholder | Secondary Stakeholder | Key Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Discovery / Ideation | **Consumer** | Chef | Need vector $\vec{N}$, Idea pool $I$ |
| Concept Development | **Chef** | Recipe Expert | Winning concept, Pugh matrix |
| Formulation | **Recipe Expert** | Chef | Prototype formula $F$ |
| Sensory Validation | **Consumer** | Recipe Expert | Hedonic score $\bar{L}$, JAR % |
| Scale-Up | **Recipe Expert** | Chef | Pilot batch, scaled parameters |
| Launch | **All three** | Marketing | Commercial SKU, brand narrative |
| Post-Launch | **Consumer** | Recipe Expert | Feedback $\vec{R}$ for next cycle |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Stakeholder Integration Flow in FPD

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Discovery] --> B[Stage 2: Concept Development]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Formulation]
    C --> D[Stage 4: Sensory Validation]
    D --> E[Stage 5: Scale Up and Pre Launch]
    E --> F[Stage 6: Launch and Post Launch]
    F -->|Feedback loop| A

    A1[Consumer Focus Groups] --> A
    A2[Chef Brainstorming] --> A
    A1 -.-> A2

    B1[Chef Pugh Matrix] --> B
    B2[Recipe Expert Feasibility] --> B
    B1 -.-> B2

    C1[Recipe Expert Bench Trials] --> C
    C2[Chef Refinement] --> C
    C1 -.-> C2

    D1[Consumer Sensory Panel] --> D
    D2[Recipe Expert JAR Analysis] --> D
    D1 -.-> D2

    E1[Recipe Expert Scale Up] --> E
    E2[Chef Premium SKU] --> E
    E1 -.-> E2

    F1[Consumer Market Response] --> F
    F2[Chef Brand Endorsement] --> F
    F3[Recipe Expert QA] --> F

    classDef consumer fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef chef fill:#FF9999,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000

    class A2,B1,C2,E2,F2 chef

```

### 4.2 The Three-Voice Intersection Model

```mermaid

    V2[Voice of Desire
    Chef]:::chef
    V3[Voice of Deliverability

    V1 --> X[Winning Food Product]
    V2 --> X
    V3 --> X

    classDef consumer fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
```
