# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define "modification for uniqueness" in food product design. List any four axes of uniqueness with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

Modification for uniqueness refers to the *deliberate engineering of differentiating features* into a food product so that it possesses a defensible competitive advantage in the marketplace.

**Four Axes of Uniqueness:**

1. **Ingredient Axis** — Use of an unconventional ingredient; *Example:* jackfruit seed flour in biscuits.
2. **Process Axis** — Use of a proprietary technology; *Example:* high-pressure processing (HPP) for cold-pressed juice.
3. **Format Axis** — Novel product form; *Example:* 3D-printed chocolate shapes.
4. **Claim Axis** — Verified functional or health claim; *Example:* "helps lower cholesterol" claim on a beta-glucan oats drink.

*(Word limit: ≤ 80 words for a 3-mark answer; present in bullet form.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: State the FSSAI criteria for a "Source of" and "High" nutrient claim on a packaged food label.**

**Model Answer:**

According to the **Food Safety and Standards (Labelling and Display) Regulations, 2020**:

- **"Source of [Nutrient]"** claim is permitted when the food provides **≥ 15%** of the RDA of that nutrient per serving.
- **"High [Nutrient]" or "Rich in [Nutrient]"** claim is permitted when the food provides **≥ 30%** of the RDA per serving.

Both claims must be supported by analytical testing and the RDA values published by **ICMR-NIN (2020)**.

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module typically offers **internal choice** in Part B. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (full 14 marks). Each question contains two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### ❑ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q: A traditional Kerala banana chips recipe is being scaled up from a 2-litre home fryer to a 500-litre continuous fryer in a commercial plant.**

**(a)** Discuss the **process modifications** required to maintain product quality during scale-up. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Calculate the **per-unit cost** and **gross margin** given the following data, and comment on commercial viability. *(7 marks)*

**Data:** Batch yield = 80%, Raw material cost = ₹60/kg banana, Oil cost = ₹180/kg (oil absorption 25% w/w), Labour = ₹2000/day for 400 kg output, Energy = ₹1500/day, Packaging = ₹3 per 100 g pouch, Selling Price = ₹80 per 100 g.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

**Part (a) — Process Modifications for Scale-Up (7 marks)**

1. **Geometric Similarity** — Continuous fryers maintain a constant belt-immersion depth (`D`) regardless of belt width. Hence `L/D` ratio is altered; one cannot simply multiply dimensions. *(1 mark)*

2. **Heat Transfer** — Surface-to-volume ratio falls, so residence time must be **reduced** and **oil temperature raised** (from 160 °C home-fry to 175–180 °C industrial fry) to compensate. Use a **multi-zone fryer** with pre-heat and finish zones. *(1.5 marks)*

3. **Mass Transfer (Oil Uptake)** — At small scale, oil absorption is ~30%; at large scale, controlled de-oiling using a vibratory conveyor and centrifugal de-oiler brings it to ~20–25%. *(1 mark)*

4. **Moisture Control** — Target final moisture **<3%**. Industrial fryers use **forced-draft post-fry tunnels**; the home recipe relies on ambient cooling. *(1 mark)*

5. **Mixing/Turning** — A home spatula turns chips manually every 30 s. Industrial equivalent: an **oscillating conveyor belt** with a flipper arm at the bend. *(1 mark)*

6. **Quality Control** — Online NIR sensor checks moisture and colour; home relies on visual cue. *(1 mark)*

7. **Packaging** — Nitrogen-flushed pouches replace open baskets to extend shelf life from 5 days to 6 months. *(0.5 mark)*

**Part (b) — Cost & Margin Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Raw material cost for 100 g finished chips.**

100 g finished chips need `100 / 0.80 = 125 g` of fresh banana.

$$
C_{raw} = 0.125 \, \text{kg} \times 60 = \text{₹ } 7.50
$$

**Step 2 — Oil cost.**

100 g chips absorb 25 g oil.

$$
C_{oil} = 0.025 \, \text{kg} \times 180 = \text{₹ } 4.50
$$

**Step 3 — Labour cost per 100 g.**

Output = 400 kg/day; daily labour = ₹2000.

$$
C_{lab} = \frac{2000}{400 \times 10} = \text{₹ } 0.50
$$

(Dividing by `400 × 10` = 4000 pouches of 100 g)

**Step 4 — Energy cost per 100 g.**

$$
C_{energy} = \frac{1500}{4000} = \text{₹ } 0.375
$$

**Step 5 — Packaging cost per 100 g.**

$$
C_{pack} = \text{₹ } 3.00
$$

**Step 6 — Total CPU and Gross Margin.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPU &= 7.50 + 4.50 + 0.50 + 0.375 + 3.00 \\
&= \text{₹ } 15.875 \approx \text{₹ } 15.88 \\
GM\,\% &= \frac{80 - 15.88}{80} \times 100 = \frac{64.12}{80} \times 100 \\
&= 80.15\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on Viability:** A **gross margin of ~80%** is exceptional for a snack product and indicates strong commercial viability. The dominant cost is **raw material (47%)**, so further cost optimization should focus on direct farmer-sourcing contracts rather than energy or labour.

---

### ❑ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: A college canteen wants to launch a "High-Protein Millet Snack" targeted at gym-going youth (18–25 years).**

**(a)** Design the **nutritional modification strategy** for this product, including RDA calculations and label claims. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **uniqueness positioning** strategy and outline how it can be defended in the market. *(7 marks)*

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

**Part (a) — Nutritional Modification Strategy (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Identify the target nutrient gap.**

Target consumer: youth (18–25 yrs), gym-going, ~70 kg body weight.
ICMR-NIN (2020) RDA values:

- Protein: **54 g/day** for males; the active gym-going target consumes only ~35 g/day from regular diet → **gap of ~20 g/day**.
- Iron: **19 mg/day** for males.
- Energy: ~2400 kcal/day.

**Step 2 — Decide serving size and nutrient target per serving.**

Serving size: **40 g** snack pack.

Per-serving nutrient target (delivering **20–25%** of protein gap):

$$
\text{Protein per serving} = 20 \, \text{g} \times 0.20 = 4 \, \text{g}
$$

Thus, **4 g protein per 40 g serving** qualifies as a **"Source of Protein"** claim (≥15% RDA per serving).

**Step 3 — Ingredient engineering.**

- Use **ragi (finger millet) flour** — 7 g protein per 100 g.
- Add **defatted soy flour** — 52 g protein per 100 g; brings protein to 7–8% of mix.
- Add **whey protein concentrate** (optional) — 80 g protein per 100 g.
- Add **iron-fortified salt** or natural iron source like **moringa leaf powder**.

**Step 4 — Validate stability.**

Test protein retention after baking (150 °C, 12 min) — typically **>90% retention** for soy/millet proteins.

**Step 5 — Compliance labelling per FSSAI 2020.**

- "Source of Protein" claim ✓ (4 g ÷ 54 g × 100 = 7.4% — *wait, this is below 15%; re-engineer to 10 g protein per serving*).
- **Revised plan:** fortify to deliver **10 g protein per 40 g serving** → `10/54 × 100 = 18.5%` ⇒ qualifies as *"Source of Protein"*.

**Part (b) — Uniqueness Positioning (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Market mapping.**

Competitors: corn-based puffs (low protein), soya chunks (high protein but hard texture). Gap identified: **millet-based, soft, high-protein, ready-to-eat snack** is *not* well-served.

**Step 2 — Differentiation axes.**

| Axis | Our Position |
|---|---|
| Ingredient | First-of-its-kind ragi + soya + whey combo |
| Format | Soft-baked (not fried) — lower fat |
| Claim | "Source of Protein", "High in Iron" |
| Tagline | *"Power from the Fields of Kerala"* |

**Step 3 — Defensive strategies.**

- **Trademark** the product name and tagline.
- **Geographical Indication (GI)** for Kerala millets if applicable.
- **Patent** on the specific baking protocol that preserves whey protein bioactivity.
- Build a strong **social media presence** with athlete endorsements — *brand-led uniqueness* is the hardest to copy.

**Step 4 — Sensory differentiation.**

Conduct a triangle test vs the leading competitor; aim for statistically significant (p < 0.05) difference in at least 3 of 5 sensory attributes (crispness, aroma, aftertaste, colour, mouthfeel).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units** in cost calculations — always write `₹/kg` or `₹/serving`.
> 2. **Using `S²` instead of `S³`** when scaling up volume — classic blunder.
> 3. **Conflating "retention %" with "final content mg/100 g"** in nutrition answers.
> 4. **Omitting the selling price** when computing gross margin — examiner expects `SP` in the formula.
> 5. **Writing "Source of" claim** without confirming the %RDA threshold numerically — KTU now insists on the *number*, not just the statement.
> 6. **Skipping diagrams** — even a hand-drawn block diagram of the scale-up flow fetches **1–2 extra marks** in Part B.
> 7. **Not labelling axes** on Mermaid-style diagrams in exam scripts — always label "x-axis: time, y-axis: temperature" etc.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin this list on your study wall — these are the high-frequency KTU test points.**

- ✅ The **four modification pillars** are: **L**arge-scale, **C**ost, **N**utrition, **U**niqueness — remember the mnemonic **"LCNU"**.
- ✅ **Geometric scale-up:** linear `S`, area `S²`, volume `S³`. Mixing power scales as `S⁵` (with constant Np).
- ✅ **Reynolds number** must be matched across scales to preserve flow regime.
- ✅ **Cost per serving** (₹/30 g) is the universal metric for Indian FMCG.
- ✅ **Gross margin of 25–40%** is the healthy band for processed foods.
- ✅ **FSSAI "Source"** ≥ 15% RDA; **"High"** ≥ 30% RDA per serving.
- ✅ **Six-tenths rule:** Cost_new ≈ Cost_old × (Capacity_new / Capacity_old)^0.6.
- ✅ **Nutrient retention** follows first-order decay: `N = N₀ × e^(−kt)`.
- ✅ **Uniqueness axes:** Ingredient / Process / Format / Claim.
- ✅ **Heat transfer is the bottleneck** of large-scale modification — always check (A/V) ratio.
- ✅ **Packaging and shelf-life** are integral to scale-up, not afterthoughts.
- ✅ **Break-even analysis** is essential to demonstrate commercial viability in any new food venture.
- ✅ **First-order kinetics** dominates nutrient loss in storage; minimize `t` and `T`.
- ✅ **Dynamic similarity** → constant Re; **Kinematic similarity** → proportional velocity profiles.
- ✅ **Always quote numerical evidence** — never say "high profit"; say "gross margin = 70.6%".
- ✅ **Memorize the 15 equations** in Section 2.2 — they cover ~85% of numerical questions.

---

*End of KTU 2024 Module 2 Notes — Topic: Modifications for Production on Large Scale, Cost Effectiveness, Nutritional Needs, or Uniqueness.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of Novel Food Ingredients and Novel Processing Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT835 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Use of Novel Food Ingredients and Novel Processing Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Novel" Food Ingredients?

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
A **Novel Food Ingredient** is defined as any food or food component that does not have a significant history of human consumption prior to **15 May 1997**, as established by the European Union Novel Food Regulation (EC) No 258/97, now updated under **Regulation (EU) 2015/2283**. These ingredients are intentionally engineered, biologically sourced from non-traditional origins, or processed using new techniques to deliver enhanced nutritional, functional, sensory, or health-promoting attributes beyond those of conventional ingredients.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A novel ingredient is NOT just "new to the market." It is a legally defined category where novelty is measured by the **absence of a documented pre-1997 consumption history** in the human food chain. This is a critical distinction that frequently appears in board examination questions.

### The Three Pillars of Novelty (Classification Trifecta)

| Pillar | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Novel Source** | Ingredient sourced from an organism with no prior consumption record | Algae proteins (Spirulina, Chlorella), insect protein (cricket flour) |
| **Novel Structure/Form** | Existing source but processed into a new molecular structure | Resistant starch, nano-encapsulated curcumin, micellar casein |
| **Novel Application** | Already consumed but now used for a new functional purpose | Plant sterols/stanols used as cholesterol-lowering agents |

### Conceptual Analogy: The "Wardrobe Upgrade" Model

Imagine your grandmother's traditional kitchen as a wardrobe containing only conventional Indian staples — wheat, rice, dal, jaggery, and ghee. These are the **classic garments**.

- A **novel source** is like importing silk from a country whose textiles were never worn in your family before — same purpose (clothing), but the material origin is unprecedented.
- A **novel structure** is like taking your existing cotton kurta and rebuilding it with nano-weave technology — the cotton is familiar, but the engineered version is structurally different.
- A **novel application** is like using your grandmother's silk saree as a wall tapestry instead of clothing — same silk, new function.

This trifecta is exactly how the **European Food Safety Authority (EFSA)** and **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)** classify novelty.

## 1.2 What are "Novel" Food Processing Technologies?

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
A **Novel Food Processing Technology** refers to a non-thermal or hybrid processing method that applies physical, biochemical, or electromagnetic principles to achieve microbial inactivation, enzyme deactivation, or structural modification of food while **minimizing the degradation of heat-sensitive nutrients, flavours, colours, and textures** that typically occurs in conventional thermal processing (e.g., pasteurization, sterilization, blanching).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction for Examiners:** Novel processing does NOT mean "newly invented." It means a **technologically superior alternative** to conventional thermal methods, where the primary advantage is **retention of freshness-like quality** while delivering **safety and shelf-life** equivalent to or exceeding thermal processing.

### Intuitive Analogy: The "Surgeon vs. Hammer" Model

Conventional thermal processing is like cracking a walnut with a hammer — effective, but you smash the kernel inside and lose nutrition.

Novel processing technologies are like a **skilled surgeon** who opens the walnut surgically, extracts the intact kernel, and seals the shell back. The walnut is "processed" (the goal is achieved), but the intrinsic quality of the kernel is preserved.

This is the philosophical shift behind all novel processing — **precision over brute force**.

## 1.3 The Driving Forces: Why Novelty in Food?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Consumer-Driven Demand Pillars** (Frequently tested in KTU ESE):

1. **Health & Wellness Demand** — Consumers seek functional benefits beyond basic nutrition (probiotics, prebiotics, omega-3 enrichment).
2. **Clean Label Movement** — Demand for ingredient lists that are recognizable and free from synthetic additives ("E-numbers").
3. **Sustainability Imperative** — Carbon footprint reduction, plant-based proteins to replace animal agriculture.
4. **Convenience & Shelf-Stable Freshness** — Cold-pressed, minimally processed ready-to-eat meals.
5. **Personalized Nutrition** — Customized bioactive delivery for specific demographic needs (elderly, athletes, diabetics).

## 1.4 The Global Regulatory Anchor

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Trap:** Students often confuse "novel food" with "functional food." These are NOT synonyms.
>
> - **Novel Food** = A *legal/regulatory category* based on pre-1997 consumption history.
> - **Functional Food** = A *marketing/scientific category* based on demonstrated health benefits beyond basic nutrition.
>
> A novel food MAY be functional (e.g., chia seeds), and a functional food MAY be novel (e.g., fortified oat milk with added beta-glucans), but the two terms belong to entirely different classification systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Analytical Sheet

## 2.1 Deep Dive: Categories of Novel Food Ingredients

### A. Plant-Based Novel Proteins

The shift from animal to plant proteins is driven by environmental and ethical considerations. The engineering challenge is that plant proteins often lack essential amino acids and have inferior functional properties (solubility, emulsification, foaming).

**Key Novel Plant Proteins:**

- **Pea Protein Isolate (PPI)** — Used in dairy alternatives; rich in lysine but low in methionine.
- **Soy Protein Isolate (SPI)** — Complete protein with all essential amino acids; functional in meat analogues due to viscoelastic gelation.
- **Hemp Seed Protein** — Contains edestin and albumin; high digestibility.
- **Mycoprotein (from *Fusarium venenatum*)** — Used in Quorn™ products; high in fibre and protein.
- **Algal Protein** — From spirulina (60–70% protein) and chlorella; complete amino acid profile.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked about "meat analogues," always mention the **texturization** step — high-moisture extrusion (HME) at 100–180 °C with shear, which aligns protein molecules to mimic fibrous meat texture.

### B. Functional Additives & Bioactive Compounds

| Class | Example | Functional Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Probiotics** | *Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG*, *Bifidobacterium longum* | Live microorganisms conferring health benefits at ≥10⁶ CFU/g |
| **Prebiotics** | Inulin, FOS (fructo-oligosaccharides), GOS | Non-digestible fibres selectively stimulating gut microbiota |
| **Omega-3 Fatty Acids** | EPA, DHA from algal oil (vs. fish oil) | Cardiovascular and cognitive health |
| **Phytochemicals** | Lycopene, resveratrol, curcumin | Antioxidant, anti-inflammatory |
| **Polyols/Sugar Alcohols** | Erythritol, xylitol, maltitol | Low-calorie sweeteners, dental-friendly |

### C. Novel Sweeteners & Fat Replacers

- **Steviol Glycosides (from *Stevia rebaudiana*)** — 200–300× sweeter than sucrose, zero calories.
- **Monk Fruit Extract (Mogrosides)** — 150–300× sweeter than sucrose.
- **Sucralose, Aspartame, Advantame** — High-intensity synthetic sweeteners.
- **Oatrim (β-glucan-based fat replacer)** — Mimics mouthfeel of fat at reduced calories.
- **Structured Emulsions (Pickering emulsions)** — Solid-particle stabilized emulsions replacing conventional surfactant-based fats.

### D. Novel Fibres

- **Resistant Starch (RS)** — Type 3 (retrograded) and Type 5 (starch-lipid complex); functions as prebiotic fibre.
- **β-Glucans** — From oats and barley; proven cholesterol-lowering effect (EFSA-claimed health claim: 3 g/day lowers LDL by 5–7%).
- **Psyllium Husk** — Soluble fibre with laxative properties.

### E. Fortification Carriers

- **Nano-encapsulated Iron (Fe-EDTA nanoparticles)** — Masks metallic taste, improves bioavailability.
- **Multiple-micronutrient powders (MNPs)** — Added to staple foods (wheat flour, rice) to address hidden hunger.

---

## 2.2 Deep Dive: Categories of Novel Processing Technologies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Classification Table for Board Examinations:**

| Technology | Physical Principle | Primary Application | Key Advantage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High Pressure Processing (HPP)** | 100–600 MPa isostatic pressure | Pasteurization of juices, meats, guacamole | Retains fresh-like quality, no heat |
| **Pulsed Electric Field (PEF)** | 20–80 kV/cm electric pulses | Liquid food pasteurization (juice, milk) | Continuous flow, minimal energy |
| **Ultrasound (Sonication)** | 20–100 kHz acoustic cavitation | Emulsification, extraction, microbial inactivation | Enhanced mass transfer |
| **Microwave Processing** | 915 MHz / 2450 MHz dielectric heating | Rapid volumetric heating | Reduced cooking time |
| **Ohmic Heating** | Direct electrical resistance heating | Continuous sterilization of pumpable foods | Uniform rapid heating |
| **Infrared (IR) Radiation** | Electromagnetic IR waves | Surface pasteurization, drying | Energy efficient |
| **Cold Plasma** | Ionized gas at <50 °C | Surface sterilization of fresh produce | Chemical-free decontamination |
| **UV-C Light (Non-thermal)** | 200–280 nm UV photons | Surface disinfection | Mercury-free, no chemical residue |
| **High Pressure Homogenization (HPH)** | 100–400 MPa shear/cavitation | Emulsion stabilization, nano-emulsion creation | Nanoscale droplet reduction |
| **Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SCFE)** | CO₂ above critical point (31 °C, 7.4 MPa) | Decaffeination, oil extraction | Solvent-free |

---

## 2.3 Detailed Mechanistic Analysis of Top 4 Technologies

### A. High Pressure Processing (HPP)

- **Mechanism:** Food is sealed in flexible packaging, placed in a pressure vessel filled with water (the pressure-transmitting medium), and subjected to **isostatic pressure of 400–600 MPa for 1–15 minutes**.
- **Microbial Inactivation:** Pressure disrupts the cytoplasmic membrane, denatures proteins, and damages ribosomes. The inactivation follows first-order kinetics:

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\frac{t}{D_p}$$

where $D_p$ is the decimal reduction time (minutes at a given pressure), $N_0$ is initial count, and $N$ is survivor count.

- **Critical Advantage:** The principle of **Le Chatelier's principle** and **isostatic pressure transmission** means pressure is transmitted uniformly and instantaneously throughout the food — regardless of size or geometry. This ensures uniform microbial inactivation.
- **Limitation:** Pressure only affects **non-covalent bonds**. It does NOT break covalent bonds. Therefore, **spore-forming bacteria (e.g., *Clostridium botulinum*) are resistant** to HPP. Spore inactivation requires combined treatments (e.g., HPP + mild heat = "Pressure-Assisted Thermal Sterilization — PATS").

### B. Pulsed Electric Field (PEF)

- **Mechanism:** Liquid food flows between two electrodes. Short high-voltage pulses (1–10 μs) of **20–80 kV/cm** are applied. This causes **electroporation** — the formation of permanent pores in microbial cell membranes, leading to leakage of cytoplasmic contents and cell death.
- **Critical Insight:** PEF works on **liquid foods only** because air breaks down at high voltage. Solid foods require submerged electrode configurations.
- **Energy Efficiency:** PEF uses ~10–100× less energy than thermal pasteurization because the electric field selectively targets membranes rather than bulk heating the food.

### C. Ultrasound Processing

- **Mechanism:** High-intensity ultrasound (20–100 kHz, power > 1 W/cm²) creates **acoustic cavitation** — microbubbles in the liquid rapidly collapse, generating localized temperatures of ~5000 K and pressures of ~50 MPa at the collapse point.
- **Dual Application:**
  - **Sonochemistry** — Cavitation drives free-radical formation (used for extraction of bioactives).
  - **Sonomechanics** — Microjets and shear disrupt cell walls and microbial membranes.

### D. Ohmic Heating

- **Mechanism:** Food itself acts as an electrical resistor. Alternating current (typically 50 Hz mains frequency) passes directly through the food, generating **volumetric heat via Joule heating (I²R)**.
- **Critical Engineering Requirement:** The food's **electrical conductivity** must be uniform. Foods containing large particulates (soups with chunks) require careful design to ensure particulates heat at the same rate as the carrier liquid.
- **Validation Equation:**

$$Q = V \cdot I \cdot t = \frac{V^2 \cdot t}{R}$$

where $V$ is voltage, $I$ is current, $t$ is time, and $R$ is the food's resistance.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Analytical Sheet

### Comparison of Conventional vs. Novel Processing

| Parameter | Conventional Thermal | Novel (Non-thermal/Hybrid) |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature range | 70–140 °C | < 50 °C (or mild heat) |
| Nutrient retention | Moderate to poor | High |
| Sensory quality | Cooked flavour | Fresh-like |
| Shelf life | Long (months–years) | Moderate (2–6 weeks) |
| Spore inactivation | Effective (sterilization) | Limited (requires combination) |
| Energy intensity | High | Variable (often lower) |
| Capital cost | Low | High |

### Kinetic Models for Novel Processing Inactivation

For non-thermal microbial inactivation, the **Weibull model** has been shown to better fit the non-linear survival curves than first-order kinetics:

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}$$

where $\alpha$ is the scale parameter (time for first decimal reduction) and $\beta$ is the shape parameter (concavity of the curve). When $\beta = 1$, the model reduces to first-order kinetics.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Application Spotlight:**
>
> - **HPP-Pasteurized Guacamole** (e.g., Sabra, Costco's Avocado Pulp): 600 MPa × 2 min delivers a 5-log reduction of *Listeria monocytogenes* while preserving the bright green colour and fresh avocado flavour. Shelf life extended from 3 days (fresh) to 30 days (refrigerated).
> - **PEF-Pasteurized Cold-Pressed Orange Juice** (e.g., Juice Tyme, Evolent): 35 kV/cm, 4 μs pulses, 100 μs total treatment time — preserves vitamin C (>95%) and fresh aroma, validated by 5-log reduction of *E. coli*.
> - **Ohmic-Heated Diced Carrots in Sauce** (e.g., Signature Foods Tetra Recart): 50 Hz AC, particulate heating validated against slowest-heating zone using CFD modelling.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: First-Order Inactivation Kinetics Under HPP

### Problem Setup
A batch of *E. coli* K-12 in phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) is subjected to High Pressure Processing at 450 MPa. The initial microbial load is $N_0 = 1 \times 10^6$ CFU/mL. The decimal reduction time ($D_p$) at 450 MPa is experimentally determined to be **1.2 minutes**. Determine the residual microbial load after exposure times of (a) 2 minutes, (b) 4 minutes, and (c) 6 minutes.

### Step 1: State the Governing Equation

The first-order microbial inactivation model under constant pressure is:

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\frac{t}{D_p}$$

### Step 2: Convert to a Computable Form

Taking the antilog:

$$\frac{N}{N_0} = 10^{-t/D_p}$$

Therefore:

$$N = N_0 \cdot 10^{-t/D_p}$$

### Step 3: Substitute Known Values

We have $N_0 = 1 \times 10^6$ CFU/mL and $D_p = 1.2$ min.

#### Sub-problem (a): t = 2 minutes

$$N = 1 \times 10^6 \cdot 10^{-2/1.2}$$

$$N = 1 \times 10^6 \cdot 10^{-1.667}$$

$$N = 1 \times 10^6 \cdot 0.0215$$

$$\boxed{N_a = 2.15 \times 10^4 \text{ CFU/mL}}$$

This corresponds to a 1.67-log reduction.

#### Sub-problem (b): t = 4 minutes

$$N = 1 \times 10^6 \cdot 10^{-4/1.2}$$

$$N = 1 \times 10^6 \cdot 10^{-3.333}$$

$$N = 1 \times 10^6 \cdot 4.64 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{N_b = 4.64 \times 10^2 \text{ CFU/mL}}$$

This corresponds to a 3.33-log reduction.

#### Sub-problem (c): t = 6 minutes

$$N = 1 \times 10^6 \cdot 10^{-6/1.2}$$

$$N = 1 \times 10^6 \cdot 10^{-5.0}$$

$$N = 1 \times 10^6 \cdot 1.0 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\boxed{N_c = 1.0 \times 10^{1} \text{ CFU/mL} = 10 \text{ CFU/mL}}$$

This corresponds to a 5-log reduction — a benchmark "pasteurization equivalence."

### Step 4: Engineering Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The calculation demonstrates the **log-linear behaviour** under constant-pressure HPP. Each additional 1.2 minutes reduces the surviving population by **one order of magnitude** (10×). For commercial pasteurization, a 5-log reduction is the regulatory benchmark (FDA Juice HACCP requires 5-log reduction of pertinent pathogens).

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Analysis: PEF Energy Density Calculation

### Problem Setup
A PEF system operates at an electric field strength of $E = 30$ kV/cm across a treatment chamber gap of $d = 0.5$ cm. Each pulse delivers energy $W_{pulse} = 5$ J into a food volume of $V = 10$ mL. The system delivers 100 pulses per second. Compute:
(a) The voltage across the chamber.
(b) The total energy input per litre of food per second.
(c) Whether this design is commercially viable (bench-mark: <100 kJ/kg for liquid pasteurization).

### Step (a): Voltage Across the Chamber

$$V = E \cdot d$$

$$V = 30 \text{ kV/cm} \times 0.5 \text{ cm}$$

$$\boxed{V = 15 \text{ kV} = 15{,}000 \text{ V}}$$

### Step (b): Energy Input per Litre per Second

Energy per second:

$$P = W_{pulse} \times \text{frequency}$$

$$P = 5 \text{ J/pulse} \times 100 \text{ pulses/s}$$

$$P = 500 \text{ J/s} = 500 \text{ W}$$

Energy density (per kg of food, assuming density ≈ 1 kg/L):

$$E_{density} = \frac{P}{V \cdot \rho} = \frac{500 \text{ J/s}}{0.010 \text{ L/s} \cdot 1 \text{ kg/L}}$$

$$\boxed{E_{density} = 50{,}000 \text{ J/kg} = 50 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

### Step (c): Commercial Viability

Since 50 kJ/kg **< 100 kJ/kg benchmark**, the design is **commercially viable** with a 50% energy margin.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection Guide for Novel Technologies

| Food Type | Recommended Novel Technology | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Liquid juice (cloudy) | PEF | Continuous flow, no particulate heating issues |
| Avocado pulp / guacamole | HPP | Solid-semi-solid, pressure transmits isostatically |
| Wine (clarification) | Ultrasound + PEF | Ultrasound accelerates ageing, PEF ensures microbial stability |
| Soups with particulates | Ohmic Heating | Volumetric heating solves cold-spot problem |
| Fresh-cut salad | UV-C + Cold Plasma | Surface decontamination of intact plant tissue |
| Functional oil extraction | Supercritical CO₂ | Solvent-free, GRAS-grade extract |
| Infant formula | HPP + Mild Heat | Destroys *Cronobacter* while preserving DHA/ARA |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Hurdle Technology Combination

### Problem Setup
A manufacturer wishes to combine HPP with mild thermal treatment ("Pressure-Assisted Thermal Processing" — PAT) to inactivate *Bacillus cereus* spores. The combined process operates at $P = 600$ MPa and $T = 70$ °C, achieving a $D$-value for spores of $D_{PAT} = 0.4$ min. The initial spore load is $N_0 = 1 \times 10^4$ CFU/g. Determine the treatment time required for a **6-log reduction**.

### Step 1: Required log reduction

$$\text{Log reduction required} = \log_{10}\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = 6$$

### Step 2: Apply first-order kinetics

$$t = D \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right)$$

### Step 3: Substitute values

$$t = 0.4 \text{ min} \times 6$$

$$\boxed{t = 2.4 \text{ minutes}}$$

### Step 4: Engineering Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> This 2.4-minute combined treatment is **commercially practical** for high-value products (e.g., refrigerated ready-to-eat meals). It achieves sterilization-equivalent safety (6-log spore inactivation) without the 121 °C × 3 min conventional retort, thereby preserving vitamin content and texture.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Novel Food" as per EU Regulation 2015/2283. List any four categories of novel food ingredients with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Novel Food** is defined as any food that was not used for human consumption to a significant degree in the European Union before **15 May 1997**, as per EU Regulation 2015/2283. This definition covers ingredients with no significant pre-1997 consumption history, irrespective of whether they are intrinsically new or produced through new processes.

**Four categories with examples:**

1. **Novel Source** — Algal proteins such as *Spirulina* (*Arthrospira platensis*) protein isolate, sourced from cyanobacteria not historically consumed in the EU.
2. **Novel Structure** — Nano-encapsulated curcumin, where the familiar turmeric compound is engineered into a nanocarrier for enhanced bioavailability.
3. **Novel Application** — Plant sterols (phytosterols) added to margarine spreads as cholesterol-lowering agents, where sterols were previously consumed in minor dietary amounts but now used as functional bioactives at elevated doses.
4. **Food from new production processes** — UV-C treated fresh-cut fruits or HPP-processed guacamole, where the raw material is conventional but the preservation process is novel.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert (Common 1-mark loss):**
> Students often write "novel food = new food" without stating the **1997 regulatory cutoff date**. This cutoff is the *legal anchor* of the definition. Without it, the answer is incomplete.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Differentiate between High Pressure Processing (HPP) and Pulsed Electric Field (PEF) in terms of principle, applicability, and microbial inactivation mechanism.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | HPP | PEF |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical Principle** | Isostatic pressure of 400–600 MPa transmitted via water medium | High-voltage electric pulses (20–80 kV/cm) across electrode gap |
| **Food Form Applicability** | Both solid and liquid foods (any geometry) | Liquid foods only (air causes dielectric breakdown) |
| **Inactivation Mechanism** | Disruption of cell membrane, protein denaturation, ribosome damage | Electroporation of cytoplasmic membrane; irreversible pore formation |
| **Mode of Operation** | Batch (pressure vessel) | Continuous flow |
| **Spore Inactivation** | Ineffective alone; requires combination with heat | Ineffective on spores |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> Do not write that "PEF uses heat" or "HPP uses electricity." These are common conceptual errors that cost marks. PEF uses **electric fields**, not electric heating. HPP uses **mechanical pressure**, not heat.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (Choose ONE: Either A or B)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
**(i) Explain the principle, equipment, and applications of High Pressure Processing (HPP) in food preservation. (5 marks)**
**(ii) Discuss why HPP alone cannot achieve commercial sterilization. (2 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Principle, Equipment, and Applications (5 marks):**

**Principle:** HPP is a non-thermal preservation technique based on the principle that high isostatic pressure (400–600 MPa, ~4000–6000 atmospheres) transmitted uniformly through a water medium inactivates vegetative microorganisms and certain enzymes while preserving the covalent structure of small molecules (vitamins, flavour compounds, pigments).

According to **Le Chatelier's principle**, reactions accompanied by a volume decrease are favoured under pressure. Pressure disrupts:
- Cell membrane integrity (phospholipid bilayer compression)
- Non-covalent bonds (hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interactions) leading to protein denaturation
- Ribosomal structure, halting protein synthesis

**Equipment Components:**
1. **Pressure vessel** — Thick-walled (typically forged monobloc steel) cylindrical chamber rated for 600+ MPa.
2. **Pressure-generating system** — High-pressure intensifier pump.
3. **Pressure-transmitting medium** — Water (with optional 5% castor oil lubricant for seals).
4. **Temperature control system** — Jacket for pre-heating or cooling.
5. **Control panel** — Pressure, time, and temperature sensors with PLC automation.
6. **Loading/unloading system** — Robotic or manual basket transfer.

**Process Cycle:** Loading → Vessel closure → Pressurization (5–10 min) → Hold (1–15 min) → Decompression (1–2 min) → Unloading.

**Applications:**
- HPP-pasteurized guacamole (Sabra, Costco)
- HPP-processed ready-to-eat meats (Hormel Natural Choice)
- HPP juices (Evolution Fresh, Odwalla)
- HPP oysters (raw oyster shucking + 5-log *Vibrio* reduction)
- HPP smoothies (Suja, Nektar)

**(ii) Why HPP alone cannot achieve commercial sterilization (2 marks):**

1. **Spore resistance:** Bacterial spores (*Clostridium botulinum*, *Bacillus cereus*) are highly resistant to pressure due to their dehydrated core, dipicolinic acid content, and thickened cortex. HPP at 600 MPa does not break covalent bonds, which are essential for inactivating spore structures.
2. **Pressure-induced dormancy:** Some spores are not killed but rather **sub-lethally injured**, leading to outgrowth during storage.
3. **Commercial solution:** Sterilization requires **Hurdle Technology** — combining HPP with mild thermal treatment (>70 °C), termed **Pressure-Assisted Thermal Sterilization (PATS)**, or using adjuncts like nisin.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
**A fruit juice manufacturer wants to extend the shelf life of freshly extracted orange juice using Pulsed Electric Field (PEF). Design the PEF process by specifying: (i) electric field strength, (ii) treatment time, (iii) number of pulses, (iv) target log reduction, (v) expected shelf life, and (vi) critical vitamin retention. Justify each parameter.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Electric Field Strength: 30–35 kV/cm**
*Justification:* Standard for liquid orange juice to achieve electroporation of *E. coli*, *Salmonella*, and *Listeria innocua* without excessive dielectric breakdown. Lower fields (<20 kV/cm) are insufficient; higher fields (>50 kV/cm) cause arcing and electrode corrosion.

**(ii) Treatment Time: 100–150 μs (cumulative)**
*Justification:* Empirical research (Jin & Zhang, 2005; Sampedro et al.) confirms 100 μs cumulative treatment at 30 kV/cm achieves ≥5-log reduction in orange juice. Longer treatment increases energy consumption without proportional microbial inactivation.

**(iii) Number of Pulses: 50–75 pulses (each 1–2 μs width at 1 Hz)**
*Justification:* Pulse width of 1–2 μs prevents overheating. Interpulse interval of 1 second dissipates heat. Total energy density: 50–100 kJ/kg.

**(iv) Target Log Reduction: ≥5-log**
*Justification:* FDA Juice HACCP regulation (21 CFR 120.24) requires a **5-log reduction** of the pertinent pathogen (typically *E. coli* O157:H7 or *Cryptosporidium parvum*).

**(v) Expected Shelf Life: 21–28 days at 4 °C**
*Justification:* PEF alone does not inactivate spores or enzymes (e.g., pectin methylesterase causing cloud loss). Refrigeration inhibits spore outgrowth. Shelf-life validation typically follows challenge tests with *Alicyclobacillus acidoterrestris*.

**(vi) Vitamin C Retention: ≥95%**
*Justification:* PEF's non-thermal nature preserves ascorbic acid, which is heat-labile (degrades >70 °C). Comparative studies show thermal pasteurization (95 °C × 15 s) retains only 75–85% vitamin C, while PEF retains >95%.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Marks Deduction Map:**
> - **(–2 marks)** If student confuses pulse width with total treatment time.
> - **(–1 mark)** If shelf life justification does not mention **refrigeration requirement**.
> - **(–1 mark)** If vitamin retention is stated without **comparative thermal benchmark**.
> - **(–2 marks)** If student does not reference the **FDA 5-log HACCP requirement**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
**(i) Classify novel food ingredients with suitable examples. (4 marks)**
**(ii) Explain the concept of "functional food" and how it differs from "novel food." Provide one example of a food that is both functional AND novel. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Classification of Novel Food Ingredients (4 marks):**

Novel food ingredients can be classified based on the nature of their novelty:

**Category 1: Novel Source (food from new origins)** — Ingredients from organisms with no prior human consumption history.
- *Examples:* Algal protein (*Spirulina*), insect protein (*Tenebrio molitor* — yellow mealworm), bacterial cellulose, krill oil.

**Category 2: Novel Structure / New Production Process (existing source, new form)** — Conventional source but processed into a structurally novel form.
- *Examples:* Resistant starch type 5 (starch-lipid complex), nano-curcumin, micellar casein, lactoferrin extracted from whey.

**Category 3: Novel Application (existing food, new use)** — A known ingredient applied in a previously unintended functional way.
- *Examples:* Plant sterols in cholesterol-lowering spreads, lycopene as a functional antioxidant in beverages, inulin as a prebiotic in dairy.

**Category 4: Engineered Ingredients (designer molecules)** — Created through deliberate biochemical engineering.
- *Examples:* Tailored triglyceride structured lipids (e.g., MLM-type for infant formula), sugar alcohols (xylitol via microbial fermentation).

**(ii) Functional vs. Novel Food (3 marks):**

A **Functional Food** is a food that, beyond its basic nutritional value, provides a demonstrable health benefit (e.g., reduced disease risk, improved physiological function) when consumed as part of a normal diet. The concept was formalized in **FOSHU (Foods for Specified Health Uses)** in Japan (1991) and is regulated under EFSA health claim frameworks in Europe.

**Key Distinction:**

| Aspect | Novel Food | Functional Food |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis** | Regulatory/legal (pre-1997 consumption history) | Scientific/marketing (health benefit) |
| **Governing Body** | EFSA Novel Food Catalog, FSSAI | EFSA Health Claims, FSSAI Nutraceutical Regulations |
| **Approval Criterion** | Absence of prior use | Demonstrated health effect |
| **Overlap** | Can be functional, novel, or both | Can be novel, but not required to be |

**Example of food that is BOTH functional AND novel:**
**Plant-sterol-enriched oat milk** with added beta-glucans.
- **Functional** because: Plant sterols have an EFSA-approved health claim for cholesterol lowering (3 g/day reduces LDL by 7–10%).
- **Novel** because: Oat milk itself is a novel category (plant-based dairy alternative not widely consumed pre-1997), and beta-glucans from oats at functional doses represent a novel application of an existing cereal.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
**Describe the working principle, advantages, limitations, and industrial applications of Ohmic Heating as a novel food processing technology.**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle (2 marks):**

Ohmic heating (also called Joule heating or electroconductive heating) is a thermal processing technology in which **alternating electric current (typically 50 Hz mains frequency)** is passed directly through a food product, which acts as an electrical resistor. The electrical energy is converted into heat according to **Joule's law**:

$$Q = I^2 R t = V I t$$

where $Q$ is heat generated (Joules), $I$ is current (Amperes), $V$ is voltage (Volts), $R$ is resistance (Ohms), and $t$ is time (seconds).

Unlike conventional heating where heat is transferred *from* a heated surface *to* the food (creating a temperature gradient), ohmic heating generates heat **volumetrically and instantaneously throughout the food mass**, eliminating surface-overcook and cold-spot problems.

**Critical Design Requirement:** The food's **electrical conductivity** must be uniform. For solid-liquid mixtures (e.g., chunky soup), the conductivity of particulates must match that of the carrier liquid — typically achieved by pre-soaking particulates in a salt-adjusted brine to equilibrate conductivity.

**Advantages (2 marks):**

1. **Rapid and uniform heating** — Volumetric heating eliminates lag-time; heating rates of 1–10 °C/s achievable.
2. **High energy efficiency** — 90–95% of electrical energy is converted to heat in the food (vs. 60–70% in steam retort due to surface and stack losses).
3. **Superior product quality** — Reduced overcooking at surfaces, better texture retention in particulates.
4. **Continuous processing** — Suitable for aseptic packaging lines (pumpable foods).
5. **No surface fouling** — Unlike heat exchangers, no scorching at heat-transfer surfaces.

**Limitations (2 marks):**

1. **Conductivity dependence** — Foods with low conductivity (fats, oils, syrups) heat poorly; foods with very high conductivity cause electrical short-circuiting.
2. **Particulate size limitation** — Particulates >25 mm can have cold centres if conductivity mismatched.
3. **Electrode corrosion** — Metal ion migration into food requires inert electrodes (titanium, graphite, platinized titanium).
4. **Regulatory sensitivity** — In India, FSSAI requires food-contact surfaces of electrodes to be **IS 3024 grade stainless steel** or equivalent inert material.
5. **Bubble formation** — Dissolved gases can cause arcing; deaeration is mandatory before ohmic processing.

**Industrial Applications (1 mark):**

1. **Signature Foods Tetra Recart** — Ohmic-heated ready-to-eat meals with chunks (carrots, potatoes, meat) in sauce for shelf-stable ambient storage.
2. **Aseptic fruit purees** (apple, mango) — High viscosity, uniform heating, no scorching.
3. **Liquid egg pasteurization** — APV ohmic systems for in-shell egg internal pasteurization research (USDA-ARS).
4. **Tomato paste sterilization** — Continuous flow ohmic + aseptic filling (Rossi & Catelli, Italy).
5. **Crab meat and seafood** — Reduces overcooking, preserves delicate texture.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Marks Deduction Map:**
> - **(–1 mark)** If "ohmic heating = microwave" — they are entirely different mechanisms (microwave uses dielectric polarization of water at 2450 MHz; ohmic uses direct current at 50 Hz).
> - **(–2 marks)** If Joule's law equation is incorrect (forgetting $I^2R$ form vs. $VI$).
> - **(–1 mark)** If the 50 Hz frequency is not mentioned (this is critical to avoid electrolysis).
> - **(–1 mark)** If "ohmic heating is non-thermal" is stated — it IS thermal, only novel in its heating mechanism.

---
