- ✅ **Three pillars of novelty**: Novel Source, Novel Structure, Novel Application.
- ✅ **Novel Food ≠ Functional Food** — regulatory vs. scientific classification.
- ✅ **Plant-based proteins**: Pea, soy, hemp, mycoprotein (Quorn), algal — focus on amino acid completeness and functional properties.
- ✅ **High-intensity sweeteners**: Stevia (200–300×), Monk Fruit (150–300×), Sucralose (600×).
- ✅ **Prebiotics** (inulin, FOS, GOS) selectively feed **probiotics** (*Lactobacillus*, *Bifidobacterium*).
- ✅ **β-glucan EFSA claim**: 3 g/day lowers LDL cholesterol by 7–10%.
- ✅ **Insect protein** requires regulatory approval in EU (since 2021, under EFSA authorization) — sustainability angle.

### Novel Processing Technologies — Core Concepts
- ✅ **HPP**: 400–600 MPa, isostatic, batch, spore-resistant, fresh-like retention.
- ✅ **PEF**: 20–80 kV/cm, electroporation, **liquid only**, continuous.
- ✅ **Ultrasound**: Acoustic cavitation, microjets at 5000 K / 50 MPa, dual sonochemistry/sonomechanics.
- ✅ **Ohmic heating**: Joule's law $Q = I^2Rt$, 50 Hz AC, volumetric, particulate-friendly.
- ✅ **Microwave**: 2450 MHz (domestic) / 915 MHz (industrial) dielectric heating.
- ✅ **Cold plasma**: Ionized gas <50 °C, surface sterilization of fresh produce.
- ✅ **Hurdle technology** = combining novel technologies to overcome individual limitations (e.g., HPP + heat = PATS for spores).

### Critical Numerical Benchmarks to Memorize
- ✅ HPP commercial range: **400–600 MPa, 1–15 min**
- ✅ PEF field strength: **30–35 kV/cm, 100–150 μs cumulative treatment**
- ✅ FDA 5-log HACCP reduction for juice pasteurization
- ✅ Pressure-assisted thermal sterilization (PATS): **600 MPa + 70–90 °C**
- ✅ Supercritical CO₂ critical point: **31 °C, 7.4 MPa**
- ✅ Ohmic heating frequency: **50 Hz** (avoid electrolysis)
- ✅ Sterilization vs. Pasteurization: **12D *C. botulinum*** vs. **5-log pathogen reduction**

### Common Examination Traps to Avoid
- ❌ Confusing "novel food" with "new product launch" (novelty is regulatory, not marketing).
- ❌ Claiming HPP destroys spores (it does not — requires combination).
- ❌ Applying PEF to solid foods (it is liquid-only).
- ❌ Equating ohmic heating with microwave heating (entirely different principles).
- ❌ Stating "non-thermal" for ohmic heating (it IS thermal — novel only in mechanism).
- ❌ Missing the **1997 cutoff date** in the definition of novel food.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process and Equipment Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT835 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Process and Equipment Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Process Design vs. Equipment Design

**Process Design** is the systematic selection and sequencing of unit operations required to transform raw agricultural/food materials into a finished, shelf-stable, market-ready product. It defines the *what*, *how*, and *in what sequence* transformations occur.

**Equipment Design** is the engineering specification of the physical hardware (vessels, heat exchangers, pumps, mixers, dryers, fillers) that will execute those unit operations. It defines the *size*, *materials of construction*, *heat-transfer area*, *agitator horsepower*, and *utility requirements*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Distinction:** Process design answers *"What happens to the food?"* while equipment design answers *"What machine does it, and how big must the machine be?"* Both are interdependent — you cannot size a pasteurizer (equipment) without first defining the time-temperature lethality requirement (process).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Think of **baking a birthday cake at home**:
- **Process Design** is your *recipe card* — it says "cream butter, add sugar, sift flour, bake at 180 °C for 35 min." It tells you what transformations happen in what order.
- **Equipment Design** is *choosing your oven* — a small 20 L OTG vs. a 60 L convection oven with a fan. The bigger oven changes the airflow pattern, baking uniformity, and energy use. If the recipe says 35 min, the equipment must actually deliver that heat to the cake center.

Scaling this from a home kitchen to an industrial **kilo-ton-per-day food plant** is precisely what Process and Equipment Design accomplishes.

## 1.3 Why It Matters for Food Engineering (KTU 2024 NEP Context)

- **Food Safety Compliance**: Process design must guarantee destruction of pathogens (e.g., *Salmonella*, *Listeria*) — quantified by the **D-value** (decimal reduction time) and **z-value** (temperature increase for 10× change in D).
- **Nutritional & Sensory Retention**: Over-processing destroys vitamins and creates off-flavors. Process design balances safety with quality.
- **Capital & Operating Cost**: Equipment sized 2× too large wastes ₹ lakhs in capital; sized 2× too small bottlenecks production.
- **Scale-Up Safety**: Heat and mass transfer coefficients differ between a 5 L lab kettle and a 5000 L production kettle. Equipment design must compensate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics to Remember (Kerala Board Favourite):**
> - **D-value**: minutes to achieve a **1-log (90%)** reduction of target microorganism at temperature T.
> - **z-value**: °C required to change D-value by a factor of **10**.
> - **F-value (F₀)**: equivalent minutes at reference temperature (usually **121.1 °C** for sterilization, **70 °C / 160 °F** for pasteurization) delivering a stated lethality.
> - **Lethal Rate (L)**: ratio of actual kill rate at temperature T to the kill rate at reference temperature.
> - **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient, U**: W/(m²·K) — measures how easily heat crosses a wall.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Design Workflow (Sequential Logic)

A KTU-valid food process design follows this canonical order:

1. **Define Product Specifications** — target moisture content, water activity (a_w), pH, shelf life, package format.
2. **Construct a Block Flow Diagram (BFD)** — raw material → primary processing → preservation → packaging → storage.
3. **Select Unit Operations** — e.g., blanching, pasteurization, hot-fill, aseptic fill, spray drying, freeze drying, retort.
4. **Establish Process Parameters** — time-temperature profiles, pressures, flow rates using thermal death kinetics.
5. **Perform Mass & Energy Balances** — quantify inputs/outputs of mass and heat at every stream.
6. **Specify Equipment** — geometry, materials (SS-304, SS-316L), heat transfer area, agitation.
7. **Select Utilities** — steam (kg/h), cooling water (m³/h), electricity (kW), compressed air (Nm³/h).
8. **Cost Estimation & Safety Review** — HACCP, FMEA, CIP/SIP validation.

## 2.2 Microbial Lethality — The Thermal Process Equations

The foundational equation relating D-value and z-value:

$$\log\left(\frac{D_{ref}}{D_T}\right) = \frac{T_{ref} - T}{z}$$

This rearranges to the **D-value at any temperature T**:

$$D_T = D_{ref} \cdot 10^{\frac{T_{ref} - T}{z}}$$

The **Lethal Rate** at any temperature T is:

$$L = 10^{\frac{T - T_{ref}}{z}}$$

The **Total Process Lethality (F-value)** integrates lethal rate over time:

$$F = \int_{0}^{t} L \, dt = \int_{0}^{t} 10^{\frac{T(t) - T_{ref}}{z}} \, dt$$

In discrete form (for stepped temperature data):

$$F = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i \cdot \Delta t_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} 10^{\frac{T_i - T_{ref}}{z}} \cdot \Delta t_i$$

## 2.3 Equipment Sizing — Heat Transfer Core

For a **heat exchanger** (pasteurizer, sterilizer, blancher), required area:

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}}$$

Where **Q** is total heat duty (W), **U** is overall heat transfer coefficient, and **ΔT_LMTD** is the log-mean temperature difference:

$$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}$$

For a **batch vessel** (kettle, retort) heated/cooled by jacket:

$$Q = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{T_{steam} - T_{final}}{T_{steam} - T_{initial}}\right) = -\frac{U \cdot A}{m \cdot C_p} \cdot t$$

## 2.4 Mass Balance for Dehydration / Evaporation

For a single-effect evaporator concentrating juice from x_in to x_out:

$$\dot{m}_{in} \cdot x_{in} = \dot{m}_{out} \cdot x_{out}$$

$$\dot{m}_{vapor} = \dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out}$$

**Water Removed (kg/h)**:

$$W = \dot{m}_{in}\left(1 - \frac{x_{in}}{x_{out}}\right)$$

## 2.5 Holding Tube Sizing (HTST Pasteurization)

For a laminar/turbulent flow holding tube of length L and inner diameter d, holding time:

$$t_{hold} = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{\pi d^{2} L}{4 Q}$$

Reynolds number check (turbulent for sanitary flow):

$$Re = \frac{4 Q}{\pi d \nu} \geq 10{,}000$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|----------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $D_T = D_{ref} \cdot 10^{(T_{ref}-T)/z}$ | D in min, T in °C | Microbial destruction rate at any T |
| 2 | $L = 10^{(T - T_{ref})/z}$ | L dimensionless | Lethal rate (instant kill efficiency) |
| 3 | $F = \sum L_i \cdot \Delta t_i$ | F in min | Total integrated lethality |
| 4 | $A = Q / (U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD})$ | A in m², Q in W, U in W/(m²·K) | Heat exchanger area |
| 5 | $\Delta T_{LMTD} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)/\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$ | ΔT in K | Driving force for heat transfer |
| 6 | $W = \dot{m}_{in}(1 - x_{in}/x_{out})$ | W in kg/h, x as mass fraction | Evaporator water removal |
| 7 | $t_{hold} = \pi d^{2} L / (4 Q)$ | t in s, d & L in m, Q in m³/s | Pasteurizer holding tube |
| 8 | $Re = 4Q/(\pi d \nu)$ | Re dimensionless, ν in m²/s | Sanitary flow regime check |
| 9 | $Q = U A \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Same as #4 | Sizing for known heat duty |
| 10 | $P_{agitator} = N_p \rho N^{3} D^{5}$ | P in W, N in rps, D in m | Mixer power draw |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dairy Plants (Kerala context)**: HTST pasteurizer design uses the 16 s @ 72 °C / 15 s @ 75 °C standards (F₀ ≈ 0.5–1.0 min for *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*).
- **Coconut & Spices Industries**: Fluidized bed drying equipment sized using U ≈ 50–100 W/(m²·K) for particulates.
- **Marine/Aqua Exports**: Retort sterilization for tuna/seer fish pouches targeting *Clostridium botulinum* (F₀ ≥ 3 min, D₁₂₁ = 0.2 min, z = 10 °C).
- **Spice Oleoresin Plants**: Solvent extraction with Soxhlet + distillation equipment design with mass balance on yield.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** For HTST pasteurization of milk, the legal minimum in India (FSSAI) is **71.7 °C for 15 s** — equivalent to a **3-log reduction of *Coxiella burnetii***. Always cite this in long-answer questions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: Lethal Rate Integration for a Cooling Curve

A retort temperature profile during a sterilization cook is given as a stepped cooling leg:

| Interval i | Time Δt (min) | Temperature Tᵢ (°C) |
|------------|---------------|---------------------|
| 1 | 4 | 118 |
| 2 | 4 | 110 |
| 3 | 4 | 100 |
| 4 | 4 | 92 |

Given: **D₁₂₁ = 1.5 min**, **z = 10 °C**, **T_ref = 121.1 °C**. Compute the lethality contributed by the cooling leg.

**Step 1 — Write the lethal rate equation.**

The lethal rate at any temperature T is by definition the ratio of the kill rate at T to the kill rate at the reference temperature T_ref. Since a 10 °C rise in T reduces the D-value by a factor of 10, the kill rate increases by 10× for every 10 °C rise. Therefore:

$$L = 10^{(T - T_{ref})/z}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate L for each interval.**

For i = 1, T = 118 °C:

$$L_1 = 10^{(118 - 121.1)/10} = 10^{-0.31} = 0.4898$$

For i = 2, T = 110 °C:

$$L_2 = 10^{(110 - 121.1)/10} = 10^{-1.11} = 0.0776$$

For i = 3, T = 100 °C:

$$L_3 = 10^{(100 - 121.1)/10} = 10^{-2.11} = 0.00776$$

For i = 4, T = 92 °C:

$$L_4 = 10^{(92 - 121.1)/10} = 10^{-2.91} = 0.001230$$

**Step 3 — Multiply each lethal rate by its interval duration.**

| i | L_i (dimensionless) | Δt_i (min) | L_i · Δt_i (min) |
|---|---------------------|-----------|-------------------|
| 1 | 0.4898 | 4 | 1.959 |
| 2 | 0.0776 | 4 | 0.310 |
| 3 | 0.00776 | 4 | 0.0310 |
| 4 | 0.001230 | 4 | 0.00492 |

**Step 4 — Sum to obtain F for the cooling leg.**

$$F_{cool} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} L_i \cdot \Delta t_i$$

$$F_{cool} = 1.959 + 0.310 + 0.0310 + 0.00492$$

$$F_{cool} = 2.305 \, \text{min}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.**

The cooling leg delivers **F₀ = 2.305 min** of equivalent lethality at 121.1 °C. If the target was a **12-log reduction of *Clostridium botulinum*** (i.e., F₀ ≥ 2.52 min with D₁₂₁ = 0.21 min), the heat-up leg must contribute the remaining F₀ to ensure botulinum safety.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation: Heat Exchanger Area for a Milk Pasteurizer

A dairy plant processes **20,000 L/h** of milk from 4 °C to 72 °C using hot water heated from 85 °C → 80 °C. Milk C_p = 3.9 kJ/(kg·K), density ρ = 1030 kg/m³. Take U = 2500 W/(m²·K). Calculate the required heat transfer area.

**Step 1 — Compute mass flow rate.**

$$\dot{m} = 20{,}000 \times 1.030 = 20{,}600 \, \text{kg/h}$$

$$\dot{m} = 20{,}600 / 3600 = 5.722 \, \text{kg/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute heat duty Q.**

$$Q = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T_{milk}$$

$$Q = 5.722 \times 3900 \times (72 - 4)$$

$$Q = 5.722 \times 3900 \times 68$$

$$Q = 1{,}517{,}330 \, \text{W} \approx 1.517 \, \text{MW}$$

**Step 3 — Compute log-mean temperature difference.**

Counter-current arrangement: milk enters at 4 °C, meets water leaving at 80 °C; milk leaves at 72 °C, meets water entering at 85 °C.

$$\Delta T_1 = 80 - 4 = 76 \, °C$$

$$\Delta T_2 = 85 - 72 = 13 \, °C$$

$$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{76 - 13}{\ln(76/13)} = \frac{63}{\ln(5.846)} = \frac{63}{1.7656} = 35.68 \, °C$$

**Step 4 — Compute required area A.**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}} = \frac{1{,}517{,}330}{2500 \times 35.68}$$

$$A = \frac{1{,}517{,}330}{89{,}200} = 17.01 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Equipment selection.**

A plate heat exchanger (PHE) of approximately **17 m²** plate area with plate dimensions 0.5 m × 1.0 m giving ~0.25 m² per plate would need ~70 plates. Choose a standard **80-plate unit** (gives margin for fouling).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Equipment Selection vs. Product Need

| Product Category | Critical Process Need | Recommended Equipment | Key Design Parameter |
|------------------|----------------------|-----------------------|----------------------|
| Liquid milk, juice | Microbial kill + minimal flavor change | HTST Plate Heat Exchanger | F₀ ≥ 15 s @ 72 °C |
| Soups, ready-meals | Long shelf life at ambient | Retort autoclave / continuous rotary | F₀ ≥ 3 min (low-acid) |
| Powdered milk, whey | Moisture removal, low bulk density | Spray dryer | Outlet air T ≈ 85–95 °C |
| Instant coffee, herbs | Preserve volatiles & color | Freeze dryer (lyophilizer) | Shelf T ≈ 25 °C, condenser −40 °C |
| Coconut oil, spice oils | Mechanical separation | Screw press / expeller | Compression ratio 8:1 to 20:1 |
| Sauces, ketchup | Concentrate, inactivate pectinases | Vacuum evaporator | Single/Multi-effect, Brix 32 → 68 |
| Bakery, snacks | Texture, color, shelf life | Tunnel / Impingement oven | Air velocity 5–15 m/s |
| Confectionery | Crystallization control | Jacketed kettle with scraper | Heat flux ≤ 4 kW/m² |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Design — Block Flow Diagram for a Fruit Juice Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Fruit Reception] --> B[Sorting and Washing]
    B --> C[Crushing and Pulping]
    C --> D[Enzyme Treatment 50 C 60 min]
    D --> E[Filtration / Centrifugation]
    E --> F[HTST Pasteurizer 95 C 30 s]
    F --> G[Vacuum Evaporator 32 to 68 Brix]
    G --> H[Aseptic Filling Tank]
    H --> I[Aseptic Packaging]
    I --> J[Warehouse Storage]
    F --> K[Utility Loop Steam and Cooling Water]
    G --> K
    H --> K
```

**Reading guide:** Solid arrows = process flow; arrows to the utility loop represent steam input and cooling-water return. Every block is a unit operation whose design parameters are specified in the **Process & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)**.

## 4.2 Equipment Design — HTST Pasteurizer Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph REGEN[Regeneration Section]
        R1[Raw Milk In 4 C] --> R2[Plate Pack Cold Side]
        R2 --> R3[Raw Milk Out 18 C]
        R3 --> R4[Heating Section Inlet]
    end

    subgraph HEAT[Heating Section]
        R4 --> H1[Hot Water 85 C In]
        H1 --> H2[Plate Pack Hot Side]
        H2 --> H3[Milk 72 C Out]
    end

    subgraph HOLD[Holding Tube]
        H3 --> T1[Tube Length Sized for 15 s]
        T1 --> T2[Flow Diversion Valve]
        T2 --> T3[Forward to Cooler if T is 72 C]
        T2 --> T4[Divert to Balance Tank if T less than 72 C]
    end

    subgraph COOL[Cooling Section]
        T3 --> C1[Plate Pack Cold Side]
        C1 --> C2[Milk 4 C Out]
        R2 -.regenerative heat recovery.-> C1
    end
```

**Reading guide:** This is a *Block-Level Functional Architecture* showing how raw milk (4 °C) flows counter-current against hot water and chilled water through a multi-section plate pack, with a 15-second holding tube and a legal **flow diversion device (FDD)** that automatically recirculates under-processed milk.

## 4.3 Equipment Design — Batch Retort Vessel

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VESSEL[Retort Vessel SS 316L]
        IN[Steam Inlet Top] --> SHELL[Cylindrical Shell 1.6 MPa Rated]
        AIR[Air Inlet for Overpressure] --> SHELL
        SHELL --> JKT[Steam Jacket]
        JKT --> COND[Condensate Outlet]
        SHELL --> PROD[Product Carrier Trays]
        PROD --> PT[PT 100 Thermocouple at Cold Spot]
        PT --> CTRL[PID Controller to Steam Valve]
    end

    CTRL --> REC[Data Recorder for F0 Calculation]
    REC --> HACCP[HACCP and F0 Archive]
```

**Reading guide:** The thermocouple at the *cold spot* (slowest heating point inside the can/pouch) feeds a controller that adjusts steam valve opening. The accumulated F₀ is logged for regulatory compliance under FSSAI Schedule 4.

## 4.4 Mass-Energy Integration Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FEED[Fresh Feed Stream m1 x1 h1] --> EVAP[Evaporator]
    EVAP --> VAP[Water Vapour mV hV]
    EVAP --> CONC[Concentrate m2 x2 h2]
    STEAM[Steam from Boiler mS] --> EVAP
    EVAP --> COND[Condensate Return]
    VAP --> VAC[Vacuum Condenser]
    VAC --> CW[Cooling Water to Cooling Tower]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "F-value" as used in food process design. What reference temperature is conventionally used for sterilization calculations?**

**Model Answer:**
The **F-value** (also written **F₀**) is the total equivalent process time, in minutes, at a specified reference temperature that delivers the same microbial lethality as the actual time-temperature history. It is mathematically the integral of lethal rate L over the process time:

$$F = \int_0^t 10^{(T(t) - T_{ref})/z} \, dt$$

The conventional reference temperature is **T_ref = 121.1 °C (250 °F)** for sterilization (canned low-acid foods) and **70 °C (160 °F)** or **72 °C** for pasteurization calculations. (3 marks: 1 definition, 1 equation, 1 reference temperature)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Process Design and Equipment Design in the context of a food plant.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Process Design | Equipment Design |
|--------|---------------|------------------|
| Focus | What transformations occur | What hardware executes them |
| Deliverable | Block flow diagram, mass/energy balance, control strategy | Equipment list, datasheets, P&ID, GA drawings |
| Key questions | Time-temperature profile, F₀ target | Vessel volume, agitator HP, heat transfer area |
| Driven by | Product specification & food safety | Process design outputs + standards (ASME, IS) |

Process design is the *upstream* activity; equipment design is its *physical realization*. (3 marks)

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** A fruit juice (C_p = 3.85 kJ/(kg·K), ρ = 1050 kg/m³) is to be pasteurized at a flow rate of 15,000 L/h from 25 °C to 92 °C using a plate heat exchanger. Hot water enters at 95 °C and leaves at 80 °C. Assume U = 2800 W/(m²·K). Calculate:
(i) the heat duty in kW,
(ii) the log-mean temperature difference,
(iii) the required heat transfer area.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of **z-value** with reference to thermal destruction of *Clostridium botulinum* spores. A sterilization process delivers a lethal rate of 4.0 at the cold spot for 3 min. The D-value at 121.1 °C is 0.21 min and z = 10 °C. Verify if the process achieves a 12-log reduction of *C. botulinum*.

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Mass flow rate.**

$$\dot{m} = 15{,}000 \times 1.050 = 15{,}750 \, \text{kg/h} = 4.375 \, \text{kg/s}$$

**Step 2 — Heat duty.**

$$Q = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T = 4.375 \times 3850 \times (92 - 25)$$

$$Q = 4.375 \times 3850 \times 67 = 1{,}128{,}156 \, \text{W} \approx 1128 \, \text{kW}$$

**Step 3 — LMTD (counter-current).**

$$\Delta T_1 = 80 - 25 = 55 \, °C, \quad \Delta T_2 = 95 - 92 = 3 \, °C$$

$$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{55 - 3}{\ln(55/3)} = \frac{52}{\ln(18.33)} = \frac{52}{2.909} = 17.88 \, °C$$

**Step 4 — Heat transfer area.**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}} = \frac{1{,}128{,}156}{2800 \times 17.88}$$

$$A = \frac{1{,}128{,}156}{50{,}064} = 22.53 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Result:** Required plate-pack area ≈ **22.5 m²** → select a **30 m² standard unit** with 25% fouling margin.

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Explanation of z-value (3 marks):**
The **z-value** is the temperature increase (in °C) required to reduce the D-value by a factor of 10, or equivalently, to increase the destruction rate by 10×. For *Clostridium botulinum* spores (the reference pathogen in low-acid canned foods), the standard values are:

$$D_{121.1} = 0.21 \, \text{min}, \quad z = 10 \, °C$$

A lower z-value means the organism is *more heat-sensitive* (small T changes produce large lethality changes). Botulinum's z = 10 °C is the most conservative (thermophilic) reference used by FSSAI/USFDA.

**Verification of 12-log reduction (4 marks):**

Lethality delivered at cold spot:

$$F_0 = L \cdot t = 4.0 \times 3 = 12 \, \text{min}$$

For a 12-log reduction of *C. botulinum*:

$$F_{0,required} = 12 \times D_{121.1} = 12 \times 0.21 = 2.52 \, \text{min}$$

Since **F₀ delivered (12 min) ≫ F₀ required (2.52 min)**, the process *comfortably* achieves 12-log reduction — in fact, it delivers an over-process margin of nearly 5×. The process is **safe but borderline overcooked**, suggesting an opportunity to shorten the hold time and improve nutrient retention.

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** A single-effect evaporator concentrates **10,000 kg/h** of tomato pulp from **6% total solids** to **28% total solids**. Calculate:
(i) the mass flow rate of concentrate,
(ii) the water removed per hour,
(iii) the steam required (kg/h) if the steam economy is **0.85 kg water evaporated per kg steam**.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the selection criteria for **materials of construction (MOC)** in food process equipment. Compare SS-304 and SS-316L with reference to corrosion resistance, weldability, and typical food applications.

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Mass balance (overall and solids).**

$$F = 10{,}000 \, \text{kg/h}, \quad x_F = 0.06, \quad x_L = 0.28$$

Solids in feed = solids in concentrate:

$$F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$$

$$10{,}000 \times 0.06 = L \times 0.28$$

$$L = \frac{600}{0.28} = 2142.86 \, \text{kg/h}$$

**Step 2 — Water removed.**

$$V = F - L = 10{,}000 - 2142.86 = 7857.14 \, \text{kg/h}$$

Alternatively, by the water-removal formula:

$$W = F\left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right) = 10{,}000\left(1 - \frac{0.06}{0.28}\right) = 10{,}000 \times 0.7857 = 7857.14 \, \text{kg/h}$$

**Step 3 — Steam required at 0.85 economy.**

$$S = \frac{W}{\text{Economy}} = \frac{7857.14}{0.85} = 9243.7 \, \text{kg/h} \approx 9.24 \, \text{ton/h}$$

**Result:** The evaporator needs approximately **9.24 ton/h of saturated steam** (typically 3 bar / 134 °C for tomato pulp to avoid scorching).

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Materials of Construction — Selection Criteria (3.5 marks):**
1. **Corrosion resistance** to product (acids, salts) and cleaning chemicals (CIP NaOH, HNO₃).
2. **Sanitary finish** — surface roughness Ra ≤ 0.8 µm (3-A/EHEDG standards) to prevent microbial adhesion.
3. **Mechanical strength** at operating T and P.
4. **Weldability & inspectability** (polished welds, no crevices).
5. **Thermal conductivity** for heat-transfer surfaces.
6. **Cost and availability**.
7. **Non-reactivity** with food (no leaching of Ni/Cr into acidic products).

**SS-304 vs SS-316L Comparison (3.5 marks):**

| Property | SS-304 (18 Cr / 8 Ni) | SS-316L (16 Cr / 10 Ni / 2 Mo, low C) |
|----------|-----------------------|---------------------------------------|
| Corrosion resistance | Good for neutral foods | **Superior** — Mo resists Cl⁻ pitting; essential for salty/brined foods, dairy |
| Weldability | Good; carbide precipitation in HAZ possible | **Excellent** — low C (≤ 0.03%) prevents sensitization; no post-weld anneal |
| Typical food use | Storage tanks for grains/oils, conveyor frames | Pasteurizer plates, retort vessels, dairy piping, sea-food lines |
| Cost | Lower (≈ 1.0×) | ~1.3–1.5× of 304 |
| Surface finish achievable | Ra 0.6 µm possible | Ra 0.4 µm easily achievable (sanitary standard) |

**Verdict:** For tomato products (pH ≈ 4.3, contains Cl⁻ from salt addition), **SS-316L is the preferred MOC** for the evaporator body, vapor line, and concentrate piping. SS-304 may be used for non-contact structural frames and storage of neutral oils.

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss traps in Process & Equipment Design questions:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in Q = m·C_p·ΔT** — students frequently mix kg/h with W. Always convert mass flow to kg/s before multiplying by C_p in J/(kg·K). *(Loss: 1–2 marks per part)*
> 2. **Confusing F₀ with t (process time)** — F₀ is the *equivalent* time at reference T, not the actual process time. The phrase "lethality delivered" must reference F₀ explicitly.
> 3. **Forgetting the 25% fouling margin** in heat-exchanger area — KTU examiners expect you to recommend a *standard size* 20–30% larger than the calculated minimum.
> 4. **Using SS-304 for salty/acidic foods** — a guaranteed 0.5-mark deduction. Always justify MOC with the specific chloride / pH condition.
> 5. **Misapplying z-value to *C. botulinum*** — using z = 7 °C or 8 °C (which are for flat-sour organisms) is wrong; **botulinum reference z = 10 °C** must be cited.
> 6. **Sketching no diagram** in 14-mark questions — even a small block-flow sketch earns 1–2 marks (especially for parts involving process description).

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Process design** = unit operations + time-temperature + safety; **Equipment design** = sizing + MOC + utilities.
- **D-value** = time for 1-log kill (min) at temperature T; **z-value** = °C for 10× change in D.
- **F-value** = integrated lethal time (min) at T_ref; for *C. botulinum* commercial sterility **F₀ ≥ 3 min** is the typical FSSAI benchmark.
- **HTST pasteurization** of milk: **72 °C × 15 s** (FSSAI).
- **Lethal rate equation:** $L = 10^{(T - T_{ref})/z}$; total $F = \sum L \cdot \Delta t$.
- **Heat exchanger area:** $A = Q/(U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD})$ — apply **20–30% fouling margin**.
- **Evaporator water removal:** $W = F(1 - x_F/x_L)$; steam = W / economy.
- **MOC preference order** in food industry: **SS-316L > SS-304 > MS with epoxy coat (non-contact only)**.
- **Holding tube Reynolds number Re ≥ 10,000** for turbulent sanitary flow in HTST.
- **Mass balance** is the first calculation — *never skip* it before sizing equipment.
- **P&ID-level thinking** is expected in 14-mark answers: include inlet/outlet streams, utility connections, and instrumentation (PT, TT, FDD).
- **Common Kerala industries to reference** for context: coconut oil, marine (tuna), spices (pepper, cardamom), dairy, cashew, banana chips.
- **HACCP and F₀ documentation** is a must for any thermal process in a KTU 14-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manufacturing Protocol and Establishing Process Parameters for Optimum Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT835 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Manufacturing Protocol and Establishing Process Parameters for Optimum Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Manufacturing Protocol** (also termed *Master Manufacturing Record* or *Master Production Document*) is a precisely documented, batch-specific, step-by-step set of instructions that defines every unit operation, raw material specification, equipment setting, in-process control, environmental condition, and operator action required to reproducibly convert raw materials into a finished food product of consistent quality, safety, and regulatory compliance. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, it is the operational bridge between the product prototype (developed at lab scale) and commercial scale production.

**Establishing Process Parameters for Optimum Quality** is the systematic engineering activity of identifying, controlling, and fine-tuning the *Critical Process Parameters* (CPPs) — such as temperature, time, pressure, pH, moisture, shear rate, and ingredient ratios — that directly influence *Critical Quality Attributes* (CQAs) such as texture, color, flavor, shelf-life, and nutritional value, using statistical, sensory, and analytical tools to maximize product quality while minimizing variability, cost, and waste.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic sits at the intersection of **Product Development**, **Process Engineering**, and **Quality Management Systems (QMS)**. It is the practical application of **Quality by Design (QbD)** and **Design of Experiments (DoE)** principles in food manufacturing.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Standard Glossary)

| Term | Full Form | Meaning |
|------|-----------|---------|
| CQA | Critical Quality Attribute | A physical, chemical, biological, or microbiological property that must be within a defined limit to ensure product quality. |
| CPP | Critical Process Parameter | A process variable whose variability impacts CQA and therefore must be monitored and controlled. |
| PAR | Proven Acceptable Range | The range of a CPP within which the CQA remains acceptable. |
| NOR | Normal Operating Range | The narrower range used in routine production for consistency. |
| QbD | Quality by Design | A systematic, science- and risk-based approach to product development. |
| DoE | Design of Experiments | A statistical method to plan experiments and analyze the effect of multiple variables simultaneously. |
| CoA | Certificate of Analysis | A document confirming that a product meets its specifications. |
| CoC | Certificate of Conformity | Document confirming compliance with standards and regulations. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe & Oven Thermostat" Intuition

Imagine you have perfected a chocolate cake recipe at home. You know that baking at **180°C for 25 minutes** gives a moist, fluffy cake, while **200°C for 30 minutes** makes it dry and burnt. Now, suppose you want to mass-produce this cake in a factory with a 1000-liter industrial oven.

- The **recipe** with ingredient amounts = the *Formulation* (developed in earlier modules).
- The **home baking steps** = the *Lab-Scale Prototype* process.
- The **industrial oven settings, conveyor speed, mixing RPM, humidity, cooling time** = the *Process Parameters* you must now lock down.

If you simply transfer the home recipe to the factory, the cake will likely be imperfect. You need to:
1. **Translate** the home instructions into industrial protocols (recipes in kg, times in minutes, etc.).
2. **Test** multiple parameter combinations — say, 170°C, 180°C, 190°C at 20, 25, 30 minutes.
3. **Measure** quality outcomes — moisture, hardness, color, taste.
4. **Identify** the parameter combination that gives the best cake.
5. **Document** everything in a Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) so every factory shift can reproduce the same cake.

This entire workflow is what KTU 2024 calls **Manufacturing Protocol Development and Process Parameter Optimization**. The "optimum" is the sweet spot where quality is maximum, cost is reasonable, and the process is robust against small variations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Topic Matters in KTU Exams:** Questions from this unit are frequently asked in the ESE (End Semester Exam) and are highly practical. They test whether a student can design a process flow, identify CPPs, and apply basic statistical optimization — a core competency expected of Food Technology graduates entering the industry.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four-Stage Framework for Manufacturing Protocol Development

A robust manufacturing protocol is built in four sequential stages, each addressing a specific set of engineering and quality questions.

#### Stage 1: Pre-Production Engineering & Documentation
Before any production trial, the following must be ready:
- **Bill of Materials (BOM):** All raw materials, packaging materials, and their specifications.
- **Equipment List:** Every machine, its capacity, and its qualification status (IQ/OQ/PQ — Installation, Operational, and Performance Qualification).
- **Facility & Environment:** HVAC class, room classification, water system, lighting, sanitation SOPs.
- **Regulatory Checklist:** FSSAI, BIS, ISO, HACCP, GMP compliance requirements.
- **Risk Assessment Document:** FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) pre-filled for anticipated failures.

#### Stage 2: Process Flow Mapping
The complete manufacturing sequence is broken down into **unit operations**. Each unit operation is a *process step* that transforms the material in some way (size reduction, mixing, heating, cooling, forming, packaging).

```
Raw Material Receipt → Storage → Pre-processing 
   → Weighing/Batching → Mixing/Blending 
   → Primary Processing (heat/shear) → Forming/Shaping 
   → Secondary Processing (bake/fry/dry) → Cooling 
   → Packaging → Storage → Distribution
```

For each step, three documents are required:
- **Process Step Description:** What is done.
- **Process Parameter:** How it is controlled.
- **In-Process Control (IPC):** What is measured during the step.

#### Stage 3: Trial Runs (Pilot + Commercial)
Three types of trial runs are conducted sequentially:

| Trial Type | Scale | Objective | Number of Batches |
|-----------|-------|-----------|-------------------|
| Lab-scale | 1–5 kg | Proof of concept | 3–5 |
| Pilot-scale | 50–500 kg | Scale-up validation | 2–3 |
| Commercial-scale | Full production capacity | Confirm robustness | 3–5 (validation batches) |

Each trial records:
- **Set parameters** (input settings).
- **Actual parameters achieved** (real values).
- **CQA measurements** (output quality).
- **Deviations and corrective actions.**

#### Stage 4: Parameter Optimization & Lock-Down
The data from trial runs is analyzed using statistical tools to find the **optimum operating window** for each parameter. The output is:
- A finalized **Master Manufacturing Protocol (MMP)**.
- A **Process Capability Report** showing how well the process can meet specifications.
- A **Control Plan** listing CPPs, target values, NOR limits, alarm limits, and the person responsible.

### 2.2 Critical Process Parameters (CPPs) — Identification Logic

A parameter is classified as *Critical* if its variation causes a meaningful change in a CQA. The CQA–CPP relationship is established through:

1. **Prior Knowledge:** Scientific literature, supplier data, regulatory guidelines.
2. **Risk Assessment (FMEA):** Severity × Occurrence × Detection score.
3. **DoE Screening:** Experiments that deliberately vary parameters and observe CQA responses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Common Pitfall:** Students often list *every* process variable as "critical." In reality, only parameters with **proven statistical correlation** to a CQA qualify as CPPs. Non-critical parameters are merely *monitored variables*.

#### Common CPPs in Food Manufacturing

| Process Category | Typical CPPs | Linked CQA |
|------------------|--------------|------------|
| Mixing/Blending | Time, speed, temperature, order of addition | Uniformity, texture, color |
| Heat Processing (Pasteurization) | Temperature, holding time, flow rate | Microbial safety, enzyme activity, flavor |
| Baking | Oven temperature, time, humidity, air velocity | Texture, color, moisture, volume |
| Extrusion | Barrel temperature, screw speed, feed rate, moisture | Expansion, density, texture |
| Drying | Inlet/outlet temperature, air velocity, residence time | Moisture content, water activity, color |
| Fermentation | pH, temperature, inoculum size, time | Acidity, flavor, alcohol, microbial load |
| Packaging | Sealing temperature, time, pressure, gas mix | Shelf-life, seal integrity, MAP effect |

### 2.3 Process Optimization Methods (KTU High-Yield Theory)

Three classes of methods are taught under the KTU 2024 scheme:

#### A. One-Factor-at-a-Time (OFAT)
- Vary **one** parameter while keeping others constant.
- Simple but **inefficient** — misses interactions between factors.
- Used only for screening or as a preliminary step.

#### B. Design of Experiments (DoE) — Statistical
The most recommended approach. Two common designs:

1. **Full Factorial Design:** Tests all possible combinations of factor levels.
   - For $k$ factors at 2 levels each → $2^k$ runs.
   - Example: 3 factors, 2 levels = $2^3 = 8$ runs.

2. **Response Surface Methodology (RSM):** A second-order design (e.g., Central Composite Design, Box-Behnken) used to model curved response surfaces and find the **optimum** (maximum quality, minimum defect, etc.).

#### C. Sensory & Consumer Optimization
- **Hedonic Scaling** (9-point): Consumer acceptance.
- **Just-About-Right (JAR) Scales:** Identify ideal levels of an attribute.
- **Descriptive Analysis (DA):** Trained panel maps sensory profile.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rendering Note:** All absolute value bars in formulas use `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Brackets `\lvert \rvert` and vertical bar `|` are NOT used inside table cells.

| Formula | Meaning | Application |
|---------|---------|-------------|
| $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Process Capability Index (potential) | Measures how well the process fits within specification limits |
| $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Process Capability Index (actual, accounts for centering) | Industry standard for capability reporting |
| $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ | Acceptable process capability | Minimum threshold for most food processes |
| $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ | Excellent process capability | Target for safety-critical attributes |
| $S/N_{LTB} = -10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^2\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (Smaller-the-Better) | Minimize undesirable CQAs (e.g., defects) |
| $S/N_{HB} = -10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\dfrac{1}{y_i^2}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (Larger-the-Better) | Maximize desirable CQAs (e.g., yield, crispness) |
| $S/N_{NTB} = -10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - T)^2\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (Nominal-the-Best) | Hit a target value with minimum variation |
| $X^2 = \dfrac{(O - E)^2}{E}$ | Chi-Square Statistic (qualitative attribute analysis) | Compare observed vs. expected defect distribution |
| $t = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{n}}$ | One-Sample t-test statistic | Compare sample mean to a target value |
| $F = \dfrac{MS_{treatment}}{MS_{error}}$ | ANOVA F-statistic | Detect significant factor effects in DoE |
| $f_{opt} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i$ | Arithmetic Mean of CQA | Used to compute S/N ratios |
| $D = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\vert x_i - x_{ref}\vert$ | Total Absolute Deviation | Sum of deviations from a reference (target) |

Where the standard symbols mean:
- $USL$ = Upper Specification Limit.
- $LSL$ = Lower Specification Limit.
- $\sigma$ = Standard deviation of the process.
- $\mu$ = Process mean.
- $y_i$ = Observed CQA value for the $i$-th run.
- $n$ = Number of observations.
- $T$ = Target value of the CQA.
- $O$ = Observed frequency, $E$ = Expected frequency.
- $MS$ = Mean Square from ANOVA.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

The same methodology is used in:
- **Biscuit manufacturing** to optimize baking time-temperature for uniform color.
- **Ready-to-Eat (RTE) meals** for retort sterilization — CPPs are time and temperature of the autoclave; CQA is commercial sterility.
- **Beverage bottling** — CPP is the carbonation pressure; CQA is dissolved CO₂ and foam stability.
- **Dairy processing** — UHT parameters; CQA is shelf-life and taste.
- **Snack extrusion** — CPPs are barrel temperature and screw speed; CQA is expansion ratio and crispness.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$) Calculation

A ready-to-drink (RTD) mango juice has a specification for Brix (soluble solids content) of **14 ± 1 °Brix**. A process validation run of 25 batches produced the following statistics:
- Sample mean: $\bar{x} = 14.20$ °Brix.
- Standard deviation: $s = 0.30$ °Brix.

**Step 1 — Identify the specification limits.**
$$
USL = 14 + 1 = 15 \text{ °Brix}
$$
$$
LSL = 14 - 1 = 13 \text{ °Brix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the two one-sided capability indices.**
The first one-sided index measures how far the mean is from the upper limit:
$$
C_{pk,upper} = \dfrac{USL - \bar{x}}{3s} = \dfrac{15 - 14.20}{3 \times 0.30} = \dfrac{0.80}{0.90} = 0.889
$$

The second measures how far the mean is from the lower limit:
$$
C_{pk,lower} = \dfrac{\bar{x} - LSL}{3s} = \dfrac{14.20 - 13}{3 \times 0.30} = \dfrac{1.20}{0.90} = 1.333
$$

**Step 3 — Take the minimum to get the overall $C_{pk}$.**
$$
C_{pk} = \min(0.889, \; 1.333) = 0.889
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**

> [!WARNING]
> $C_{pk} = 0.889$ is **below 1.00**, which means the process is **NOT capable**. The mean is shifted toward the USL. The process needs to be re-centered (target $\bar{x} = 14.0$ instead of 14.20) and/or the variation ($s$) must be reduced.

**Step 5 — Calculate the equivalent $C_p$ (which ignores centering).**
$$
C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6s} = \dfrac{15 - 13}{6 \times 0.30} = \dfrac{2.0}{1.80} = 1.111
$$

> **Interpretation:** $C_p = 1.111$ shows the *spread* is acceptable, but the *centering* is the issue. This is a classic exam scenario where the examiner tests whether the student knows the difference between $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Full Factorial DoE (2³ Design)

**Scenario:** A food technologist is optimizing the *crispness* of a baked snack. Three CPPs (factors) are suspected:
- Factor A: Baking temperature (°C) — Low = 160, High = 200.
- Factor B: Baking time (min) — Low = 10, High = 20.
- Factor C: Sugar content (%) — Low = 5, High = 15.

The CQA (response) is **crispness score** on a 1–10 scale, where **higher is better** (Larger-the-Better).

All $2^3 = 8$ runs were carried out in random order. The crispness scores are:

| Run | A (Temp) | B (Time) | C (Sugar) | Crispness ($y$) |
|-----|----------|----------|-----------|-----------------|
| 1 | 160 | 10 | 5 | 4.2 |
| 2 | 200 | 10 | 5 | 5.8 |
| 3 | 160 | 20 | 5 | 3.5 |
| 4 | 200 | 20 | 5 | 7.1 |
| 5 | 160 | 10 | 15 | 5.5 |
| 6 | 200 | 10 | 15 | 6.0 |
| 7 | 160 | 20 | 15 | 4.8 |
| 8 | 200 | 20 | 15 | 8.2 |

**Step 1 — Compute the main effect of Factor A (Temperature).**

For a $2^3$ design, the main effect of A is the average response at the *high* level minus the average response at the *low* level:
$$
\bar{y}_{A^+} = \dfrac{5.8 + 7.1 + 6.0 + 8.2}{4} = \dfrac{27.1}{4} = 6.775
$$
$$
\bar{y}_{A^-} = \dfrac{4.2 + 3.5 + 5.5 + 4.8}{4} = \dfrac{18.0}{4} = 4.500
$$
$$
\text{Effect}_A = \bar{y}_{A^+} - \bar{y}_{A^-} = 6.775 - 4.500 = +2.275
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the main effect of Factor B (Time).**
$$
\bar{y}_{B^+} = \dfrac{3.5 + 7.1 + 4.8 + 8.2}{4} = \dfrac{23.6}{4} = 5.900
$$
$$
\bar{y}_{B^-} = \dfrac{4.2 + 5.8 + 5.5 + 6.0}{4} = \dfrac{21.5}{4} = 5.375
$$
$$
\text{Effect}_B = 5.900 - 5.375 = +0.525
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the main effect of Factor C (Sugar).**
$$
\bar{y}_{C^+} = \dfrac{5.5 + 6.0 + 4.8 + 8.2}{4} = \dfrac{24.5}{4} = 6.125
$$
$$
\bar{y}_{C^-} = \dfrac{4.2 + 5.8 + 3.5 + 7.1}{4} = \dfrac{20.6}{4} = 5.150
$$
$$
\text{Effect}_C = 6.125 - 5.150 = +0.975
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the two-factor interaction AB (as an example of how interactions are obtained).**

The AB interaction effect is calculated by computing the effect of A at the two levels of B and then averaging.
$$
\text{Effect}_{A \text{ at } B^+} = \dfrac{7.1 + 8.2}{2} - \dfrac{3.5 + 4.8}{2} = 7.65 - 4.15 = 3.50
$$
$$
\text{Effect}_{A \text{ at } B^-} = \dfrac{5.8 + 6.0}{2} - \dfrac{4.2 + 5.5}{2} = 5.90 - 4.85 = 1.05
$$
$$
\text{Effect}_{AB} = \dfrac{3.50 - 1.05}{2} = +1.225
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the results.**

| Factor | Effect | Meaning |
|--------|--------|---------|
| A (Temperature) | +2.275 | Strong positive — higher temperature → crispier |
| B (Time) | +0.525 | Weak positive — longer time slightly helps |
| C (Sugar) | +0.975 | Moderate positive — more sugar helps |
| AB Interaction | +1.225 | Meaningful — temperature effect depends on time |

**Step 6 — Determine the optimum using S/N ratio (Larger-the-Better).**

For each run, compute:
$$
S/N_i = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{y_i^2}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(y_i)
$$

> Note: For Larger-the-Better with a single observation per run, the S/N ratio simplifies to $20 \log_{10}(y_i)$.

For the optimum run (Run 8: 200°C, 20 min, 15% sugar with $y = 8.2$):
$$
S/N_{max} = 20 \log_{10}(8.2) = 20 \times 0.9138 = 18.28 \text{ dB}
$$

**Conclusion:** The combination **200°C, 20 min, 15% sugar** gives the highest crispness, and the **strongest single factor is temperature**. However, the AB interaction shows that going to high temperature *without* sufficient time is wasteful — both must be high to maximize crispness.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for Choosing a Parameter Optimization Method

| Situation | Recommended Method | Reason |
|-----------|--------------------|--------|
| Quick check of one parameter, limited resources | OFAT | Fast, simple, low cost |
| Multiple factors suspected, need ranking | Fractional Factorial / Plackett-Burman | Efficient screening |
| Need to model curvature and find optimum | Response Surface Methodology (RSM) | Second-order model, predicts optimum |
| Subjective response (taste, texture perception) | Sensory Design + Just-About-Right | Captures human perception |
| Process has too much noise, want robust optimum | Taguchi Robust Design | Maximizes S/N ratio |
| Confirming process capability after optimization | $C_{pk}$ Analysis + Control Charts | Verifies long-term reproducibility |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Process Parameter Optimization Workflow

The figure below illustrates the complete closed-loop workflow for establishing process parameters, from product idea to validated commercial production.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Product Concept and Formulation] --> B[Identify CQAs from Spec]
    B --> C[Brainstorm Possible CPPs]
    C --> D[Risk Assessment FMEA Scoring]
    D --> E[Screening DoE Plackett Burman]
    E --> F{Significant Factors Identified}
    F -- No --> G[Refine Factor List and Re Run]
    G --> E
    F -- Yes --> H[Optimization DoE RSM Full Factorial]
    H --> I[Build Mathematical Model]
    I --> J[Predict Optimum Settings]
    J --> K[Pilot Scale Trial Runs]
    K --> L[Measure CQAs and Compute Cpk]
    L --> M{Cpk above 1.33}
    M -- No --> N[Adjust Settings Re Run]
    N --> K
    M -- Yes --> O[Lock Master Manufacturing Protocol]
    O --> P[Commercial Validation Batches]
    P --> Q[Release for Routine Production]
    Q --> R[Ongoing Monitoring and SPC]
    R --> S{Process Drift Detected}
    S -- Yes --> T[Trigger CAPA Corrective Action]
    T --> E
    S -- No --> R
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: CQA–CPP Relationship Mapping

This block diagram maps the functional interaction between the *quality attribute layer*, the *process parameter layer*, and the *control layer* in a typical food manufacturing line.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN[Input Layer]
        IN1[Raw Materials Specification]
        IN2[Equipment Calibration Status]
        IN3[Operator Training Records]
    end

    subgraph CP[Critical Process Parameters]
        CP1[Temperature]
        CP2[Time]
        CP3[Pressure]
        CP4[pH]
        CP5[Moisture]
        CP6[Mixing Speed]
    end

    subgraph CQ[Critical Quality Attributes]
        CQ1[Texture]
        CQ2[Color]
        CQ3[Flavor]
        CQ4[Microbial Safety]
        CQ5[Shelf Life]
        CQ6[Moisture Content]
    end

    subgraph CL[Control and Feedback Layer]
        CL1[In Process Sensors]
        CL2[Statistical Process Control Charts]
        CL3[Capability Indices Cpk]
        CL4[Corrective Action System]
    end

    IN --> CP
    CP --> CQ
    CQ --> CL
    CL -- Feedback Loop --> CP
    CL1 --> CL2
    CL2 --> CL3
    CL3 --> CL4
    CL4 -- Adjust --> CP1
    CL4 -- Adjust --> CP2
```

### 4.3 Typical Manufacturing Protocol — Document Structure Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MP[Master Manufacturing Protocol] --> M1[Section 1 Product and Batch Info]
    MP --> M2[Section 2 Bill of Materials]
    MP --> M3[Section 3 Equipment and Facility]
    MP --> M4[Section 4 Step by Step Manufacturing Procedure]
    MP --> M5[Section 5 In Process Controls and Sampling]
    MP --> M6[Section 6 Packaging and Labeling]
    MP --> M7[Section 7 Storage and Shelf Life]
    MP --> M8[Section 8 Quality Release Tests]
    MP --> M9[Section 9 Deviation and CAPA Log]

    M1 --> M1a[Product Name]
    M1 --> M1b[Batch Size]
    M1 --> M1c[Reference to Formulation]

    M2 --> M2a[Ingredient List with Codes]
    M2 --> M2b[Quantity per Batch]
    M2 --> M2c[Acceptance Limits]

    M4 --> M4a[Step 1 Raw Material Prep]
    M4 --> M4b[Step 2 Mixing]
    M4 --> M4c[Step 3 Heat Processing]
    M4 --> M4d[Step 4 Forming]
    M4 --> M4e[Step 5 Cooling]
    M4 --> M4f[Step 6 Packaging]

    M5 --> M5a[Frequency of Sampling]
    M5 --> M5b[Method of Test]
    M5 --> M5c[Acceptance Criteria]
```

### 4.4 Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram — Sources of Process Variation

This diagram maps the seven classical sources of variation in food manufacturing, all of which must be controlled to establish optimum process parameters.

```mermaid
fishbone
    Problem[CQA Out of Spec] --> M1
    Problem --> M2
    Problem --> M3
    Problem --> M4
    Problem --> M5
    Problem --> M6

    M1[Man Operator Skill Training Fatigue] --> Problem
    M2[Machine Wear Calibration] --> Problem
    M3[Material Variability Lot to Lot] --> Problem
    M4[Method SOP Version Deviation] --> Problem
    M5[Measurement Instrument Error] --> Problem
    M6[Environment Temp Humidity Season] --> Problem
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define Critical Process Parameters (CPP) with one example.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Critical Process Parameter (CPP)** is a process variable whose variability has a direct and statistically significant impact on one or more Critical Quality Attributes (CQA) of the product, and therefore must be monitored and tightly controlled within a defined range to ensure product quality. **Example:** In pasteurization of milk, the *holding temperature* (e.g., 72°C) and the *holding time* (e.g., 15 s) are CPPs that directly affect the CQA "microbial safety" and "shelf life" of the milk.

*(Marks: 1 for definition, 1 for relevance, 1 for example.)*

---

#### Question 2: What is the difference between $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$?
`[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$C_p$ is the *process capability index* that measures the potential capability of a process by comparing the specification width to the process spread, **assuming the process is perfectly centered**. It is calculated as $C_p = (USL - LSL) / (6\sigma)$. In contrast, $C_{pk}$ is the *actual* capability index that accounts for the **centering** of the process mean relative to the specification limits and is calculated as the minimum of the upper and lower one-sided indices. $C_{pk} \leq C_p$ always, and a low $C_{pk}$ (with a high $C_p$) indicates the process needs to be re-centered.

*(Marks: 1 for $C_p$, 1 for $C_{pk}$, 1 for distinction.)*

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question A: Design a Manufacturing Protocol for a Ready-to-Eat (RTE) Extruded Snack

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model | 14 Marks]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Identify the Critical Quality Attributes (CQAs) and Critical Process Parameters (CPPs) for an extruded corn-based RTE snack, and explain their interrelationship.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe a 3-factor Response Surface Methodology (RSM) design to optimize the snack's *expansion ratio* and *crispness score*. Show how you would analyze the data to find the optimum settings and validate them using $C_{pk}$.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — List the CQAs (2 marks).**
For an extruded corn snack, the CQAs are:
1. **Expansion Ratio** (volume of snack ÷ volume of feed) — drives perceived crispness.
2. **Crispness Score** (sensory + instrumental hardness) — primary texture attribute.
3. **Moisture Content** — must be $< 3\%$ for shelf stability.
4. **Water Activity ($a_w$)** — must be $< 0.6$ for microbial safety.
5. **Color ($L^*, a^*, b^*$)** — uniform golden color.
6. **Bulk Density** — affects packaging and cost.

**Step 2 — List the CPPs (2 marks).**
1. **Barrel Temperature** (°C) — typical range 130–180°C.
2. **Screw Speed** (RPM) — typical range 200–500 RPM.
3. **Feed Moisture Content** (%) — typical range 12–18%.
4. **Feed Rate** (kg/h).
5. **Die Geometry** (fixed in this study).

**Step 3 — Build the CQA–CPP relationship matrix (3 marks).**

| CPP → CQA | Barrel Temp | Screw Speed | Feed Moisture | Feed Rate |
|-----------|------------|-------------|---------------|-----------|
| Expansion Ratio | ↑ (more puffing) | ↓ (less residence time reduces expansion) | ↓ (steam flashes more) | Complex |
| Crispness | ↑ (better structure) | ↑ (finer cells) | ↓ (crisper) | Moderate |
| Moisture | ↓ (dries melt) | ↑ (less time) | ↑ (wetter feed) | — |
| Color (browning) | ↑ (Maillard) | Moderate | Moderate | — |

> The matrix shows that **barrel temperature, screw speed, and feed moisture** are the most influential CPPs and should be taken as the three factors for optimization (DoE).

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Choose a Central Composite Design (CCD) (1 mark).**
For 3 factors, a Rotatable CCD requires:
- $2^3 = 8$ factorial points.
- $2 \times 3 = 6$ axial points (at $\alpha = 1.682$).
- $6$ center points (for replication and error estimation).
- **Total = 20 runs.**

The factor levels are coded from $-1.682$ to $+1.682$.

| Factor | $-\alpha$ | $-1$ | $0$ | $+1$ | $+\alpha$ |
|--------|----------|------|-----|------|----------|
| Barrel Temp (°C) | 119.1 | 140 | 160 | 180 | 200.9 |
| Screw Speed (RPM) | 131.8 | 200 | 300 | 400 | 468.2 |
| Feed Moisture (%) | 9.6 | 12 | 15 | 18 | 20.4 |

**Step 2 — Run the 20 experiments and measure both responses (1 mark).**
- $y_1$ = Expansion Ratio (Larger-the-Better).
- $y_2$ = Crispness Score (Larger-the-Better).

**Step 3 — Fit a second-order polynomial model (1.5 marks).**
$$
y = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{3}\beta_i x_i + \sum_{i=1}^{3}\beta_{ii} x_i^2 + \sum_{i<j}\beta_{ij} x_i x_j + \varepsilon
$$

**Step 4 — Use ANOVA to confirm significance (1 mark).**
Verify $p < 0.05$ for the model and lack-of-fit $p > 0.05$. Compute $R^2_{adj} \geq 0.80$.

**Step 5 — Find the numerical optimum (1.5 marks).**
Set partial derivatives to zero:
$$
\dfrac{\partial y}{\partial x_1} = \beta_1 + 2\beta_{11}x_1 + \beta_{12}x_2 + \beta_{13}x_3 = 0
$$
$$
\dfrac{\partial y}{\partial x_2} = \beta_2 + 2\beta_{22}x_2 + \beta_{12}x_1 + \beta_{23}x_3 = 0
$$
$$
\dfrac{\partial y}{\partial x_3} = \beta_3 + 2\beta_{33}x_3 + \beta_{13}x_1 + \beta_{23}x_2 = 0
$$
Solve the linear system for $x_1, x_2, x_3$. Decode the values to obtain the actual optimum (e.g., 175°C, 280 RPM, 13.5% moisture).

**Step 6 — Validate the optimum and compute $C_{pk}$ (1 mark).**
Run 5 confirmation batches at the predicted optimum. Measure expansion ratio. If the specification is expansion $\geq 4.0$:
- Compute $\bar{x}$ and $s$ from the 5 batches.
- Calculate $C_{pk} = (\bar{x} - 4.0) / (3s)$ (one-sided since only lower limit exists).
- Confirm $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ before locking the protocol.

---

#### Question B: Establish Optimum Baking Parameters for a Sponge Cake Using Taguchi's L9 Orthogonal Array

`[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024 Model | 14 Marks]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Identify three CPPs and one CQA for a sponge cake, and construct a Taguchi L9 orthogonal array. Compute the S/N ratio (Larger-the-Better) for each run.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the S/N ratios, determine the optimal factor levels, estimate the predicted optimum S/N, and recommend a confirmation test strategy.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — CQAs and CPPs (1 mark).**
- **CQA:** Specific Volume (cm³/g) — a measure of cake fluffiness.
- **CPPs (factors):**
  - A = Baking Temperature (°C) — Levels: 160, 180, 200.
  - B = Baking Time (min) — Levels: 20, 30, 40.
  - C = Egg Quantity (% of flour weight) — Levels: 80, 100, 120.

**Step 2 — Taguchi L9 layout (1.5 marks).**

| Run | A (°C) | B (min) | C (%) |
|-----|--------|---------|-------|
| 1 | 160 | 20 | 80 |
| 2 | 160 | 30 | 100 |
| 3 | 160 | 40 | 120 |
| 4 | 180 | 20 | 100 |
| 5 | 180 | 30 | 120 |
| 6 | 180 | 40 | 80 |
| 7 | 200 | 20 | 120 |
| 8 | 200 | 30 | 80 |
| 9 | 200 | 40 | 100 |

**Step 3 — Specific volume data (3 trials per run for replication, 1 mark).**

| Run | $y_1$ | $y_2$ | $y_3$ | Mean $y$ |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|----------|
| 1 | 2.8 | 2.9 | 3.0 | 2.90 |
| 2 | 3.5 | 3.6 | 3.4 | 3.50 |
| 3 | 3.1 | 3.0 | 3.2 | 3.10 |
| 4 | 3.8 | 3.9 | 3.7 | 3.80 |
| 5 | 4.2 | 4.3 | 4.1 | 4.20 |
| 6 | 3.5 | 3.6 | 3.4 | 3.50 |
| 7 | 3.2 | 3.1 | 3.3 | 3.20 |
| 8 | 3.9 | 4.0 | 3.8 | 3.90 |
| 9 | 3.0 | 2.9 | 3.1 | 3.00 |

**Step 4 — S/N ratios (Larger-the-Better) (3.5 marks).**

Formula:
$$
S/N = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\bar{y}^2}{s^2}\right)
$$
where $s$ is the standard deviation of the 3 trials.

A simpler and acceptable approximation for the KTU exam is to use the mean squared:
$$
S/N \approx 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum y_i^2\right)
$$
*(To avoid manual log computations in the exam, students are often allowed to show the procedure symbolically.)*

| Run | $\bar{y}$ | $\sum y_i^2$ | $S/N$ (dB) |
|-----|-----------|--------------|------------|
| 1 | 2.90 | 25.25 | 9.25 |
| 2 | 3.50 | 36.77 | 10.79 |
| 3 | 3.10 | 29.65 | 9.95 |
| 4 | 3.80 | 43.94 | 11.64 |
| 5 | 4.20 | 52.94 | 12.47 |
| 6 | 3.50 | 36.77 | 10.79 |
| 7 | 3.20 | 30.74 | 10.10 |
| 8 | 3.90 | 45.65 | 11.82 |
| 9 | 3.00 | 26.82 | 9.51 |

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute mean S/N at each factor level (2.5 marks).**

| Factor | Level 1 | Level 2 | Level 3 | Best Level |
|--------|---------|---------|---------|-----------|
| A (Temp) | $(9.25+10.79+9.95)/3 = 9.997$ | $(11.64+12.47+10.79)/3 = 11.633$ | $(10.10+11.82+9.51)/3 = 10.477$ | **A2 = 180°C** |
| B (Time) | $(9.25+11.64+10.10)/3 = 10.330$ | $(10.79+12.47+11.82)/3 = 11.693$ | $(9.95+10.79+9.51)/3 = 10.083$ | **B2 = 30 min** |
| C (Egg) | $(9.25+10.79+11.82)/3 = 10.620$ | $(11.64+10.79+9.51)/3 = 10.647$ | $(9.95+12.47+10.10)/3 = 10.840$ | **C3 = 120%** |

**Step 2 — Predicted optimum S/N at A2 B2 C3 (2 marks).**

Using the additive model:
$$
S/N_{pred} = \bar{S/N}_{total} + (S/N_{A2} - \bar{S/N}_{total}) + (S/N_{B2} - \bar{S/N}_{total}) + (S/N_{C3} - \bar{S/N}_{total})
$$
where $\bar{S/N}_{total} = (9.997+11.633+10.477)/3 = 10.702$ (averaged across A's levels).

Then:
$$
S/N_{pred} = S/N_{A2} + S/N_{B2} + S/N_{C3} - 2 \bar{S/N}_{total}
$$
$$
S/N_{pred} = 11.633 + 11.693 + 10.840 - 2(10.702) = 12.762 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert back to predicted mean specific volume (1 mark).**

$$
10 \log_{10}(\bar{y}^2) = 12.762 \implies \log_{10}(\bar{y}^2) = 1.2762 \implies \bar{y}^2 = 18.89 \implies \bar{y} \approx 4.35 \text{ cm}^3/\text{g}
$$

**Step 4 — Confirmation test strategy (1.5 marks).**
1. Bake 3–5 confirmation cakes at A2 B2 C3.
2. Measure specific volume.
3. Apply one-sample t-test:
   - $H_0: \mu = 4.35$ vs $H_1: \mu \neq 4.35$.
   - Compute $t = (\bar{x} - 4.35) / (s / \sqrt{n})$.
   - If $\vert t \vert \leq t_{0.05, n-1}$ (e.g., $t_{0.05, 4} = 2.776$), accept the model.
4. Calculate $C_{pk}$ against the industrial specification (e.g., specific volume $\geq 4.0$ cm³/g) and ensure $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$.
5. Lock the protocol and add to MMP.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **S/N formula choice matters.** For Larger-the-Better, the correct form is $-10 \log_{10}(1/n \cdot \sum (1/y_i^2))$. Using $-10 \log_{10}(\bar{y}^2/s^2)$ is a different formulation. Mixing the two is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks in Part B.
> 2. **Confirm prediction is additive only when interactions are negligible.** Taguchi's S/N additive prediction is approximate. KTU expects the student to *acknowledge this caveat*, not blindly apply the formula.
> 3. **State the assumption of "negligible interaction"** when using the additive prediction model.
> 4. **Always pair optimization with confirmation.** A predicted optimum without a confirmation run is incomplete — KTU deducts 1 mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **Manufacturing Protocol** is the codified, written set of instructions that translates a formulation into reproducible batches at commercial scale.
- **CQAs** are quality attributes that matter to the consumer and must be controlled (e.g., texture, color, safety, shelf-life). **CPPs** are process variables that influence CQAs (e.g., temperature, time, moisture).
- The link between CQAs and CPPs is established using **risk assessment (FMEA)** and **statistical Design of Experiments (DoE)** — not by intuition alone.
- The **three main optimization methods** are: One-Factor-at-a-Time (OFAT), Full/Fractional Factorial DoE, and Response Surface Methodology (RSM). RSM is preferred for finding the *true optimum*.
- **Taguchi's L9 / L18 orthogonal arrays** combined with **S/N ratios** are widely used in food industry for robust optimization. Three S/N types: Larger-the-Better (LTB), Smaller-the-Better (STB), and Nominal-the-Best (NTB).
- **Process capability indices:**
  - $C_p = (USL - LSL) / (6\sigma)$ — spread-only.
  - $C_{pk} = \min\!\big((USL-\mu)/(3\sigma),\; (\mu - LSL)/(3\sigma)\big)$ — spread + centering.
  - **Acceptance threshold:** $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ for general use; $\geq 1.67$ for safety-critical.
- **Trial run sequence** must be: Lab-scale $\rightarrow$ Pilot-scale $\rightarrow$ Commercial validation batches. Each level must be qualified (IQ/OQ/PQ).
- **Documentation deliverables** of process parameter optimization: Master Manufacturing Protocol, Control Plan, FMEA, Process Capability Report, and Validation Report.
- **Common exam traps to avoid:**
  - Confusing $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$.
  - Applying the wrong S/N formula (LTB vs. STB vs. NTB).
  - Forgetting to confirm a predicted optimum with a physical run.
  - Calling every process variable a "CPP" without statistical evidence.
  - Mixing up NOR (Normal Operating Range) and PAR (Proven Acceptable Range) in definitions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensory Evaluation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT835 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sensory Evaluation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sensory Evaluation** is defined as a scientific method used to evoke, measure, analyze, and interpret human responses to food products as perceived through the five senses: **sight (vision), smell (olfaction), taste (gustation), touch (tactile/somesthesis), and hearing (audition)**. The American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM) defines it as *"a scientific discipline used to evoke, measure, analyze, and interpret those responses to products as perceived through the senses of sight, smell, touch, taste, and hearing."*

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus, sensory evaluation is positioned as an **objective, reproducible, and statistically validated measurement system** that transforms subjective human perception into quantifiable data — bridging the gap between consumer acceptance and laboratory instrument readings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — OEFTT835):** Sensory evaluation is the *only* measurement tool that directly quantifies **human perception**, complementing instrumental analyses (texture analyzers, colorimeters, GC-MS for aroma). It is a mandatory component of **new product development (NPD)**, **quality assurance (QA)**, and **shelf-life studies**.

## 1.2 The Five Sensory Modalities in Food

| Sense | Receptor Type | Detected Attributes |
|---|---|---|
| **Vision** | Photoreceptors (rods/cones) | Color, size, shape, gloss, surface texture, viscosity cues |
| **Olfaction** | Olfactory epithelium (~10⁶ receptors) | Aroma, fragrance, off-odors |
| **Gustation** | Taste buds (5 basic tastes) | Sweet, sour, salty, bitter, umami |
| **Tactile/Somesthesis** | Mechanoreceptors, thermoreceptors | Texture, hardness, viscosity, temperature |
| **Audition** | Cochlear hair cells | Crispness, crunchiness, sizzle, fizz |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Critic" View

Imagine you walk into a restaurant and taste a new dessert. Your **brain instantly performs sensory evaluation** without you realizing:
- Your **eyes** judge the color, plating, and gloss.
- Your **nose** captures the aroma wafting up.
- Your **tongue** registers sweetness, creaminess, and bitterness.
- Your **ears** hear the crisp snap of caramelized sugar.
- Your **fingers** (or fork pressure) feel the firmness of the cake.

A trained sensory panel does the **same thing**, but with **controlled samples, blind-coded presentations, standardized booths, and statistical analysis** — turning your "delicious!" reaction into a precise number that can be graphed, compared, and defended in a boardroom.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Sensory evaluation does NOT replace instruments. It complements them. A colorimeter can measure reflectance, but only a human panel can confirm whether a consumer *perceives* a "natural apple-green" hue as appetizing.

## 1.4 Why Sensory Evaluation Matters in Food Product Design

1. **Consumer Acceptance Prediction** — Will the product sell?
2. **Product Matching** — Can we replicate a competitor's product?
3. **Shelf-Life Monitoring** — Does the sensory profile degrade over time?
4. **Quality Control** — Are production batches consistent?
5. **Ingredient Substitution** — How does a cheaper ingredient affect perception?
6. **Cost Optimization** — Cheaper formula without perceptible quality loss.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Sensory Tests

Sensory tests are broadly classified into three major categories based on their purpose:

### 2.1.1 Discriminative Tests (Difference Tests)
Used to determine whether a perceptible difference exists between two or more products. These are *objective* tests requiring trained or semi-trained panels.

| Test Type | Number of Samples | Procedure | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Triangle Test** | 3 | Two identical, one different — identify the odd one | Detect any sensory difference |
| **Duo-Trio Test** | 3 (1 reference + 2 coded) | Match coded sample to reference | Detect difference from a control |
| **Paired-Preference Test** | 2 | Choose preferred sample | Directional preference |
| **Two-Out-of-Five Test** | 5 | Identify group of 2 or 3 identical | When samples are very similar |
| **Ranking Test** | 3+ | Rank samples by intensity of attribute | Order samples by attribute strength |
| **A-Not-A Test** | Sequential | Is this sample "A" or "Not-A"? | Production line shifts |

### 2.1.2 Descriptive Tests
Used to identify and quantify specific sensory attributes of a product. Requires a **highly trained panel** (10–12 panelists, 100+ hours training).

- **Flavor Profile Method (FPM)** — Developed by Arthur D. Little (1949). Uses 15 cm unstructured line scales, 4–6 trained panelists.
- **Texture Profile Method (TPM)** — Developed by General Foods (1963, Brandt, Skinner & Coleman). Quantifies textural parameters.
- **Quantitative Descriptive Analysis (QDA)** — Developed by Tragon Corporation (Stone, Sidel, Oliver, Woolsey, Singleton, 1974). Uses 10–12 trained panelists, 15 cm unstructured scales.
- **Spectrum™ Method** — Developed by Gail Civille. Uses absolute reference standards (e.g., "sugar-water sweetness equivalents").
- **Free-Choice Profiling** — Each panelist generates their own descriptors (no consensus).

### 2.1.3 Affective (Consumer / Hedonic) Tests
Used to measure **liking, preference, or acceptance** of products among target consumers. Uses **untrained consumer panels** (minimum 50–100 consumers per cell).

- **Hedonic Rating** — 9-point hedonic scale (1 = dislike extremely, 9 = like extremely).
- **Preference Mapping** — Internal/External Preference Mapping.
- **Acceptance Test** — Yes/No acceptance, or "Just-About-Right (JAR)" scales.
- **Paired-Preference** — Forced choice between two products.
- **Home-Use Tests (HUT)** — Consumers test product in their natural environment.

## 2.2 The Sensory Panel — Hierarchy

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  CONSUMER PANEL (Untrained, n ≥ 50–100)              │
│  Affective/Hedonic Tests only                        │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  TRAINED PANEL (8–12 panelists, 100+ hours)          │
│  Descriptive Analysis (QDA, Spectrum, FPM)           │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  EXPERT PANEL (3–5 specialists)                      │
│  Highly specialized discrimination                   │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## 2.3 Test Environment & ISO Standards

> [!NOTE]
> Sensory booths must comply with **ISO 8589:2007** — *Sensory analysis — General guidance for the design of test rooms.*

Key environmental controls:
- **Lighting:** Color-correct, adjustable (red/green/blue filters to mask color differences).
- **Temperature:** 20 ± 2 °C.
- **Humidity:** 50–55 % RH.
- **Noise:** < 45 dB; isolated booths.
- **Odor control:** Activated carbon filtration, positive air pressure.
- **Sample temperature:** Controlled ± 1 °C (water baths).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statistical Tools for Sensory Evaluation**

| Test / Parameter | Formula | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Triangle Test Critical Value** | $n_c = $ table value (one-tailed $\alpha = 0.05$, $P = 1/3$) | Minimum correct judgments for significance |
| **Chi-Square (Paired Preference)** | $\chi^2 = \dfrac{(\vert O_A - O_B \vert - 1)^2}{O_A + O_B}$ | Yates-corrected $\chi^2$, df = 1 |
| **Hedonic Mean Score** | $\bar{H} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} H_i}{n}$ | Average liking (scale 1–9) |
| **Coefficient of Variation (CV)** | $CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\bar{x}} \times 100\,\%$ | Panel reproducibility check |
| **One-Way ANOVA (F-statistic)** | $F = \dfrac{MS_{between}}{MS_{within}} = \dfrac{SS_{between}/(k-1)}{SS_{within}/(n-k)}$ | Test difference among samples |
| **Tukey's HSD** | $HSD = q_{\alpha,k,df} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{MS_{within}}{n}}$ | Post-hoc pairwise comparison |
| **Panel Performance (p-value)** | $p_i = \dfrac{\text{correct}_i - \text{wrong}_i}{N}$ | Individual panelist accuracy |
| **Standard Error of Mean (SEM)** | $SEM = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Confidence interval of $\bar{H}$ |
| **Pearson Correlation (r)** | $r = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$ | Instrument ↔ Sensory correlation |
| **Just-About-Right Penalty Analysis** | $\%\,\text{penalty} = \sum \vert f_{JAR} - f_{adjusted}\vert$ | Identify ideal attribute level |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical Pipe Substitution:** All $\vert\, \cdot \,\vert$ symbols in formulas use LaTeX `\vert` notation to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Students writing in Word/LaTeX should use the standard `|x|` directly.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **FMCG Industry:** PepsiCo, Nestlé, Mondelez — use QDA panels of 12 panelists for every product reformulation.
- **Brewing:** Trained panels for hop aroma profiling in IPAs.
- **Dairy:** Texture profiling of yogurt (firmness, smoothness, mouthfeel).
- **Sensory-Instrument Correlation:** Driving **electronic noses (e-noses)** and **electronic tongues (e-tongues)** calibration.
- **Regulatory:** Codex Alimentarius, FSSAI, and FDA accept sensory data for shelf-life claims.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Triangle Test Minimum Correct Responses

The triangle test presents 3 coded samples to a panelist: **2 identical + 1 different** (random arrangement). The probability of guessing correctly by chance is $P = 1/3$ (binomial with three equally likely positions for the odd sample).

**Step 1 — Null Hypothesis Setup**

$$H_0: p = \dfrac{1}{3} \quad \text{(no difference, panelist is guessing)}$$

$$H_1: p > \dfrac{1}{3} \quad \text{(a real perceptible difference exists)}$$

**Step 2 — Binomial Probability Distribution**

The number of correct responses $X$ out of $n$ panelist-trials follows:

$$P(X = k) = \binom{n}{k} \left(\dfrac{1}{3}\right)^k \left(\dfrac{2}{3}\right)^{n-k}$$

**Step 3 — Critical Number of Correct Responses**

For a one-tailed test at $\alpha = 0.05$, the critical value $n_c$ is the smallest $k$ such that:

$$P(X \geq k) \leq 0.05 \quad \text{under } H_0$$

**Step 4 — Worked Example (n = 30 panelist-trials)**

We seek the smallest $k$ such that:

$$\sum_{i=k}^{30} \binom{30}{i} \left(\dfrac{1}{3}\right)^i \left(\dfrac{2}{3}\right)^{30-i} \leq 0.05$$

Iterating:
- For $k = 15$: cumulative probability $\approx 0.0499$ ← **just below 0.05**
- For $k = 14$: cumulative probability $\approx 0.1133$ ← above 0.05

Therefore, the critical value is $n_c = 15$. If $\geq 15$ out of 30 panelists correctly identify the odd sample, we reject $H_0$ and conclude that a significant sensory difference exists between the two products.

**Result Table (Triangle Test, one-tailed $\alpha = 0.05$, $P = 1/3$):**

| Panelists (n) | Minimum Correct ($n_c$) |
|---|---|
| 6 | 4 |
| 12 | 7 |
| 18 | 9 |
| 24 | 12 |
| 30 | 15 |
| 36 | 17 |
| 48 | 22 |
| 60 | 27 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Justification:** This derivation is *engineering-relevant* because it provides the statistical confidence level a sensory scientist needs before signing off on a product reformulation. A reformulation that fails this test is statistically indistinguishable from the original — and can be marketed as "no change in taste."

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hedonic Score Mean & Standard Error

**Problem:** A consumer test of a new mango drink is conducted on 60 consumers using the 9-point hedonic scale. The collected scores are: sum = 432, sum of squares = 3,360. Compute the mean, variance, SEM, and 95 % confidence interval.

**Step 1 — Sample Mean**

$$\bar{H} = \dfrac{\sum H_i}{n} = \dfrac{432}{60} = 7.20$$

**Step 2 — Sample Variance**

$$s^2 = \dfrac{\sum H_i^2 - \dfrac{(\sum H_i)^2}{n}}{n-1}$$

Substituting:

$$s^2 = \dfrac{3360 - \dfrac{432^2}{60}}{60 - 1}$$

$$s^2 = \dfrac{3360 - \dfrac{186624}{60}}{59} = \dfrac{3360 - 3110.4}{59} = \dfrac{249.6}{59} = 4.2305$$

**Step 3 — Standard Deviation**

$$s = \sqrt{4.2305} = 2.057$$

**Step 4 — Standard Error of the Mean**

$$SEM = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}} = \dfrac{2.057}{\sqrt{60}} = \dfrac{2.057}{7.746} = 0.2656$$

**Step 5 — 95 % Confidence Interval**

Using $t_{0.025,\,59} \approx 2.001$:

$$CI_{95\%} = \bar{H} \pm t \cdot SEM = 7.20 \pm (2.001)(0.2656)$$

$$CI_{95\%} = 7.20 \pm 0.5314$$

$$\boxed{CI_{95\%} = (6.67,\ 7.73)}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation**

Since the 95 % CI lies entirely within the "Like Slightly" (6) to "Like Moderately" (7) range, the product is **acceptable to the consumer population with high confidence**. The 60-consumer minimum is the **ASTM and ISO 11136** recommended sample size for hedonic tests.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Chi-Square for Paired Preference Test

**Problem:** 80 consumers are given two juice samples (A and B) blind-coded. 48 prefer A, 32 prefer B. Test for significant preference at $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Step 1 — Expected Frequencies (Under H₀: No Preference)**

$$E_A = E_B = \dfrac{80}{2} = 40$$

**Step 2 — Yates-Corrected Chi-Square**

$$\chi^2_{Yates} = \dfrac{(\vert O_A - O_B \vert - 1)^2}{O_A + O_B}$$

$$\chi^2_{Yates} = \dfrac{(\vert 48 - 32 \vert - 1)^2}{48 + 32} = \dfrac{(16 - 1)^2}{80} = \dfrac{225}{80} = 2.8125$$

**Step 3 — Decision**

Critical value: $\chi^2_{0.05,\,df=1} = 3.841$

Since $2.8125 < 3.841$, **fail to reject $H_0$**. Although more consumers preferred A, the difference is **not statistically significant** at the 5 % level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marketing Implication:** A company cannot honestly claim "consumers prefer Brand A" based on this 48 vs 32 split alone. They would need to increase sample size to ~150 to detect a 60:40 preference with 80 % power.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Test

| Research Question | Recommended Test | Panel Type | Sample Size |
|---|---|---|---|
| Are two products different? | Triangle / Duo-Trio | Trained | 25–40 |
| Which product is sweeter? | Ranking | Semi-trained | 8–15 |
| How intense is the bitterness? | QDA / Spectrum | Trained | 10–12 |
| Do consumers like the product? | 9-point Hedonic | Consumer | 50–100 |
| What is the optimum sugar level? | JAR + Penalty | Consumer | 75–150 |
| Does flavor change over storage? | Time-Intensity / TDS | Trained | 10–12 |
| Is production batch consistent? | Triangle vs reference | Trained | 20–30 |
| Which variant will sell? | HUT + CLT | Consumer | 100–300 |
