<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define FSSAI and explain its role in regulating the food industry in India.*

**Model Answer:**
FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India) is the apex statutory body established under the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006, operating under the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, Government of India.

**Role of FSSAI:**
1. **Setting food standards** — Framing regulations for food products, additives, contaminants, and labeling.
2. **Licensing and registration** of Food Business Operators (FBOs) across India.
3. **Enforcement** through state food safety commissioners and designated officers.
4. **Consumer awareness** — Educating consumers on food safety and nutrition.
5. **Scientific advisory** — Risk assessment and research through scientific panels.
6. **Coordination** with international bodies like Codex Alimentarius, WHO, and FAO.
7. **Recall of unsafe food** and imposing penalties for non-compliance.

---

**Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*List any six mandatory labeling requirements as per FSSAI regulations.*

**Model Answer (any six):**
1. **Name of the food** — Clear and accurate product name.
2. **List of ingredients** — In descending order of composition.
3. **Nutritional information** — Energy, protein, carbohydrate, fat, sugar, sodium per 100g/100mL.
4. **FSSAI logo and license number** — 14-digit number with logo.
5. **Net quantity** — In metric units (g, kg, mL, L).
6. **Date of manufacture and Best Before/Use By date**.
7. **Manufacturer/packer/importer name and complete address**.
8. **Vegetarian/Non-vegetarian symbol** — Green dot for veg, Brown dot for non-veg.
9. **Batch/lot/code number** for traceability.
10. **Allergen declaration** if applicable.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006 in detail. Describe its salient features and how it replaced multiple earlier food laws in India. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of Codex Alimentarius Commission in international food standardization. List any four major Codex standards relevant to food product development. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006:**

The FSS Act, 2006 is the **umbrella legislation** for food safety in India. It consolidated **8 different Acts and Orders** that previously governed the food sector:

| Earlier Act/Order | Year | Subject |
|---|---|---|
| Prevention of Food Adulteration Act | 1954 | Adulteration prevention |
| Fruit Products Order (FPO) | 1955 | Processed fruits |
| Meat Food Products Order (MFPO) | 1973 | Meat products |
| Vegetable Oil Products Order (VOPO) | 1998 | Vegetable oils |
| Edible Oils Packaging Order | 1998 | Oil packaging |
| Solvent Extracted Oil Order | 1967 | Solvent extraction |
| Milk and Milk Products Order | 1992 | Dairy regulation |
| Essential Commodities Act (relevant provisions) | 1955 | Supply control |

**Salient Features of FSS Act, 2006:**
1. **Single regulatory authority** — FSSAI replaces multiple regulators.
2. **Risk-based approach** — Emphasis on scientific risk assessment.
3. **Three-tier licensing** — Basic, State, and Central.
4. **Mandatory food recall** provisions.
5. **Special provisions** for proprietary and novel foods.
6. **Penalties** — Imprisonment up to 6 months (general) and life imprisonment (for adulteration causing death).
7. **Compensation to victims** under Section 39.
8. **Establishment of Food Safety Appellate Tribunal**.

**(b) Role of Codex Alimentarius Commission:**

The **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)** is a joint body of **FAO (Food and Agriculture Organization)** and **WHO (World Health Organization)**, established in **1963**. It develops international food standards, guidelines, and codes of practice to protect consumer health and ensure fair food trade.

**Functions of Codex:**
1. **International harmonization** of food standards.
2. **Reference point** for WTO in resolving SPS and TBT disputes.
3. **Scientific basis** for food safety standards.
4. **Capacity building** for developing countries.

**Four Major Codex Standards:**
1. **Codex General Standard for Food Additives (GSFA, Codex Stan 192-1995)**.
2. **Codex General Standard for the Labelling of Pre-packaged Foods (Codex Stan 1-1985)**.
3. **Codex Alimentarius Code of Hygienic Practice for Refrigerated Packaged Foods with Extended Shelf Life (CAC/RCP 46-1999)**.
4. **Codex Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (Codex Stan 193-1995)**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the seven principles of HACCP. How does HACCP differ from traditional quality control methods? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the types of FSSAI licenses with turnover criteria. Outline the procedure for obtaining an FSSAI license for a medium-scale food processing unit. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Seven Principles of HACCP:**

**HACCP (Hazard Analysis Critical Control Point)** is a systematic, science-based preventive approach to food safety.

**Principle 1: Hazard Analysis**
- Identify potential **biological** (Salmonella, E. coli), **chemical** (pesticides, toxins), and **physical** (glass, metal) hazards.
- Assess severity and likelihood of occurrence.

**Principle 2: Determine Critical Control Points (CCPs)**
- Identify points where hazards can be controlled or eliminated.
- Use the **CCP Decision Tree** (Q1: Do preventive control measures exist? Q2: Does this step eliminate or reduce the hazard? Q3: Could contamination occur in excess of acceptable levels? Q4: Will a subsequent step eliminate the hazard?).

**Principle 3: Establish Critical Limits**
- Define maximum/minimum values (e.g., cooking temperature ≥ **75°C**, pH ≤ **4.5** for acidified foods).

**Principle 4: Monitoring Procedures**
- Establish observation and measurement procedures (e.g., continuous temperature recording).

**Principle 5: Corrective Actions**
- Define actions when critical limits are exceeded (e.g., product isolation, rework, disposal).

**Principle 6: Verification Procedures**
- Confirm HACCP system effectiveness through audits, microbiological testing, calibration.

**Principle 7: Record-Keeping and Documentation**
- Maintain records for at least **1 year** or product shelf-life + 6 months.

**Difference between HACCP and Traditional Quality Control:**

| Aspect | Traditional QC | HACCP |
|---|---|---|
| Approach | Reactive (end-product testing) | Preventive (process control) |
| Focus | Final product inspection | Entire process from raw to finished |
| Hazard control | Post-production detection | Pre-production prevention |
| Cost | High (waste from rejections) | Lower (reduces rework) |
| Coverage | Limited to detectable defects | Comprehensive (biological, chemical, physical) |

**(b) Types of FSSAI Licenses:**

| License Type | Annual Turnover | Issuing Authority |
|---|---|---|
| Basic Registration | Up to ₹12 lakh | State Food Safety Authority |
| State License | ₹12 lakh to ₹20 crore | Designated Officer, State |
| Central License | Above ₹20 crore; specific categories (dairy, meat, export) | FSSAI Headquarters |

**Procedure for FSSAI License for Medium-Scale Unit:**

**Step 1:** Determine the category (State License for turnover between ₹12 lakh and ₹20 crore).

**Step 2:** Prepare **Form B** and supporting documents:
- Identity and address proof.
- Layout plan of premises.
- Equipment list.
- Water analysis report.
- List of food products to be manufactured.

**Step 3:** Register on the **FoSCoS portal** (https://foscos.fssai.gov.in).

**Step 4:** Pay the prescribed fee.

**Step 5:** Designated Officer conducts **physical inspection** for compliance with Schedule 4 (GMP/GHP).

**Step 6:** License issued with **14-digit number** upon compliance.

**Step 7:** Display the FSSAI logo and number prominently on all product labels.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing FSSAI with FDA** — FSSAI is for India; FDA is for the USA. Examiners deduct marks for interchange.
> 2. **Missing the 14-digit FSSAI number format** — Always mention it specifically.
> 3. **Skipping turnover thresholds** — Questions on licensing type MUST include turnover criteria.
> 4. **Writing fewer than 7 HACCP principles** — All seven must be listed in correct order.
> 5. **Confusing ADI with MRL** — ADI is for additives (mg/kg bw/day); MRL is for pesticide residues (mg/kg food).
> 6. **Not mentioning Codex Alimentarius** — For international questions, Codex reference is mandatory.
> 7. **Ignoring the Green/Brown dot symbols** for veg/non-veg foods — A very common labeling omission.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Regulatory Aspects**

- ⭐ **FSSAI** is the apex food regulator in India under the **FSS Act, 2006**.
- ⭐ **FSS Act, 2006** replaced **8 earlier Acts and Orders**.
- ⭐ **Three-tier licensing**: Basic Registration (≤ ₹12 lakh), State License (₹12L – ₹20 Cr), Central License (> ₹20 Cr).
- ⭐ **FSSAI license number** is a **14-digit unique identifier**.
- ⭐ **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)** is a joint **FAO/WHO** body established in **1963**.
- ⭐ **HACCP has 7 Principles** — memorize in order: Hazard Analysis → CCPs → Critical Limits → Monitoring → Corrective Actions → Verification → Records.
- ⭐ **ADI = NOAEL / Safety Factor (typically 100)** — measured in mg/kg body weight/day.
- ⭐ **MRL** (Maximum Residue Limit) for pesticides is in **mg/kg of food**.
- ⭐ **Mandatory Labeling**: Name, ingredients, nutritional info, FSSAI logo+number, net quantity, veg/non-veg symbol (Green/Brown dot), Best Before date, manufacturer address, batch number, allergens.
- ⭐ **BIS** sets quality standards; **AGMARK** grades agricultural commodities; **APEDA** promotes exports.
- ⭐ **Schedule 4 of FSSAI** covers GMP and GHP requirements for food premises.
- ⭐ **Food Safety Appellate Tribunal** hears appeals against FSSAI orders.
- ⭐ **ISO 22000** integrates HACCP with ISO management system requirements.
- ⭐ **FoSTaC (Food Safety Training and Certification)** is mandatory for food safety supervisors.
- ⭐ **Novel foods and GM foods** require prior product approval from FSSAI before commercialization.
- ⭐ **Class I, II, III recalls** — Class I is for dangerous/serious hazards (immediate).
- ⭐ **WTO agreements**: SPS (Sanitary and Phytosanitary) and TBT (Technical Barriers to Trade) reference Codex standards.
- ⭐ **Legal Metrology Act, 2009** governs declaration of weights and measures on packaged goods.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Approval for Proprietary Product
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT835 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Approval for Proprietary Product

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: APPROVAL FOR PROPRIETARY PRODUCT

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proprietary Food (as per FSSAI, India):**
> A proprietary food is an article of food that has not been standardized under any of the regulations made under the Food Safety and Standards Act, **2006**, and is manufactured and sold under a unique brand/trade name. Such products require **prior product approval** from the **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)** before being introduced into the market.

According to the **Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011** (with subsequent amendments, notably the **Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Third Amendment Regulations, 2020**), proprietary foods are clearly categorized as a distinct regulatory class — bridging the gap between standardized foods and novel/traditional foods.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> Under Module 4 of *OEFTT835 – Food Product Design and Development*, students are expected to understand the Indian regulatory pathway for introducing **non-standardized food products** into the market. The proprietary food approval route is the most commonly used pathway for startups and innovators launching novel formulations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant vs. Franchise" Metaphor

Imagine you want to open a food business:

- **Standardized Product (e.g., packaged milk, biscuits)** = Opening a **franchise of a well-known brand** (McDonald's). The recipe, the ingredients, the cooking temperature, the portion size — **everything is already prescribed** in a strict manual (the food standard). You just follow it.
- **Proprietary Product** = Opening your **own unique restaurant** with a creative new menu item (say, "Dragon Fruit & Basil Kombucha"). Since no standard exists in the rulebook for this product, you must get **special permission** from the food safety regulator (FSSAI) by submitting your recipe, lab reports, and safety data — analogous to a **novelty approval**.
- **Novel Food** = Introducing something **never eaten before** in India (e.g., insect protein bars). This requires an even more rigorous **novel food assessment**.

So in simple terms:
> **Proprietary food approval = The formal "permission slip" you obtain from FSSAI to legally sell a new, non-standardized food product under your own brand.**

### 1.3 Scope and Applicability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A product qualifies as a "Proprietary Food" when ALL the following conditions are true:**
> 1. The product has **no existing standard** under FSSAI regulations.
> 2. It is sold under a **specific brand/trade name** by a single manufacturer/licensee.
> 3. It does **NOT** claim any therapeutic/health benefits.
> 4. Its ingredients, additives, and process are disclosed to FSSAI.
> 5. It meets general **hygiene and safety requirements** of the FSS Act.

### 1.4 Examples of Proprietary Foods (Industry-Standard List)

| Category | Example Products |
|---|---|
| Functional Beverages | Herbal energy drinks, kombucha, nootropic lattes |
| Snack Innovations | Quinoa puffs, plant-protein chips |
| Dairy Alternatives | Oat-yogurt with live cultures, almond-based paneer |
| Fortified Foods | Immunity-boosting honey with vitamins |
| Gourmet Sauces | Truffle-infused mayonnaise, exotic chili pastes |
| Meal Replacements | Keto smoothie mixes, plant-based protein powders |

### 1.5 Regulatory Authority

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Statutory Bodies Involved:**
> - **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India):** Apex regulator — granting final product approval.
> - **State Food Safety Authorities:** Issue the **manufacturing license** post product approval.
> - **Referral Labs (e.g., FSSAI notified labs):** Conduct product safety testing.

### 1.6 Distinction from Other Regulatory Categories

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT confuse Proprietary Food with Novel Food or Health Supplements.** The approval pathways, documentation, and timelines are distinct.

| Aspect | Proprietary Food | Novel Food | Health Supplement |
|---|---|---|---|
| Regulatory Reference | FSS (Food Products Standards & Food Additives) Regulations, 2011 (3rd Amendment, 2020) | FSS (Novel Food) Regulations, 2016 (introduced 2022) | FSS (Health Supplements, Nutraceuticals, etc.) Regulations, 2022 |
| Prior History | No standard but ingredients known | No prior history of safe use in India | Specific health/functional claims |
| Approval Body | FSSAI Product Approval Cell | FSSAI Novel Food Committee | FSSAI |
| Typical Timeline | 30–60 working days | 6–12 months | 60–90 working days |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Statutory Foundation — FSSAI's Legal Basis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Backing:**
> The proprietary food approval system derives its legal authority from:
> - **Section 22 of the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006** — empowers FSSAI to regulate manufacture, storage, distribution, sale, and import of food articles not covered by standards.
> - **Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011** — provides the technical framework.
> - **Operational Guidelines issued by FSSAI** — prescribe the procedural workflow via the **FoSCoS (Food Safety Compliance System)** online portal.

## 2.2 Eligibility Criteria — Who Can Apply?

> [!NOTE]
> **Eligibility Conditions for Proprietary Food Approval:**
> 1. The applicant must hold (or apply simultaneously for) an **FSSAI license/registration** under Section 31 of the FSS Act.
> 2. The product must contain only **permitted ingredients, additives, and processing aids** as per existing FSSAI regulations.
> 3. The product must **NOT** contain ingredients listed as **prohibited** under Schedule I or exceeding **maximum limits** under the FSSAI additive regulations.
> 4. The manufacturer must ensure compliance with general hygienic practices — **Schedule 4** of the FSS Act (Good Manufacturing Practices).
> 5. The applicant must nominate a **Food Safety Officer (FSO) / Quality Manager** for the manufacturing unit.

## 2.3 Pre-Approval Checklist — The "PACT" Framework

| Step | Action | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **P** — Product Characterization | Define complete composition, ingredients, additives, nutritional profile, and intended use. | Establishes scope of approval. |
| **A** — Additives Verification | Cross-check every additive against Schedule VII (permitted list with MRLs). | Avoids automatic rejection. |
| **C** — Compliance Mapping | Align product with Schedule 4 (GMP) and labeling requirements. | Reduces query iterations. |
| **T** — Testing Plan | Identify FSSAI-notified NABL-accredited labs for shelf-life, microbiological, and chemical testing. | Ensures valid analytical data. |

## 2.4 Documents Required for Proprietary Food Approval

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Examiners frequently ask for the **list of documents** required for proprietary food approval. Memorize this list — it is a high-yield question.

The following documents (typically uploaded on FoSCoS) are mandatory:

1. **Form A** — Application form (online) with complete product details.
2. **Recipe/Formulation Sheet** — Quantitative composition (ingredients and additives in percentage).
3. **Process Flow Chart** — Step-by-step manufacturing process.
4. **Product Label** (mock-up) — Including all mandatory labeling as per FSSAI.
5. **NABL-Accredited Lab Test Reports** — Microbiological, chemical, shelf-life, and nutritional analysis.
6. **Certificate of Analysis (COA)** of each major ingredient.
7. **FSSAI License/Registration Copy** of the manufacturing facility.
8. **Trademark/Brand Registration Certificate** (or application receipt) — for the proprietary name.
9. **Stability Study Data** — covering the proposed shelf life under prescribed conditions.
10. **Declaration of Non-Prohibited Ingredients** — signed by the Food Safety Manager.
11. **MSDS (Material Safety Data Sheets)** — for additives and processing aids.

## 2.5 The Approval Workflow — 7 Logical Steps

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Process:**
> Proprietary food approval follows a structured, online workflow under the **FoSCoS** portal.

**Step 1: Pre-Consultation (Optional but Recommended)**
- Engage with FSSAI through the **pre-meeting consultation service** to gauge the regulatory fit of your product.

**Step 2: Online Application Submission**
- Register on **https://foscos.fssai.gov.in** → Login → Apply for **Product Approval (Proprietary Food)**.

**Step 3: Document Upload & Payment**
- Upload all documents listed in Section 2.4.
- Pay the prescribed **application fee** (revised periodically by FSSAI).

**Step 4: Preliminary Scrutiny by FSSAI**
- FSSAI Product Approval Cell verifies completeness. Deficiencies trigger a **"Query Letter"** with a stipulated response window.

**Step 5: Scientific Evaluation**
- Detailed review by FSSAI scientists for safety, additive usage, shelf-life adequacy, and labeling compliance.

**Step 6: Grant or Rejection**
- **Approval Certificate** issued (valid typically for **3 years**, renewable).
- Rejection orders are issued with reasons; appeal provision under Section 31 (6) of the FSS Act.

**Step 7: Manufacturing License (Post-Approval)**
- Apply to **State Food Safety Authority** for the manufacturing license using the approval certificate.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Critical Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table summarizes all quantitative thresholds and key constraints students must memorize for KTU examinations.**

| Parameter | Standard Value / Reference | Regulatory Source |
|---|---|---|
| Application fee (Proprietary Food) | **INR 7,500** per product (subject to revision) | FSSAI Order |
| Approval validity | **3 years** from date of issue | FSSAI Operational Guidelines |
| Renewal timeline | Within **180 days** before expiry | FSSAI Order |
| Maximum shelf life permitted (without special study) | **9 months** at ambient conditions | Proprietary Food Guidance |
| Additives limit | Must not exceed **GMP or prescribed MRL** | Schedule VII |
| Permitted health/nutritional claims | **NOT allowed** on proprietary food | FSSAI labeling regulations |
| Microbiological standards | Must comply with **Schedule 4 (Microbiological Requirements)** | FSS Act, 2006 |
| Labeling font height (principal display panel) | **≥ 1.5 mm** for packs ≤ 100 cm²; **≥ 3 mm** for larger packs | FSS (Labelling & Display) Regulations, 2020 |

### 2.7 Shelf Life Determination — Conceptual Formula

For accelerated shelf-life testing, FSSAI accepts the **Arrhenius-based temperature extrapolation**:

$$ Q_{10} = \frac{k_{T+10}}{k_T} $$

where:
- $Q_{10}$ is the temperature coefficient (typically **assumed ≈ 2.0** for food products).
- $k_T$ is the rate constant of degradation at temperature $T$.
- $k_{T+10}$ is the rate constant at $T + 10°C$.

The accelerated shelf life $ASL$ is related to the real-time shelf life $RTL$ by:

$$ \log(RTL) = \log(ASL) + \left(\frac{T_E - T_A}{10}\right) \cdot \log(Q_{10}) $$

where:
- $T_E$ = Elevated test temperature (°C).
- $T_A$ = Ambient storage temperature (°C).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This relationship is foundational to **predictive food science** and is used by R&D teams in the food industry to validate shelf-life claims without conducting full real-time studies (which can take 1–2 years for long-shelf-life products).

### 2.8 Post-Approval Obligations

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often forget the post-approval obligations. Approval is **not a one-time event** — it triggers ongoing compliance duties.

1. **Shelf-life conformance:** Every manufactured batch must conform to the approved shelf-life study.
2. **Label conformity:** No deviation from the approved label without fresh approval.
3. **Annual product audit:** Quality manager must maintain records for **minimum 1 year** post-shelf-life expiry.
4. **Adverse event reporting:** Any consumer complaint or food safety incident must be reported to the Food Safety Commissioner within **24 hours**.
5. **Traceability:** Compliance with Section 31(3) — recall and traceability systems.

### 2.9 Real-World Utility

In the food industry, the proprietary food route is the **fastest commercial pathway** for innovation-led startups. For instance, a Bengaluru-based startup launching a **moringa-laced instant dosa mix** would use this approval route because:
- No standard exists for "moringa-fortified instant mix."
- The product is novel but ingredients are safe and known.
- No therapeutic claims (e.g., no claim of "cures diabetes").
- Once approved, the brand enjoys a **competitive moat** — competitors cannot use the same proprietary name.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Shelf-Life Estimation Using $Q_{10}$ Approach

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a **favourite KTU Part B (14-mark) question** as it combines conceptual regulatory understanding with engineering mathematics.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A proprietary spice blend was subjected to accelerated shelf-life testing at **40°C**, and the critical quality parameter (peroxide value) reached its rejection threshold in **45 days**. Using the Arrhenius $Q_{10}$ model with $Q_{10} = 2.0$, calculate the **expected shelf life at ambient storage temperature of 25°C**.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify given quantities**

$$ T_E = 40^\circ C, \quad T_A = 25^\circ C, \quad ASL = 45 \text{ days}, \quad Q_{10} = 2.0 $$

**Step 2: Recall the fundamental Arrhenius relationship**

The general Arrhenius equation governing reaction rate with temperature is:

$$ k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT} $$

where:
- $k$ = rate constant of the degradation reaction.
- $A$ = pre-exponential factor (assumed constant).
- $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol).
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**.
- $T$ = absolute temperature in Kelvin.

**Step 3: Derive the $Q_{10}$ ratio**

Taking the ratio of rate constants at two temperatures $T + 10$ and $T$:

$$ Q_{10} = \frac{k_{T+10}}{k_T} = \frac{A \cdot e^{-E_a / R(T+10)}}{A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}} $$

The pre-exponential factor $A$ cancels out:

$$ Q_{10} = e^{\frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T+10}\right)} $$

Since $\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T+10}\right) = \dfrac{10}{T(T+10)}$, we obtain:

$$ \ln(Q_{10}) = \frac{E_a \cdot 10}{R \cdot T(T+10)} $$

**Step 4: Establish proportionality between shelf life and rate constant**

Since shelf life $\tau \propto \dfrac{1}{k}$ (lower degradation rate = longer shelf life), we have:

$$ \frac{\tau_A}{\tau_E} = \frac{k_E}{k_A} $$

**Step 5: Generalize to temperature differences not equal to 10°C**

For a temperature difference $\Delta T = T_E - T_A$:

$$ \frac{\tau_A}{\tau_E} = (Q_{10})^{\Delta T / 10} $$

Taking logarithm on both sides:

$$ \log(\tau_A) - \log(\tau_E) = \left(\frac{\Delta T}{10}\right) \cdot \log(Q_{10}) $$

Rearranging:

$$ \log(\tau_A) = \log(\tau_E) + \left(\frac{T_E - T_A}{10}\right) \cdot \log(Q_{10}) $$

This is the **master equation** used in proprietary food shelf-life estimation.

**Step 6: Substitute numerical values**

$$ \log(\tau_A) = \log(45) + \left(\frac{40 - 25}{10}\right) \cdot \log(2.0) $$

Evaluate each component:

$$ \log(45) = 1.6532 $$

$$ \left(\frac{15}{10}\right) \cdot \log(2.0) = 1.5 \cdot 0.3010 = 0.4515 $$

Therefore:

$$ \log(\tau_A) = 1.6532 + 0.4515 = 2.1047 $$

$$ \tau_A = 10^{2.1047} \approx 127.4 \text{ days} $$

**Step 7: Convert to months**

$$ \tau_A = \frac{127.4}{30} \approx 4.25 \text{ months} $$

### 3.1.3 Final Result

> **The proprietary spice blend has an estimated real-time shelf life of approximately 127 days (≈ 4.25 months) at 25°C.** Since this is well below the **9-month threshold** (see Section 2.6), the applicant should consider antioxidant addition or improved packaging to extend shelf life if a longer claim is desired.

### 3.1.4 Analytical Insight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** For every 10°C reduction in storage temperature, the shelf life approximately doubles (when $Q_{10} = 2.0$). This is the basis of **cold-chain logistics** in the food industry.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Proprietary vs. Standardized vs. Novel Food Decision Framework

A food product developer typically uses the following decision tree before choosing the proprietary food route:

| Step | Decision Question | If YES | If NO |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Is there an existing FSSAI standard matching your product? | Apply for **FSSAI License only** (no product approval needed). | Proceed to Step 2. |
| 2 | Is your product substantially equivalent to a known/safe food? | Apply via **Proprietary Food route**. | Proceed to Step 3. |
| 3 | Has the product/novel ingredient been consumed safely in India before 2007? | Apply via **Proprietary Food route** with safety evidence. | Apply via **Novel Food** route. |
| 4 | Does your product claim health benefits or disease prevention? | Apply under **Health Supplements/Nutraceuticals** regulation. | Stay on Proprietary Food route. |

## 3.3 Compliance Cost Breakdown — Indicative Financial Model

For a startup, the indicative cost (excluding lab fees) of a proprietary food approval in India is:

| Cost Component | Approximate Cost (INR) |
|---|---|
| Application Fee | 7,500 |
| NABL Lab Testing (microbiological + chemical) | 25,000 – 60,000 |
| Stability Study (3 months) | 50,000 – 1,00,000 |
| FSSAI Consultant / Legal | 15,000 – 30,000 |
| Total | **~ 1,00,000 – 2,00,000** |

This model is useful for **project planning** in food product design and development coursework.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Proprietary Food Approval Workflow

The following block diagram illustrates the end-to-end proprietary food approval pathway under FSSAI's FoSCoS portal.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Idea Conceptualization] --> B[Product Formulation Development]
    B --> C{Is FSSAI Standard Available?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Apply for FSSAI License Only]
    C -- No --> E[Pre Consultation with FSSAI Optional]
    E --> F[FoSCoS Portal Registration]
    F --> G[Prepare Documentation Set]
    G --> H[Upload Application and Documents]
    H --> I[Pay Application Fee INR 7500]
    I --> J[FSSAI Preliminary Scrutiny]
    J -- Deficiencies Found --> K[Query Letter to Applicant]
    K --> L[Applicant Response within Stipulated Window]
    L --> J
    J -- Complete --> M[Scientific Evaluation by FSSAI Panel]
    M --> N{Approval Decision}
    N -- Approved --> O[Approval Certificate Validity 3 Years]
    N -- Rejected --> P[Rejection Order with Reasons]
    P --> Q[Appeal Provision under Section 31 6]
    O --> R[Apply for Manufacturing License with State FSSAI]
    R --> S[Commence Commercial Production]
    S --> T[Post Approval Compliance Audits]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Document Inter-relationships for Proprietary Food Approval

The following architecture diagram depicts how the various submission documents relate to each other and feed into the FSSAI evaluation system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Form A Application] --> B1[FoSCoS Portal Submission Gateway]
    A2[Recipe Formulation Sheet] --> B1
    A3[Process Flow Chart] --> B1
    A4[Mock Label] --> B1
    A5[NABL Lab Reports] --> B1
    A6[Stability Study Data] --> B1
    A7[Brand Trademark Proof] --> B1
    A8[FSSAI License Copy] --> B1
    A9[MSDS Sheets] --> B1
    B1 --> C1{FSSAI Product Approval Cell}
    C1 --> D1[Scientific Evaluation Engine]
    D1 --> E1{Compliance Check}
    E1 -- Pass --> F1[Approval Certificate Issued]
    E1 -- Fail --> G1[Query Letter or Rejection]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Pre-Approval Compliance Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start: New Food Product Idea] --> P2{Is the product}
    P2 -- Standardized --> P3[Use FSSAI License Only]
    P2 -- Novel Ingredient --> P4[Use Novel Food Route]
    P2 -- Non Standardized but Known Safe Ingredients --> P5[Use Proprietary Food Route]
    P2 -- Therapeutic Claims --> P6[Use Nutraceutical Route]
    P5 --> P7{All Additives within Schedule VII MRL}
    P7 -- No --> P8[Reformulate or Reject]
    P7 -- Yes --> P9{Shelf Life <= 9 Months at Ambient}
    P9 -- No --> P10[Conduct Stability Study to Justify]
    P9 -- Yes --> P11[Eligible for Proprietary Food Approval]
    P11 --> P12[Proceed to FoSCoS Application]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper Style]**

### Question A1: Define "Proprietary Food" as per FSSAI. Give two examples.

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Proprietary food is an article of food that has not been standardized under any regulation framed under the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006, and is manufactured and sold under a unique brand or trade name, requiring prior product approval from FSSAI.

**Examples:**
1. Herbal energy drinks (e.g., ginseng-based beverages).
2. Quinoa-based snack bars with mixed dry fruits.

> [3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for examples]

---

### Question A2: Mention any three documents required for FSSAI proprietary food approval.

**Model Answer:**

The three mandatory documents are:
1. **Quantitative formulation/recipe sheet** listing all ingredients and additives in percentage.
2. **NABL-accredited laboratory test reports** covering microbiological, chemical, and nutritional analysis.
3. **Mock product label** compliant with FSS (Labelling & Display) Regulations, 2020.

> [3 marks: 1 mark each]

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – University Past Question Style, Module 4 Choice-Based]**

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Pattern Match]**

> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the **eligibility criteria and procedure** for obtaining proprietary food approval from FSSAI under the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006.

> **(b)** [7 Marks] Describe the **shelf-life requirements and labelling provisions** specific to proprietary foods. Include a note on prohibited claims.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Statutory Authority**
Proprietary food approval is governed by:
- **Section 22 of the FSS Act, 2006** — power to regulate non-standardized foods.
- **FSS (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011** (with 2020 amendment).
- **Operational Guidelines issued by FSSAI.**

**Step 2: Eligibility Criteria**

> The following conditions must be satisfied:
> 1. The product must NOT have a pre-existing FSSAI standard.
> 2. The applicant must hold or apply for an **FSSAI manufacturing license/registration**.
> 3. All ingredients and additives must be **permitted** as per Schedule VII, with **additive usage within MRL**.
> 4. The manufacturing facility must comply with **Schedule 4 GMP**.
> 5. The product must not make any therapeutic claim.

**Step 3: Procedure (7 stages)**

| Stage | Action |
|---|---|
| 1 | Pre-consultation with FSSAI (optional) |
| 2 | FoSCoS portal registration |
| 3 | Online application upload with all documents |
| 4 | Fee payment of INR 7,500 |
| 5 | Preliminary scrutiny by FSSAI |
| 6 | Scientific evaluation by FSSAI panel |
| 7 | Grant of approval certificate (validity 3 years) or rejection |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Shelf-Life Requirements**

- Maximum **9 months** at ambient storage conditions unless supported by a **scientific stability study**.
- Stability study must follow **accelerated or real-time protocols**.
- Arrhenius $Q_{10}$ model is the standard extrapolation tool.
- Lab report must specify storage conditions (temperature, humidity).

**Step 2: Labeling Provisions**

As per the **FSS (Labelling and Display) Regulations, 2020**, the label must include:
1. **Name of the food**
2. **List of ingredients** (descending order of weight)
3. **Nutritional information** (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrates, etc.)
4. **Net quantity**
5. **Date of manufacture and best-before/use-before date**
6. **Brand name and manufacturer's address**
7. **FSSAI logo and license number**
8. **Vegetarian/Non-vegetarian symbol**
9. **Allergen declaration** (if applicable)
10. **Customer care details**

**Step 3: Prohibited Claims**

Proprietary foods **CANNOT** carry:
- ❌ Disease prevention or cure claims.
- ❌ "Health supplement" or "nutraceutical" claims.
- ❌ Unsubstantiated functional claims.
- ❌ Comparative claims unless substantiated.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam – July 2023 – Pattern Match]**

> **(a)** [7 Marks] Differentiate between **Proprietary Food** and **Novel Food**. Under what regulatory circumstances must an applicant choose the novel food route over the proprietary food route?

> **(b)** [7 Marks] A manufacturer submits a novel spice blend for proprietary food approval. Accelerated stability testing at 45°C shows peroxide value reaches rejection threshold in **30 days**. Using the Arrhenius $Q_{10}$ model ($Q_{10} = 2.0$), calculate the **expected shelf life at 25°C**. Comment on whether this shelf life is acceptable under proprietary food regulations.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Tabular Comparison**

| Parameter | Proprietary Food | Novel Food |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Non-standardized food with known/safe ingredients | Non-standardized food using **novel ingredients** with no prior history of safe consumption in India |
| Legal Basis | FSS (Food Products Standards & Food Additives) Regulations, 2011 (3rd Amendment, 2020) | FSS (Novel Food) Regulations, 2016 |
| Approval Timeline | 30–60 working days | 6–12 months |
| Risk Assessment | Limited scientific scrutiny | Full safety assessment, toxicology, allergenicity, dietary exposure |
| Examples | Herbal coffee, quinoa bars | Insect protein bars, algae-based DHA |
| Governing Body | FSSAI Product Approval Cell | FSSAI Novel Food Committee |

**Step 2: When to Choose Novel Food Route**

> The novel food route is mandatory when:
> 1. The food contains a **novel ingredient** not consumed significantly in India before **2007**.
> 2. The food is produced by a **novel process** that changes its nutritional value, metabolism, or contaminant level.
> 3. The product contains **engineered nanomaterials** or **genetically modified** components.
> 4. Available data is **insufficient** to establish substantial equivalence with existing foods.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Given Data**

$$ T_E = 45^\circ C, \quad T_A = 25^\circ C, \quad ASL = 30 \text{ days}, \quad Q_{10} = 2.0 $$

**Step 2: Apply the Master Equation**

$$ \log(\tau_A) = \log(\tau_E) + \left(\frac{T_E - T_A}{10}\right) \cdot \log(Q_{10}) $$

**Step 3: Substitute Values**

$$ \log(\tau_A) = \log(30) + \left(\frac{45 - 25}{10}\right) \cdot \log(2.0) $$

$$ \log(\tau_A) = 1.4771 + (2.0)(0.3010) $$

$$ \log(\tau_A) = 1.4771 + 0.6020 = 2.0791 $$

**Step 4: Solve for $\tau_A$**

$$ \tau_A = 10^{2.0791} \approx 119.95 \text{ days} \approx 120 \text{ days} $$

**Step 5: Convert to months**

$$ \tau_A = \frac{120}{30} = 4 \text{ months} $$

**Step 6: Regulatory Compliance Comment**

> **Conclusion:** The estimated shelf life at 25°C is **120 days (4 months)**.
>
> - ✅ **Acceptable** under proprietary food regulations (since it is well below the 9-month ambient shelf-life threshold).
> - 📦 **Packaging recommendation:** Use **metallized pouches** or **nitrogen-flushed packaging** to extend shelf life further.
> - ⚗️ **Formulation recommendation:** Add permitted antioxidants like **BHA (≤ 200 mg/kg)** or **tocopherol blends** to retard lipid oxidation.
>
> Final answer: The product is **regulatorily compliant** and may be filed for proprietary food approval with a **shelf life claim of 4 months at ambient conditions (25°C)**.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Conflating Proprietary Food with Health Supplements.** The most common error. Remember: **Proprietary Food = No therapeutic claim allowed**.
> 2. **Forgetting the validity period.** Approval is for **3 years** — students often write "permanent" or "5 years". This loses **2 marks**.
> 3. **Wrong fee.** Some textbooks quote outdated fees. The current **proprietary food application fee is INR 7,500** (verify with latest FSSAI notification).
> 4. **Missing the 9-month shelf-life threshold.** Without scientific justification, proprietary foods cannot claim more than 9 months at ambient conditions.
> 5. **No mention of FoSCoS portal.** Examiners specifically look for the online application system — forgetting this loses **1–2 marks**.
> 6. **Confusing Section 22 with Section 31 of FSS Act.** Section 22 = regulatory power; Section 31 = licensing. Both are relevant but distinct.
> 7. **Skipping allergen declaration** in the label mock-up — mandatory under FSS (Labelling & Display) Regulations, 2020.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Approval for Proprietary Food**

- **Definition:** Non-standardized food sold under a unique brand, requiring FSSAI approval.
- **Legal basis:** FSS Act, 2006 (Section 22) + FSS (Food Products Standards & Food Additives) Regulations, 2011 (3rd Amendment, 2020).
- **Application portal:** **FoSCoS** (https://foscos.fssai.gov.in).
- **Application fee:** **INR 7,500** per product.
- **Approval validity:** **3 years**.
- **Default shelf life limit:** **9 months** at ambient (without special study).
- **Shelf-life model:** **Arrhenius $Q_{10}$** equation.
- **Prohibited claims:** No therapeutic or disease-cure claims.
- **Mandatory documents (high-yield):** Form A, recipe sheet, process flow, mock label, NABL lab reports, stability data, brand trademark, FSSAI license.
- **Labelling compliance:** FSS (Labelling & Display) Regulations, 2020.
- **Distinguish from:** Novel Food (untested ingredients), Health Supplement (therapeutic claims), Standardized Food (existing standard exists).
- **Key engineering formula:**

$$ \log(\tau_A) = \log(\tau_E) + \left(\frac{T_E - T_A}{10}\right) \cdot \log(Q_{10}) $$

- **Post-approval duties:** Compliance audits, recall/traceability system, adverse event reporting within 24 hours.
- **Industrial relevance:** Fastest regulatory pathway for food startups launching innovative products.
- **Appeals mechanism:** Section 31(6) of FSS Act for rejected applications.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Food Safety Management System and Quality Audits for a Food Product
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT835 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Food Safety Management System and Quality Audits for a Food Product

# 🍱 FOOD SAFETY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM (FSMS) & QUALITY AUDITS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Learning Anchor:** This topic is mapped to **Course Outcome CO4** of OEFTT835 — *"Apply food safety management systems and quality audit principles in the design and development of food products."* It directly supports **Bloom's Level: Apply / Analyze**.

---

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition — Food Safety Management System (FSMS)

A **Food Safety Management System (FSMS)** is a systematically structured, documented, and continually improved network of interrelated procedures, controls, prerequisites, and management practices designed to ensure that food products are safe for human consumption throughout the entire farm-to-fork continuum.

According to **ISO 22000:2018**, an FSMS is formally defined as:

> *"A set of interrelated or interacting elements that an organization uses to establish policies and objectives and to achieve those objectives, specifically to provide safe food for end consumers."*

The three foundational pillars of any FSMS are:

| Pillar | Description | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Prerequisite Programmes (PRPs)** | Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP), Good Hygiene Practices (GHP), sanitation, pest control | Operational baseline (must be operational before HACCP) |
| **HACCP Plan** | Hazard Analysis & Critical Control Points — preventive risk-based system | Core scientific component |
| **Management System Elements** | Documentation, training, internal audits, corrective actions, management review | ISO 22000 / FSSC 22000 framework |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A frequent mistake students make is treating **HACCP** and **FSMS** as synonyms. HACCP is a *tool* (the scientific engine), while FSMS is the *overarching management framework* that hosts HACCP. Always remember: **FSMS ⊃ HACCP ⊃ PRPs**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen" Intuition

Imagine you are opening a cloud-kitchen that delivers food to 5,000 customers daily.

- **PRPs** = The clean kitchen floor, washed hands, refrigerated storage, pest-free walls — the *housekeeping*. Without these, no fancy system can save you.
- **HACCP** = The chef wearing a thermometer while cooking chicken, ensuring the internal temperature hits **74 °C** for at least **15 seconds**. He is monitoring the *critical point* where bacteria could survive.
- **Management System** = The owner who keeps records of temperature logs, trains new cooks, reviews monthly complaint data, and calls an external auditor to certify the kitchen. This is *governance*.

**Food Safety = Clean Kitchen + Sharp Chef + Diligent Owner.**

If any of these three fails, the customer gets food poisoning. Similarly, FSMS failures in real life cause outbreaks like the **2008 Chinese melamine scandal** or the **2018 romaine lettuce *E. coli* outbreak** in the USA.

---

## 1.3 Quality Audit — Formal Definition

A **Quality Audit** is a *systematic, independent, and documented* examination of a food safety/quality system, conducted to determine whether activities and related results comply with planned arrangements, and whether these arrangements are implemented effectively and are suitable to achieve objectives.

Audits can be:

- **First-Party (Internal Audit)** — Conducted by the organization on itself.
- **Second-Party (Supplier Audit)** — Conducted by a customer on its supplier.
- **Third-Party (Certification / External Audit)** — Conducted by an independent certification body (e.g., Bureau Veritas, TÜV SÜD) for ISO 22000 / FSSC 22000 certification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mandatory Recall:** For a 14-mark question, you must be able to differentiate between **Inspection**, **Surveillance**, **Audit**, and **Certification** — these four are commonly confused.

---

## 1.4 Regulatory & Standards Framework Relevant to India

| Authority / Standard | Scope | Jurisdiction |
|---|---|---|
| **FSSAI** (Food Safety & Standards Authority of India) | FSS Act 2006, licensing, FSSAI 14-digit license, FoSCoS portal | Mandatory in India |
| **Codex Alimentarius Commission** | International food standards, guidelines, codes of practice | Global reference (WHO/FAO) |
| **ISO 22000:2018** | Generic FSMS standard | International |
| **FSSC 22000 v6** | GFSI-benchmarked scheme (ISO 22000 + ISO/TS 22002-1 + additional requirements) | Global GFSI recognition |
| **BRCGS** (Brand Reputation Compliance Global Standards) | UK-origin, retailer-driven | Global |
| **SQF** (Safe Quality Food) | USA-origin | Global |
| **HACCP (Codex, 1993, revised 2020)** | 7 principles + 12 steps | Universal scientific method |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Codex Alimentarius HACCP System — 12 Logical Steps & 7 Principles

The **Codex Alimentarius** (Latin for *"Food Code"*) lays down the universally accepted **7 Principles of HACCP**, preceded by **5 Preliminary Steps** — together called the **12 Steps of HACCP**.

### 2.1.1 Preliminary Steps (Foundation)

| Step # | Activity | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Step 1** | Assemble the HACCP Team | Multidisciplinary: production, QA, microbiology, engineering |
| **Step 2** | Describe the Product | Composition, physical/chemical properties, packaging, shelf-life |
| **Step 3** | Identify Intended Use | Target consumer (general public, infants, immunocompromised) |
| **Step 4** | Construct Process Flow Diagram | Block diagram from raw material reception → dispatch |
| **Step 5** | On-site Verification of Flow Diagram | Walk-through to confirm accuracy |

### 2.1.2 The 7 Principles (Active HACCP)

| Principle | KTU-Compressed Explanation |
|---|---|
| **P1 — Hazard Analysis (HA)** | Identify Biological, Chemical, Physical hazards. Determine CCPs using the **Decision Tree** |
| **P2 — Determine CCPs** | Critical Control Points — steps where control is *essential* to prevent/eliminate a hazard |
| **P3 — Establish Critical Limits (CLs)** | Max/min values (e.g., temperature ≤ 4 °C, pH ≤ 4.5) |
| **P4 — Establish Monitoring Procedures** | What, How, When, Who, Frequency |
| **P5 — Establish Corrective Actions (CA)** | What to do when monitoring shows a deviation |
| **P6 — Establish Verification Procedures** | Calibration, microbial testing, internal audits |
| **P7 — Establish Record-Keeping & Documentation** | All records retained for traceability |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Codex 2020 Update:** The Codex 2020 revision introduced **"Other Quality Attributes" (OQAs)** — meaning CCPs can now control food *quality* (not just safety) — e.g., browning control in pre-cut apples. This is a high-yield exam point.

---

## 2.2 The CCP Decision Tree (Codex Logic)

The **decision tree** is a four-question logic gate applied at each process step:

```
Q1: Do preventive control measures exist?
      ├── NO → Not a CCP (modify process/product) → STOP
      └── YES ↓
Q2: Is this step specifically designed to eliminate/reduce the hazard
    to an acceptable level?
      ├── YES → CCP  → STOP
      └── NO ↓
Q3: Could contamination with the hazard occur in excess of acceptable
    level(s), or could it increase to unacceptable level(s)?
      ├── NO → Not a CCP → STOP
      └── YES ↓
Q4: Will a subsequent step eliminate/reduce the hazard to acceptable
    level?
      ├── NO → CCP  → STOP
      └── YES → Not a CCP (control via PRP)
```

---

## 2.3 Quality Audit — Theoretical Framework

### 2.3.1 Audit Principles (ISO 19011:2018)

1. **Integrity** — Auditor must be honest and ethical.
2. **Fair Presentation** — Findings reflect actual state.
3. **Due Professional Care** — Reasonable judgment.
4. **Confidentiality** — Information security.
5. **Independence** — Auditor independent from area audited.
6. **Evidence-Based Approach** — Findings must be verifiable.

### 2.3.2 Types of Non-Conformities (NCs)

| Type | Definition | Timeline for Closure |
|---|---|---|
| **Major NC** | Complete absence of, or total breakdown in, a system element | Typically **30 days** with documented evidence |
| **Minor NC** | Isolated incident or single lapse; system still works | Typically **60–90 days** |
| **Observation / OFI** (Opportunity for Improvement) | Risk not yet an NC but could become one | Not mandatory to close |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandate:** All numerical concepts, thresholds, and audit metrics for this topic are consolidated below.

### Table 1 — Critical Limits for Common Hazards

| Hazard Type | Process Step | Critical Limit | Basis |
|---|---|---|---|
| *Salmonella* in poultry | Cooking | $\geq 74\ ^\circ\text{C}$ for $\geq 15\ \text{s}$ | USDA-FSIS |
| *Listeria* in RTE meats | Post-cook chilling | $\leq 4\ ^\circ\text{C}$ within $4\ \text{h}$ | FDA |
| *Clostridium botulinum* | Low-acid canned foods | $F_0 \geq 3\ \text{min}$ ($121.1\ ^\circ\text{C}$ equivalent) | Codex |
| Aflatoxin in peanuts | Sorting | $\leq 20\ \mu\text{g/kg}$ (B1+B2+G1+G2) | FSSAI |
| Heavy metals (Pb) | General food | $\leq 0.01\ \text{mg/kg}$ (infant foods) | FSSAI |

> [!NOTE]
> **Avoid pipe-syntax pitfalls:** Critical limits must be expressed with `\vert` not `\vertvert`. For ranges like "between 4 and 60 °C," use `\vert$4-60^\circ\text{C}$\vert` inside LaTeX blocks instead of inline markdown tables.

### Table 2 — Audit Scoring & Determination

| Metric | Formula | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Audit Effectiveness Score | $\text{Eff} = \dfrac{\text{NC}_{\text{closed on time}}}{\text{NC}_{\text{raised total}}} \times 100\%$ | System closure discipline |
| Sample Size for Internal Audit | $n = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2}$ | Statistical sampling |
| Verification Frequency (CCP) | $f_{\text{verify}} \geq \dfrac{f_{\text{monitor}}}{3}$ | At least one-third of monitoring rate |
| Calibration Drift Tolerance | $\delta = \dfrac{\vert x_{\text{reading}} - x_{\text{standard}} \vert}{x_{\text{standard}}} \times 100\% \leq 5\%$ | Equipment reliability |

> Where $Z$ = Z-score (1.96 for 95 % confidence), $p$ = expected proportion (commonly 0.5 for conservative estimate), $e$ = margin of error (typically 0.05).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food Industry 4.0:** Modern FSMS integrates **IoT sensors** for continuous CCP monitoring (e.g., real-time temperature loggers uploading to cloud dashboards).
- **Predictive Microbiology:** Tools like **ComBase** model microbial growth using *γ-concept* equations, helping determine CCPs quantitatively.
- **Blockchain Traceability:** Audit trails are increasingly recorded on blockchain to provide tamper-proof traceability — relevant to FSSAI's 2023 *Food Safety Connect* initiative.
- **Root-Cause Analysis:** Failures in FSMS are analyzed using **5-Why** or **Ishikawa (Fishbone)** diagrams — common in KTU 14-mark answers.

---

## 2.6 The Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) Cycle in FSMS

The ISO 22000:2018 framework is explicitly built on the **PDCA cycle**:

| Stage | FSMS Activity |
|---|---|
| **Plan** | Establish FSMS scope, policies, PRP, HACCP plan |
| **Do** | Implement and operate the FSMS — training, monitoring |
| **Check** | Internal audits, monitoring of CCPs, calibration |
| **Act** | Management review, corrective actions, continual improvement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** If a question asks "Explain how ISO 22000:2018 integrates PDCA," always draw **two interlocking cycles** — one for the management system and one for HACCP. This visual answers a 7-mark sub-question perfectly.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandatory Compliance:** Every algebraic transition is explicitly written. No shortcuts, no skipping steps.

---

## 3.1 Derivation I — Sample Size Determination for an Internal Food Safety Audit

### Contextual Problem

A Kerala-based spice-processing unit plans to conduct an internal FSMS audit across **N = 280** raw-material receiving lots in a quarter. The Quality Manager wants to sample a statistically valid subset such that the sample proportion estimate has:

- **Confidence level:** 95 % → $Z = 1.96$
- **Margin of error:** $e = 0.05$ (i.e., ±5 %)
- **Expected proportion of non-conforming lots:** $p = 0.5$ (most conservative; no prior data)

### Step 1 — Identify the Applicable Formula

For an *infinite* or large population, the standard sample-size formula is:

$$
n_0 \;=\; \frac{Z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$
n_0 \;=\; \frac{(1.96)^2 \cdot (0.5)(1-0.5)}{(0.05)^2}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Each Component (Explicit)

First compute $(1.96)^2$:

$$
(1.96)^2 \;=\; 1.96 \times 1.96 \;=\; 3.8416
$$

Next compute $p(1-p)$:

$$
p(1-p) \;=\; 0.5 \times 0.5 \;=\; 0.25
$$

Next compute $e^2$:

$$
e^2 \;=\; 0.05 \times 0.05 \;=\; 0.0025
$$

### Step 4 — Numerator and Denominator Assembly

$$
n_0 \;=\; \frac{3.8416 \times 0.25}{0.0025} \;=\; \frac{0.9604}{0.0025}
$$

### Step 5 — Final Division

$$
n_0 \;=\; 384.16
$$

Since the result is non-integer, round **up** for conservative audit design:

$$
n_0 \;\approx\; 385\ \text{lots}
$$

### Step 6 — Apply Finite Population Correction (FPC) — Step 6a is Required Because $n_0 > N \times 0.05$

The **Finite Population Correction (FPC)** is required when sample size exceeds 5 % of population. Here:

$$
\frac{n_0}{N} \;=\; \frac{385}{280} \;=\; 1.375 \;>\; 0.05
$$

So FPC applies. The corrected sample size $n$ is:

$$
n \;=\; \frac{n_0}{1 + \dfrac{n_0 - 1}{N}}
$$

### Step 7 — Substitute into FPC Equation

$$
n \;=\; \frac{385}{1 + \dfrac{385 - 1}{280}} \;=\; \frac{385}{1 + \dfrac{384}{280}}
$$

### Step 8 — Evaluate the Fraction in the Denominator

$$
\frac{384}{280} \;=\; 1.3714
$$

$$
n \;=\; \frac{385}{1 + 1.3714} \;=\; \frac{385}{2.3714}
$$

### Step 9 — Final Corrected Sample Size

$$
n \;\approx\; 162.35 \;\Rightarrow\; 163\ \text{lots}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Without FPC, the auditor would have to sample **385 lots — more than the entire population!** This would force 100 % inspection, which is impractical and costly. FPC gives a realistic, statistically valid audit sample of **163 lots**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Calibration Drift Tolerance Verification

### Problem

A digital thermometer used for CCP monitoring reads **99.4 °C** when inserted in a **standard reference bath at 100.0 °C**. Calculate the calibration drift and decide if the instrument passes the **5 % tolerance** criterion.

### Step 1 — Define Drift Formula

$$
\delta \;=\; \frac{\vert x_{\text{reading}} - x_{\text{standard}} \vert}{x_{\text{standard}}} \times 100\%
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Absolute Deviation

$$
\vert x_{\text{reading}} - x_{\text{standard}} \vert \;=\; \vert 99.4 - 100.0 \vert \;=\; 0.6\ ^\circ\text{C}
$$

### Step 3 — Divide by Standard and Multiply

$$
\delta \;=\; \frac{0.6}{100.0} \times 100\% \;=\; 0.6\%
$$

### Step 4 — Compare to Tolerance

$$
\delta \;=\; 0.6\% \;\leq\; 5\% \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{PASS}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The thermometer is fit-for-purpose. If $\delta > 5\%$, the instrument must be **recalibrated or replaced** and all readings since the last calibration must be re-verified — a **Major NC**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — HACCP CCP Determination Logic for Pasteurized Milk

### Scenario

A dairy plant pasteurizes cow milk. Use the Codex Decision Tree to identify CCPs.

**Process Steps:**

1. Raw milk reception
2. Cold storage (4 °C)
3. Standardization
4. **Pasteurization (72 °C, 15 s)**
5. Cooling (4 °C)
6. Aseptic packaging
7. Cold storage & dispatch

### Analytical Matrix

| Step | Hazard | Q1: Preventive Control? | Q2: Designed to Eliminate? | Q3: Could Contamination Occur? | Q4: Later Step Eliminates? | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Reception | *Salmonella*, antibiotics | Yes (lab testing) | No | Yes | Yes (pasteurization) | **Not CCP** → Control via PRP |
| 2. Storage | Pathogen growth | Yes (temp control) | No | Yes | Yes | Not CCP (PRP) |
| 3. Standardization | None significant | — | — | — | — | **Not CCP** |
| 4. **Pasteurization** | Pathogens (*Mycobacterium bovis*, *Coxiella*) | Yes | **Yes — STEP SPECIFICALLY DESIGNED** | — | — | **CCP-1** |
| 5. Cooling | *Bacillus cereus* spore germination | Yes | No | Yes | No (no further kill step) | **CCP-2** |
| 6. Packaging | Recontamination | Yes (aseptic) | No | Yes | No | Not CCP (PRP) |
| 7. Dispatch | Temperature abuse | Yes (cold chain) | No | Yes | No | Not CCP if CCP-2 holds |

### Critical Limits Established

$$
\text{Critical Limit}_{\text{CCP-1}}:\;\; T \geq 72\ ^\circ\text{C},\ \ \tau \geq 15\ \text{s}
$$

$$
\text{Critical Limit}_{\text{CCP-2}}:\;\; T \leq 4\ ^\circ\text{C}\ \text{within 4 h}
$$

### Monitoring Equations — Lethality (F-value) Calculation

The pasteurization efficacy is given by the **lethal rate** $L$ integrated over time:

$$
F_{T_{\text{ref}}}^{z} \;=\; \int_0^{\tau} 10^{\frac{T(t) - T_{\text{ref}}}{z}} \, dt
$$

Where:
- $T_{\text{ref}}$ = reference temperature (e.g., 72 °C for milk)
- $z$ = temperature change for 10-fold change in D-value (typically $z = 6.6\ ^\circ\text{C}$ for *M. bovis*)
- $T(t)$ = temperature profile during the holding time

For an ideal isothermal hold at exactly 72 °C:

$$
F \;=\; 10^{\frac{72 - 72}{6.6}} \times 15 \;\text{s} \;=\; 1 \times 15 \;\text{s} \;=\; 15\ \text{s}
$$

> **Interpretation:** This 15-second residence time delivers the minimum lethal effect required by FSSAI (Part II — Milk and Milk Products Regulations). Any deviation below 72 °C must trigger the **Corrective Action** defined in P5.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing HACCP, ISO 22000, FSSC 22000

| Feature | HACCP (Codex) | ISO 22000:2018 | FSSC 22000 v6 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Codex, 1993 | ISO, 2005 (revised 2018) | GFSI Foundation |
| **Scope** | Food safety only | Food safety + management system | FSMS + sector-specific PRPs |
| **PRPs** | Implied | Mandatory (Clause 8) | Mandatory (ISO/TS 22002-1) |
| **GFSI Recognition** | No | No | **Yes** |
| **Certification** | Not certifiable alone | Yes | Yes |
| **Management Review** | Not required | Required (Clause 9) | Required |
| **Risk-Based Thinking** | Limited | Core requirement (Clause 6) | Mandatory |

---

## 3.5 Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Using the 5-Why Method — Step-by-Step

**Observation (Audit Finding):** *Listeria monocytogenes* detected in finished RTE chicken product (2 log CFU/g, exceeding FSSAI limit of absent in 25 g).

| Why # | Question | Answer |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Why was *Listeria* present in finished product? | Post-cook recontamination occurred. |
| 2 | Why did recontamination occur? | Slicing blade temperature was 12 °C (above 4 °C limit). |
| 3 | Why was the slicer blade warm? | Slice-room temperature was 18 °C (should be ≤ 10 °C). |
| 4 | Why was the room temperature high? | HVAC unit #3 had been malfunctioning for 5 days. |
| 5 | Why was HVAC not repaired for 5 days? | Maintenance request was not logged due to absence of shift supervisor last weekend. |

**Root Cause → Corrective Action:** Implement a *mandatory backup shift supervisor roster*; enforce daily HVAC performance verification via BMS (Building Management System); revise Preventive Maintenance SOP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** A 5-Why analysis showing *the true systemic root cause* (not just a symptom) earns full marks in the **"corrective action"** part of an audit question.

# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Many students write **"HACCP"** when the question asks for **"FSMS"** — read the question stem carefully.
> - Do not confuse **Critical Control Point (CCP)** with **Control Point (CP)** or **OPRP (Operational Prerequisite Programme)**. CCPs are *must-eliminate* points; OPREs control *likelihood of contamination* but are not "critical" — partial credit only if you mix these up.
> - When listing HACCP principles, do not skip **P6 (Verification)** and **P7 (Documentation)** — these are commonly forgotten.
> - In audit questions, always mention **ISO 19011:2018** explicitly — a generic "ISO standard" loses marks.

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]

**Define a Critical Control Point (CCP). Give one example from a dairy processing plant.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Critical Control Point (CCP)** is a step in the food process at which control measures can be applied to **prevent, eliminate, or reduce a food safety hazard to an acceptable level**, and at which the failure of control would *likely* result in an unacceptable health risk to the consumer.

**Example:** The **pasteurization step** in milk processing — maintaining a temperature of $\geq 72\ ^\circ\text{C}$ for $\geq 15\ \text{s}$ is a CCP designed to eliminate pathogenic bacteria such as *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*, *Salmonella spp.*, and *Listeria monocytogenes*.

A deviation at this CCP (e.g., temperature dropping to 65 °C) would result in pathogen survival, making this step *critical*.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 1]

**Differentiate between First-Party, Second-Party, and Third-Party audits with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per type):**

| Type | Conducted By | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **First-Party (Internal) Audit** | The organization's own trained auditors | Verify own FSMS compliance and readiness | QA team auditing the dairy plant monthly |
| **Second-Party (Supplier) Audit** | A customer organization on its supplier | Verify supplier's ability to meet specified requirements | A retail chain auditing its contract bakery for hygiene |
| **Third-Party (Certification) Audit** | Independent accredited certification body | Grant/withhold certification for compliance to a published standard | TÜV SÜD certifying the plant against ISO 22000:2018 |

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### 🔷 Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Expected]

**(a)** Explain the **seven principles of HACCP** as per Codex Alimentarius. Illustrate your answer with a flow diagram and describe how a CCP is differentiated from a Control Point (CP). (7 marks)

**(b)** A seafood processing unit in Kerala exports frozen shrimp to the EU. Design a HACCP plan for the **peeling, deveining, and freezing** line. Identify at least **two CCPs** with their critical limits, monitoring procedures, and corrective actions. (7 marks)

#### MODEL ANSWER

**(a) Seven Principles of HACCP (7 marks — distribute 1 mark per principle + 1 for CCP vs CP distinction):**

1. **Principle 1 — Hazard Analysis (HA):** Identify biological (e.g., *Vibrio cholerae*), chemical (e.g., histamine, heavy metals), and physical (e.g., metal fragments) hazards. Assess severity and likelihood.
2. **Principle 2 — Determine CCPs:** Apply the Codex Decision Tree. A CCP is a step where *absence of control will result in unacceptable risk*; a CP (or OPRP) is a step where control measures address likelihood but are not the *final kill/reduction* barrier.
3. **Principle 3 — Establish Critical Limits:** Maximum and/or minimum values e.g., cooking temperature $\geq 75\ ^\circ\text{C}$; pH $\leq 4.5$; water activity $\leq 0.85$.
4. **Principle 4 — Establish Monitoring Procedures:** Define **What** (parameter), **How** (instrument), **Frequency** (continuous or periodic), and **Who** (responsible person).
5. **Principle 5 — Establish Corrective Actions:** Specific actions to bring the process back into control when a deviation is observed (e.g., hold product, re-cook, dispose, calibrate equipment).
6. **Principle 6 — Establish Verification Procedures:** Independent activities that confirm the HACCP system is working — internal audits, calibration, end-product testing, review of records.
7. **Principle 7 — Record-Keeping and Documentation:** Maintain records of hazard analysis, CCP monitoring, deviations, corrective actions, and verification activities. Records must be retained for the product's shelf life plus one year (or as per regulation).

**CCP vs CP — The Differentiator:**

| Feature | CCP | CP (OPRP) |
|---|---|---|
| Risk if failed | Unacceptable health risk | Quality risk or controlled-but-not-critical hazard |
| Decision tree | Marks as CCP | Not marked as CCP |
| Example in dairy | Pasteurization | Cold storage of raw milk |

**(b) HACCP Plan for Shrimp Processing Line (7 marks):**

**Product Description:** Frozen raw peeled and deveined (PD) shrimp; packed in 1 kg blocks; storage at $-18\ ^\circ\text{C}$; target consumers — general public including export to EU retailers.

**Flow Diagram:**

Raw material reception → Washing → Sorting → Peeling/Deveining → Washing → Glazing → Metal Detection → Weighing/Packing → Freezing (Blast freezer $-35\ ^\circ\text{C}$) → Cold storage → Dispatch.

**Identified CCPs:**

| CCP | Hazard | Critical Limit | Monitoring | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CCP-1: Metal Detection** | Physical — metal fragments | Fe $\leq 1.5\ \text{mm}$, Non-Fe $\leq 2.0\ \text{mm}$, SS $\leq 2.5\ \text{mm}$ | Continuous; hourly test piece verification | Reject last product back to last good check; segregate & inspect held lot; verify detector calibration |
| **CCP-2: Blast Freezing** | Biological — *Listeria*, *Salmonella* growth; histamine formation | Product core $\leq -18\ ^\circ\text{C}$ within 4 h | Continuous temperature data logger + 30-min manual log | Hold lot; re-test histamine (must be $\leq 100\ \text{mg/kg}$); convert to non-export grade if rejected |

**Verification:** End-product microbiological testing (5 samples per batch for *Salmonella*, *L. monocytogenes*), histamine testing (per Codex 2020), daily calibration of metal detector.

**Records:** Daily CCP log, calibration log, deviation register, corrective action register, batch traceability record.

---

### 🔷 Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Expected — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Discuss the **structure and key requirements of ISO 22000:2018**. Explain how it integrates the PDCA cycle with HACCP and PRPs. (7 marks)

**(b)** A fruit juice company is preparing for a **third-party certification audit** for ISO 22000:2018. Outline the **complete audit process** as per ISO 19011:2018, including pre-audit activities, on-site audit conduct, and post-audit follow-up. List the documentation that the company must maintain. (7 marks)

#### MODEL ANSWER

**(a) ISO 22000:2018 — Structure and PDCA Integration (7 marks):**

ISO 22000:2018 has **10 clauses**, of which Clauses 4–10 follow the harmonized ISO High-Level Structure (HLS):

- **Clause 4 — Context of the Organization:** Internal/external issues, needs of interested parties, FSMS scope.
- **Clause 5 — Leadership:** Top management commitment, food safety policy, roles and responsibilities.
- **Clause 6 — Planning:** Actions to address risks and opportunities; FSMS objectives and planning to achieve them.
- **Clause 7 — Support:** Resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented information.
- **Clause 8 — Operation:** Operational planning and control; **PRPs (Clause 8.2)**; **HACCP plan using Codex principles (Clause 8.5)**.
- **Clause 9 — Performance Evaluation:** Monitoring, measurement, analysis; **internal audit (Clause 9.2)**; management review (Clause 9.3).
- **Clause 10 — Improvement:** Nonconformity and corrective action; continual improvement.

**PDCA Integration:**

- *Plan* is realized through Clauses 4, 5, 6 and 8 (planning of FSMS, PRPs, HACCP).
- *Do* is realized through Clause 7 (resources, training, communication, documentation) and operational implementation of Clause 8.
- *Check* is realized through Clause 9 (monitoring, internal audit, management review).
- *Act* is realized through Clause 10 (corrective actions and continual improvement).

**PRPs and HACCP Relationship:**

PRPs provide the *foundational environment* (Clause 8.2) — GMP, sanitation, equipment calibration, pest control, allergen management. HACCP plan (Clause 8.5) is layered *on top* of PRPs and addresses the *significant hazards* identified through hazard analysis. Without functioning PRPs, HACCP cannot reliably function.

**(b) Third-Party Certification Audit Process per ISO 19011:2018 (7 marks):**

**Phase 1 — Pre-Audit Activities:**

1. **Audit Programme Establishment:** Define objectives, scope, criteria, frequency.
2. **Selection of Audit Team:** Lead auditor + technical experts; declare independence.
3. **Document Review:** Review FSMS manual, procedures, HACCP plan, previous audit reports.
4. **Stage 1 Audit (Documentation Review + Readiness Check):** Verify documentation conforms to ISO 22000; identify gaps; confirm readiness for Stage 2.
5. **Audit Plan Preparation:** Schedule, area allocation, auditor assignments.

**Phase 2 — On-Site Audit Conduct (Stage 2):**

1. **Opening Meeting:** Confirm scope, criteria, confidentiality, methodology.
2. **Site Tour:** Visual inspection of facilities, storage, production, sanitation.
3. **Document Sampling:** Review CCP monitoring records, calibration records, training records, supplier evaluations.
4. **Personnel Interviews:** Speak with operators, supervisors, QA staff.
5. **Collecting Objective Evidence:** Triangulate documents + observation + interview.
6. **Audit Findings Generation:** Categorize as Conformity, Major NC, Minor NC, OFI.
7. **Closing Meeting:** Present findings and conclusions; clarify timelines for NC closure.

**Phase 3 — Post-Audit Follow-up:**

1. **Audit Report Distribution:** Within 2 weeks typically.
2. **Corrective Action Submission:** Auditee submits root-cause analysis and corrective action plan within agreed timeline (Major: 30 days; Minor: 60–90 days).
3. **Verification of Corrective Actions:** Auditor reviews evidence of closure; may conduct an on-site follow-up if Major NCs are raised.
4. **Certification Decision:** Independent certification body committee decides to grant, withhold, or suspend certificate. Initial certification typically valid for **3 years**, with surveillance audits at **12-month intervals** and re-certification audit at year 3.

**Mandatory Documentation to Maintain (FSSC 22000 / ISO 22000):**

1. FSMS Manual (scope, policy, objectives)
2. Documented procedures (PRPs, HACCP, traceability, recall, supplier control, internal audit, corrective action, management review)
3. Hazard analysis worksheet and CCP decision tree
4. CCP monitoring records and corrective action records
5. Calibration records for monitoring instruments
6. Training records and competency matrix
7. Supplier approval and monitoring records
8. Internal audit reports and follow-up evidence
9. Management review minutes
10. Customer complaint and recall records
11. Food fraud and food defense vulnerability assessments (per ISO 22000:2018 Clause 8.3)

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Anchor:** Memorize these bullets for a 14-mark audit-style question.

- ✅ FSMS = PRP + HACCP + Management System; HACCP alone is **not** an FSMS.
- ✅ Codex HACCP = **7 Principles + 5 Preliminary Steps** = 12-step methodology.
- ✅ The **Codex Decision Tree** is a 4-question logic for CCP determination.
- ✅ **ISO 22000:2018** uses the **PDCA cycle**; HACCP fits inside *Plan* and *Do*.
- ✅ **FSSC 22000 v6** is **GFSI-benchmarked**; ISO 22000 alone is **not**.
- ✅ Audits: **First-party (internal)**, **Second-party (supplier)**, **Third-party (certification)**.
- ✅ ISO 19011:2018 governs audit *process*; ISO 22000:2018 governs *what is audited*.
- ✅ Audit findings: **Major NC** (system failure), **Minor NC** (isolated lapse), **OFI** (risk warning).
- ✅ Calibration drift must be $\leq 5\%$ to be audit-acceptable; otherwise treat as Major NC.
- ✅ Statistical sample size formula: $n_0 = \dfrac{Z^2 p(1-p)}{e^2}$; apply FPC if $n_0/N > 0.05$.
- ✅ Critical limit examples: Pasteurization $72\ ^\circ\text{C}/15\ \text{s}$; Listeria control $T \leq 4\ ^\circ\text{C}$; Botulinum cook $F_0 \geq 3$ min; Aflatoxin $\leq 20\ \mu\text{g/kg}$.
- ✅ FSSAI limits (always cite *FSSAI* for India-specific questions; cite *Codex* for international).
- ✅ **5-Why analysis** is the most cited RCA tool in KTU answer keys for audit questions.
- ✅ Certification cycle: **3-year validity**, **annual surveillance**, **year-3 re-certification**.
- ✅ For seafood exports to EU, refer to **EU Regulation 853/2004** and **Codex Code of Practice CAC/RCP 52-2003**.
- ✅ Always link **Clause numbers** (ISO 22000:2018) in your answer to score the full marks.
- ✅ Final cross-check: Every answer should mention at least one **standard, one principle, one numerical value, and one real-world example**.
